BIBLIOGEAPHY 


OF   THE    LITERATURE    RELATING    TO 


NEW    ZEALAND 


T.    M.    HOCKBN, 


M.R.C.S.  Esq.,  P.L.S.,  F.R.G.S.,  Fell.  Roy.  Anthrop.  Inst.,  F.J.S.,  Member  of  the 

Council  of  the  University  of  Otago; 

Author  of  "  Contributions  to  the  Early  History  of  New  Zealand,"  etc. 


HE    MAHI    NUI    RAW  A    ATU." 

— Maori  saying. 


Printed    by    John    Mackay,    Government    Printer. 


1909. 


'*     / 


I    i 


iVfl  13 


J'!.  ;:i;;o  j' 


n  ?  7  G  0 


INTEODUCTION. 


The  literature  relating  to  New  Zealand  is  not  only  unusually  interest- 
ing but  is  unusually  extensive.  Fifty  years  ago,  in  the  preface  to  his 
fascinating  "  Story  of  New  Zealand,"  Dr.  Thomson  stated  that  at 
that  time,  and  when  the  colony  was  but  twenty  years  old,  there  had 
been  printed  concerning  it  no  less  than  ninety  volumes,  two  hundred 
pamphlets,  and  a  hundredweight  of  parliamentary  papers.  The  sub- 
joined sketch  will  further  indicate  how  numerous  have  been  the  stirring 
incidents  and  important  events  making  the  history  of  this  young 
country  ;  and  these  have,  fortunately,  never  lacked  the  service  of 
competent  chroniclers. 

From  the  time  when  Tasman  first  placed  on  the  map  of  the  globe 
that  streak,  so  like  a  note  of  interrogation,  which  announced  his  dis- 
covery of  New  Zealand  there  has  attached  to  it  an  unabated  and 
curious  interest.  To  him  it  was  the  Ultima  Thule  of  the  world,  and 
it  represented  a  part  of  that  geographical  Will-o'-the-wisp,  the  Terra 
Australis  Incognita,  which  so  long  engaged  the  quarrelsome  contro- 
versies of  the  learned  in  their  attempts  to  provide  for  the  due  balance 
of  the  globe  itself. 

When  the  genius  of  Cook — "  Oceani  investigator  acerrimus  "^so 
accurately  unfolded  its  coasts,  and  so  graphically  told  its  first  story,  a 
halo  of  romance  gathered  round  it  which  was  never  wholly  dispersed 
until  the  advent  of  steam  and  electricity. 

Bold  sailors  approached  its  shores  with  dread,  for  within  them  wete 
enacted  terrible  scenes  of  ferocity,  massacre,  and  cannibalism.  Yet 
in  strong  contrast  were  the  peaceful  labours  of  the  Protestant  mission, 
almost  the  earliest  in  Pacific  seas,  and  founded  by  the  apostolic  Mars- 
den.  By  them,  as  is  usual  with  such  labours,  was  the  way  prepared 
for  future  trade  and  settlement.  Moved  by  the  spirit  of  adventure 
and  of  scientific  research,  voyagers,  chiefly  of  English  and  French 
nationality,  were  from  the  first  attracted  to  the  little-known  shores. 
The  records  of  these  visits,  together  with  the  letters  and  journals  of 

696927 


iv  Bibliography  of  New  Zealand  Literature. 

the  missionaries,  form  a  special  chapter  of  this  literature,  though  it  is 
greatly  to  be  deplored  that  much  of  this  material  has  been  destroyed, 
either  purposely  or  by  accident. 

The  time  at  length  arrive^^when  permanent  settlement  was  accom- 
plished by  the  New  Zealand  Company  under  the  scheme  devised  by 
Edward  Gibbon  Wakefield,  and  since  known  by  his  name.  The  cir- 
cumstances attending  this  important  movement  were  dramatic  enough 
— refused  assistance,  as  it  was,  by  a  hostile  Colonial  Office,  bitterly 
opposed  and  thwarted  by  the  powerful  Church  Missionary  Society, 
and  endangered  by  the  designs  of  the  French.  Whilst  these  events 
no  doubt  contribute  largely  to  the  number  of  those  documents  whose 
weight  is  computed  by  Dr.  Thomson  in  avoirdupois,  they  form  another 
important  historic  chapter. 

So  raised  to  the  status  of  a  British  colony,  the  successive  events 
in  its  history  never  lacked  the  old  qualities  of  stirring  incident  and 
interest.  Amongst  these  may  be  noted  the  mode  of  its  early  develop- 
ment by  means  of  various  semi-isolated  special  settlements  ;  the 
fierce  and  repeated  struggles  of  the  colonists  with  a  native  race  in- 
telligent though  savage  ;  and  the  important  concession  of  self-govern- 
ment, which  was  amongst  the  earliest  conferred  upon  the  colonies. 
This  latter  boon  was  speedily  follow^ed  by  an  exceedingly  active  poli- 
tical life.  Add  to  all  this  the  unusual  good  fortune  of  having  amongst 
the  pioneers  and  founders  of  the  colony  men  of  distinction  and  cul- 
ture, and  the  materials  for  an  extensive  literature  are  again  evident. 

Untrammelled  by  old  traditions,  New  Zealand  has  of  late  years- 
imposed  upon  herself  the  task  of  grappling  anew  with  many  of  those 
social  problems  which  the  Mother- country  has  in  vain  endeavoured 
to  solve.  What  the  result  may  be  lies  in  the  future,  but  the  present 
strenuous  efforts  of  the  daughter  colony  are  watched  with  absorbing 
interest  by  many  outside  her  borders,  and  have  already  added  another 
weighty  chapter  to  recent  history. 

Although  the  bright  skies,  exhilarating  climate,  and  glorious 
scenery  of  New  Zealand,  apart  from  events  in  its  history,  abundantly 
provide  material  from  which  the  poet,  painter,  novelist,  and  he  who 
treads  the  more  arduous  paths  of  literature  draw  life  and  inspiration, 
still  the  genius  to  worthily  unfold  and  interpret  such  treasures  has 
not  yet  descended. 

The  birth  of  the  poet  is  still  awaited.  Perhaps  his  advent  is  not 
far  distant,  and  may,  indeed,  be  heralded  by  that  yearly  increasing 
burden  of  song,  which  here  and  there  is  lightened  by  undoubted  talent, 
rhythm,  and  beauty.  Perhaps  incessant  struggle  with  the  hardships 
of  colonial  life  has  hitherto   stifled  that  emotional   quality  which. 


Introduction, 


deeply  stirring  the  mind  and  heart,  is  indeed  the  veritable  touchstone 
of  true  poetry. 

Something  akin  may  be  asserted  of  the  painter  and  his  art.  Whilst 
New  Zealand  scenery  has  been  depicted  by  two  or  three  accomplished 
artists  with  a  force  and  spirit  worthy  of  comparison  with  the  best 
examples,  yet  the  superior  height  of  genre  and  historical  painting 
has  been  but  little  attempted,  despite  the  wealth  of  available  material. 
To  this  assertion  one  or  two  exceptions  should  be  made,  as  in  "  The 
Setting-out  of  the  Maori  from  Hawaiki "  and  "  The  Coming  of  the 
Maori,"  both  in  the  Auckland  Public  Gallery.  Special  studies  await- 
ing the  artist's  brush  are  incidents  in  the  descry  by  Tasman,  the  dis- 
covery by  Cook,  the  introduction  of  the  Gospel  by  Samuel  Marsden, 
the  arrival  of  the  first  settlers,  and  the  signing  of  the  Treaty  of  Wai- 
tangi.  The  literature  to  assist  in  the  interpretation  of  these  subjects 
is  abundant,  and  is  set  forth  in  the  following  pages. 

Fiction  and  the  lighter  class  of  literature  have  mostly  taken  the 
shape  of  serial  and  descriptive  story,  with  little  attempt  so  far  to 
emulate  the  culture  and  sustained  effort  of  the  best  novelists.  With 
regard  to  scientific,  historical,  and  certain  other  literary  work  the 
case  is,  happily,  different,  and  it  may  truly  be  averred  that  no  young 
country  has  contributed  so  much  of  a  high  character  to  the  general 
stock  of  such  knowledge  as  New  Zealand. 

After  Dr.  Thomson's  very  full  list,  which  is  appended  to  the  second 
volume  of  his  work,  and  contains  nearly  five  hundred  references,  the 
first  attempt  at  New  Zealand  bibliography  was  made  by  Mr.  J.  D. 
Davis,  of  Auckland,  whose  small  volume  [q.v.)  appeared  in  1887. 
As  the  title  indicates,  it  is  incomplete,  and  it  is,  moreover,  often  in- 
correct. In  1889  appeared  the  bibliography  by  Mr.  J.  Collier,  the 
former  accomplished  librarian  of  the  General  Assembly  Library  at 
Wellington.  This  gentleman  possessed  every  qualification  for  his 
task,  and  the  present  author  expresses  his  sincere  regret  that  ill  health 
and  consequent  departure  from  New  Zealand  prevented  Mr.  Collier 
from  pursuing  such  further  literary  work  as  would  no  doubt  have 
included  a  more  complete  second  edition. 

The  volume  now  before  the  reader  is  the  outcome  of  continuous 
research  undertaken  through  a  long  series  of  years  in  the  intervals  of 
leisure  from  the  demands  of  an  exacting  profession.  A  cursory  in- 
spection will  show  how  frequently  the  author  has  strayed  from  the 
ordinary  track  of  a  bibliography.  He  has  introduced  many  little 
sidelights,  biographical  references,  dates,  and  other  special  points, 
and  an  attempt  has  been  made  to  run  throughout  a  thread  of  his- 
torical interest. 


yi  Bibliography  of  New  Zealand  Literature. 

Another  feature,  de'cided  oii  after  much  consideration,  is  the  giving 
of  a  full  list  of  the  contents  and  illustrations  of  the  rarer  and  least- 
known  works,  such  as  Thevenot,  de  Brosses,  Callander,  Dalrymple,  the 
early  French  voyagers,  Webber,  Angas,  and  others.  The  idea  was 
that  the  contents  of  these  rare  volumes  being  thus,  as  it  were,  focussed 
might  prove  of  great  assistance  to  the  student  engaged  in  a  special 
line  of  research.  Similarly  are  collected  at  some  length  many  publica- 
tions of  another  class,  but  also  of  rarity,  such  as  the  reports  of  the  New 
Zealand  Company,  the  Colonial  Gazette,  the  New  Zealand  Journal,  the 
Australian  and  New  Zealand  Gazette,  the  Karere  Maori,  and  the  Govern- 
ment Gazette.  The  last  of  these,  in  its  earlier  numbers  at  least,  is  not 
only  rare,  but  extremely  puzzling  to  collate. 

Whilst  every  effort  has  been  made  to  make  this  record  complete, 
it  is  probable  that  many  items  must  have  escaped  enumeration.  Any 
such  omissions  are  almost  certainly  of  little  moment,  and  it  is  not 
unlikely  that  they  are  chiefly  confined  to  the  long  list  of  versifiers 
whose  contributions,  as  before  stated,  form  a  yearly  heavy  burden. 
This  seems  the  proper  place  to  thank  Mr.  Bertram  Stevens,  of  Sydney, 
for  the  long  list  of  recent  versifiers  to  be  found  in  the  Addenda. 

Though  the  chronological  order  has  been  adhered  to  throughout 
this  work,  the  index  is  a  corrective  to  the  difficulty  that  might  be 
found  by  a  student  in  searching  for  any  particular  subject  of  which 
he  desires  to  make  a  special  study.  A  feature  of  the  index  is  that  it 
provides  a  series  of  "  subject  heads." 

A  word  should  here  be  said  with  regard  to  the  enumeration  of  the 
newspapers  of  the  colony.  The  number  of  these  is  so  extensive  that 
the  author  decided  to  detail  those  alone  which  were  issued  during 
the  earlier  days  of  the  colony. 

The  recent  increasing  association  between  New  Zealand  and  the 
Antarctic  makes  it  advisable  to  specify  Dr.  Mill's  important  biblio- 
graphy of  between  eight  and  nine  hundred  references  which  is  ap- 
pended to  the  Royal  Geographical  Society's  "  Antarctic  Manual," 
published  in  1901.  Dr.  Cockayne  has  also  published  a  bibliography 
relative  to  the  outlying  islands  of  New  Zealand  and  elsewhere,  in  the 
"  Transactions  of  the  New  Zealand  Institute  "  for  1903,  Vol.  xxxvi, 
p.  326. 

It  but  remains  to  express  the  aithor's  most  grateful  thanks  to 
the  Government  Printing  Department  for  unremitting  care  and 
attention  during  the  progress  of  this  work.  The  Government  Printer 
has  taken  great  interest  in  it,  both  before  and  since  it  was  placed  in 
his  hands.     The  Supervisor's  remarkable  knowledge  of  his  work  has 


Introduction.  vii 


been  of  the  most  constant  and  inestimable  value.  The  Reader,  upon 
whom  the  compilation  of  the  index  principally  devolved,  has  also 
rendered  invaluable  service.  To  his  work  may  well  be  applied  the 
Maori  saying  on  the  title-page,  "  He  mahi  nui  rawa  atu  " — "  Truly 
this  is  a  very  laborious  deed."  No  pains  have  been  spared  to  make 
the  index  comprehensive  and  copious. 

The  end  sought  will  be  accomplished  if  the  student  derives  as 
much  satisfaction  and  advantage  from  the  use  of  this  bibliography 
as  the  author  has  derived  pleasure  from  its  preparation. 

T.  M.  HOCKEN. 
Dunedin,  1st  June,  1909.

CORRIGENDA. 

Page  2, — "  Thevenot,  M."  Add  at  the  end  of  title  :  "  Le  tout  enrichi  de 
Pigures,  de  Plantes  |  non  decrites,  d'Animaux  inconnus  a  I'Europe,  de  Cartes 
Geogra-  |  phiques,  qui  n'ont  point  encore  ete  publiees.  |  Novvelle  Edition,  | 
augmentee  de  plusicurs  Rolations  curieuses.  |  Tome  premier.  {  Contenant  la  I  et 
II  partie,  |  A  Paris,  |  chez  Tiiomas  Moette  Libraire,  rue  de  la  Bouclerie,  a 
saint  Alexis.  |  MDCXCV^I.  |  Avec  privilege  de  Sa  Majeste."  Of  the  several  copies 
— not  editions — of  Thevenot,  the  one  I  have  noted  is  the  last  and  the  most  com- 
plete. It  contains  the  rare  chart  of  Terre  Australe,  with  Hollandia  Nova, 
Zealandia  Nova,  Terra  de  Diemen's,  and  Tasman's  route  thereon.  There  are 
also  the  63  plates  of  ancient  Mexican  figures  or  hieroglyphs  and  a  quaint 
figure  of  the  "  Dronte,  autrement  appslle  par  les  Hollandois  Dod-aers  "  (the 
dodo). 

Page  47. — Line  8.  Before  "  Voyage  de,"  &c.,  insert  "  D'Urville,  Cap.  M.  J. 
Dumont." 

Page  85. — For  still  further  reference  to  the  New  Zealand  Company,  vide 
Report  of  Select  Committee  on  New  Zealand,  1844,  and  Appendix,  pp.  211,  219, 
455,  and  786  ;  parliamentary  papers,  1845,  22nd  April ;  and  New  Zealand  Herald, 
Sth.  October,  1892. 

Page  89. — "  Reports  of  New  Zealand  Company."  The  last  sentence  but  one 
on  the  page  requires  modification.  Deficient  in  facilities  for  the  safe  keeping 
of  this  valuable  gift,  the  New  Zealand  Government  wisely  deferred  its  acceptance 
until  the  present  year  (1909). 

Page  93. — Line  25.     For  Montague  read  Montague. 

Page  95. — "  Te  Karere  o  Nui  Tireni  "  should  have  been  placed  in  the  year 
1842.  More  correct  details  of  the  editors  will  be  found  in  the  Maori  section  of  this 
volume. 

Page  122. — Line  48.     For  Montague  read  Montagne. 

Page  124.—"  [Wakefield,  E.  G.]  Rough  Draft,"  &c.  Read  this  with  the 
entry  on  page  123  :   "  The  Petition  of  the  New  Zealand  Company,"  &c. 

Page  136.—"  The  Anglo-Maori  Warder."  The  "  leader  "  of  the  18th  July 
gives  an  acute  and  remarkable  sketch  of  Governor  Grey's  character. 

Page  139. — Line  32.     For  Neu  read  Neue.  . 

Page  149.—"  Wakefield,  E.  G.  The  Southern  Colonies,"  &c.  This  important 
letter  was  reprinted  in  the  New  Zealand  Spectator  of  the  20th  February,  1850, 
and  the  Wellington  Independent  of  the  same  date.  A  previous  important  letter 
is  in  the  New  Zealand  Spectator,  15th  September,  1849. 

Page  157. — Line  2.     For  E wards  read  Edwards. 

Page  161. — Line  1.     For  [Lacatt,  — ]  read  fLucatt,  Edward.] 

Page  215. — Line  1.  For  Schmarda,  L.,  read  Schwarda,  T.  ;  also,  for  1853-6^7 
read  185.3-57. 

Page  270. — "  Wakefield,  F."  There  was  yet  another  brother,  John  Howard 
Wakefield,  who  was  a  colonel  in  the  H.E.I.C.S. 

Page  340. — "  Fabrieius  "  is  the  pen-name  of  C.  D.  Wright. 

Page  353. — Line  39.     For  Skiggen  read  Skizzen. 

Page  397. — Line  12.     For  phonogams  read  phaenogams. 

Page  400. — "  Report  of  the  First  Meeting,"  &c.  Add  at  end  of  paragrai)h 
giving  dates  of  meetings,  "  11th  January,  1909,  Brisbane." 

Page  430. — Line  28.  For  Petit  read  Servant.  Father  Petit  arrived  in 
October,  1839,  with  his  brethren  Baty  and  Epalle. 

Page  451. — Line  34.      For  Orgine  read  Origine.

BIBLIOGEAPHY 


OF   THE 


LITERATURE   OF   NEW  ZEALAND 


1643-1898. 

Heeres,  J.  E.  Tasman,  Abel  Janszoon.  Abel  Janszoon  Tasman's  |  Journal  f 
of  his  discovery  of  Van  Diemen's  Land  and  New  Zealand  in  1642  |  with 
documents  relating  to  his  exploration  of  Australia  |  in  1644  being  photo- 
lithographic facsimiles  of  the  original  |  manuscript  in  the  Colonial  Archives 
at  the  Hague  with  |  an  English  translation  and  facsimiles  of  original  map& 
to  I  which  are  added  Life  and  Labours  of  Abel  Janszoon  Tasman  |  By  J.  E. 
Heeres.  LL.D.,  Professor  at  the  Dutch  Colonial  Institute  ',  Delft  and  obser- 
vations made  with  the  compass  on  Tasman's  |  Voyage  by  Dr,  W.  van  Bem- 
melen    Assistant-Director    of    the    Royal    Meteorological    Institute    Utrecht 

I I  Amsterdam  j  Frederik  Muller  and  Co.  |  (F.  Adama  van  Scheltema 

and  Anton  Messing)  |  1898. 

Roy.  fol.,  13|  X  7f,  vellum  and  gilt ;  price,  £7.  Pp.  :  n.p.  6  ;  facsimile- 
of  original  Journal  (n.p.),  195 ;  translation,  59 ;  life  and  labours,  163  ; 
compass  observations,  21.  10  maps.  Reproductions  of  the  50  original 
illustrations  and  maps,  chiefly  coast-lines. 

This  magnificent  volume  gives  at  last  the  complete  account  of  Tasman's 
life  and  labours.  The  Journal  is  the  long-lost  one,  discovered  about  1854 
at  Batavia,  and  forms  the  source  of  the  various  abridged  and  imperfect 
texts  of  two  hundred  years  ago  and  later,  as  given  below.  It  was  first  edited 
by  Jacob  Swart  in  1860  in  the  original  Dutch. 

Tasman  (1603-59)  sailed  from  Batavia  for  Mauritius  August  14,  1642; 
discovered  and  named  Van  Diemen's  Land  ;  then,  on  December  13,  Stat«n 
Land,  as  he  first  called  New  Zealand  ;  the  Tonga  and  Fiji  Groups,  New 
Guinea,  &c.  ;    and  returned  June  15,  1643. 


Abridgments  and  Translations. 

1674.  Van  Nierop,  D.  R.  Een  kort  verhael  uyt  het  journael  \an 
den  Kommander  Abel  Jansen  Tasman  int'ontdekken  van  t'onbekende  Suit 
Landt  in  Jare  1642.  (A  short  relation  fiom  the  journal  of  the  Commander 
Abel  Jansen  Tasman,  in  the  discovery  of  the  Unknown  South  Land,  in  the 
year  1642.)     By  Dirck  Rembrantz  van  Nierop  :   Amsterdam. 


Bibliography  of  [1643-1898 


Heeres,  J.  E. — continued. 

Translations  from  this  were  soon  after  printed  in  most  European 
languages,  as  in  Narborough's  Collection,  1694  and  1711  ;  Dr.  Hook's  Philo- 
sophical Collection,  1682 ;   Thevenot,  Paris,  1681. 

1726.  Valentyn,  Frangois.  Oud  en  nieuw  Oost-Indien  .  .  .  Deel 
V.  Dordrecht  en  Amsterdam.  MDCCXXVI.  Omstanding  Verhaal  van  de 
Geschiedenissen  en  Zaaken  .  .  .  Derde  Deel  Banda,  p.  47.  (The  old  and 
new  East  Indies,  &c.  A  full  account  of  the  history  and  trade,  &c.)  Through 
his  marriage  connections  Valentyn  had  access  to  the  archives  and  journals 
of  the  Dutch  East  India  Company,  many  of  which  he  published.  His  abridg- 
ment of  Tasman  appears  in  this  huge  and  magnificently  illustrated  folio 
work  of  five  volumes  with  many  charts  and  views,  and  formed  the  chief 
source  from  which  many  others  drew — as  Dr.  John  Harris's  Collection,  1702-5, 
2  vols,  folio,  which,  edited  by  Campbell  ("  Navigantium  atque  Itinerantium 
BibUotheca  "),  appeared  in  2  vols,  folio,  1744  (from  this  Callendar  made 
his  copy) ;   De  Hondt's  Collection,  1749  ;  and  others. 

1776.  Woide,  Rev.  G.  C.  Translation  from  a  copy  of  the  original 
(abridged)  belonging  to  Mr.  (Sir  Joseph)  Banks,  by  whom  both  were  after- 
wards bequeathed  to  the  British  Museum.  This  supplied  a  still  more 
complete  account,  and  it  appears  in  "  Burney's  Voyages,"  Vol.  iii,  1813, 
forming  the  best  to  date. 

1854-60.  Swart,  Jacob.  Journaal  |  van  |  de  Reis  naar  het  On- 
bekende  |  Zuidland,  |  in  den  Jare  1642,  |  door  |  Abel  Jansz.  Tasman,  |  met 
de  Schepen  |  Heemskerck  en  de  Zeehaen.  |  Medegedeeld  en  met  eenige 
Aanteekeningen  voorzien  |  door  |  Jacob  Swart,  j  .  .  .  |  Met  eene  Kaart, 
I  Te  Amsterdam,  |  bij  de  Wed  van  Keulen.  |  1860.  (Journal  of  the  Voyage 
to  the  Unknown  Southland  in  the  year  1642  by  A.  J.  Tasman,  with  the  ships 
Heemskerk  and  Seahen.  Revised,  and  with  several  notes  and  a  chart.) 
6|  X  3^.  Pp.  viii,  189,  and  large  folding  chart  of  the  voyage.  Contains 
Tasman's  complete  journal,  his  instructions  and  various  official  letters. 
Dr.  Heeres  discusses  critically  this  and  the  other  copies. 

Vide  also  "  Earliest  Literature  relating  to  New  Zealand,"  and  "  Abel 
Tasman  and  his  Journal,"  by  Dr.  Hocken,  in  Trans.  N.Z.  Inst.,  1894-95-96. 
Also  "  New  Zealand  Reader,"  "  Voyage  of  Tasman  in  1642,"  by  Basil  Stocker.

Maps,  diagrams,  &c.     Fol.     2  vols.     Paris.     Many  plates  and  charts. 

This  interesting  collection  contained  at  least  fifty  items,  with  "  pieces 
complementaires  "  or  additions  made  from  time  to  time  until  1696.  Amongst 
these  is  "Route  d'Abel  Tasman  autour  de  la  Terre  Australe,  avec  la  de- 
couverte  de  la  Nouvelle  Zelande  et  de  la  Terre  de  Diemens,  1642-43,"  which 
forms  No.  50  in  Vol.  ii.  The  chart  of  this  voyage  forms  No.  21  in  Vol.  i, 
*'  Carte  de  cette  cinquieme  Partie  du  Monde,  1663."

Ide  Brosses,  Charles.]     Histoire  |  des  |  Navigations  1  aux  I  Terres  Australes.  | 
Contenant  ce  que  ron  s^ait  des  mcEurs  &  |  des  productions  des  Contrees 
decouvertes  jusqu'a  |  ce  jour ;    &  ou  il  est  traite  de  I'utilite  d'y  faire  de  | 
plus    amples  decouvertes,    &   des  moyens  d'y  former  |    un  etablissement. 
Nee  usquam,  &c.   Hor.  |  A  Paris,  |  Chez  Durand,  rue  du  Foin,  au  GriflFon.  | 
MDCCLVI.  I  Avec  Approbation  &  Privilege  du  Roy. 

2  vols.  4to,  7x4.  Vol.  i :  pp.  xiv,  463 ;  errata,  4.  Vol.  ii :  pp. 
514,  2.     Seven  charts,  chiefly  of  the  Southern  Hemisphere. 

Collection  of  voyages  from  Vespuccio  in  1501  to  Le  Hen-Brignon  in 
1747,  named  and  divided  for  the  first  time  into  the  three  groups  of  Australasia, 
Polynesia,  and  Magellanica,  since  adopted  by  geographers.  Tasman's 
voyage  is  taken  from  Thevenot  and  from  Bernard's  abridgment ;  Amsterdam, 
1722.  As  these  voyages  were  translated  into  English  by  Callander  in  his 
"  Terra  Australis  Cognita"  {q.v.),  they  are  not  specified  here. 

De  Brosses  (1709-77)  was  first  President  of  the  Parliament  at  Burgundy. 
His  friend  Buffon,  the  natiu'alist,  induced  him  to  publish  this  collection. 


1766-68.

tralis, or  Southern  Hemi-  |  sphere,  during  the  Sixteenth,  Seven-  |  teenth,  and 
Eighteenth  Centuries.  |  Containing  1  an  Account  of  the  Manners  of  the 
People,  and  the  |  Productions  of  the  Countries,  hitherto  found  in  the  | 
Southern  Latitudes  ;  the  Advantages  that  may  |  result  from  further  Dis- 
coveries on  this  great  Continent,  |  and  the  Methods  of  establishing  Colonies 
there  to  the  |  advantage  of  Great  Britain.  |  With  |  a  Preface  by  the  Editor, 
in  which  some  geographical,  ]  nautical,  and  commercial  Questions  are  dis- 
cussed. 1  — Vol.  I.  I  — Nee  usquam  Deus  abscidit,  |  Terras  oceano  dissoci- 
abili.  Hor.  |  — Edinburgh :  |  Printed  by  A.  Donaldson,  and  sold  at  his 
Shops  in  I  London  and  Edinburgh.  1  — MDCCLXVI.

and  New  Zelande.

the  following  Collection  of  Voyages  to  the  Southern  Hemisphere  is  inscribed 
by  his  most  obliged  and  most  faithful  humble  servant  John  Callander."

At  the  end  of  this  third  volume,  p.  745,  is  "  Craigforth,  near  Stirling, 
Feb.  25,  1768." 

Chiefly  an  incomplete  translation  of  de  Brosse's  "  Histoire  des  Naviga- 
tions aux  Terres  Australes,  1756"  (q.v.),  with  considerable  additions  from 
Hakluyt,  Purchas,  Thevenot,  and  others.  Tasman's  Journal  is  taken  from 
Campbell's  edition  of  Harris,  and  is  by  no  means  so  full  as  that  of  Valentijn. 
Callander,  who  died  in  1789,  was  a  Scotch  advocate,  and  a  somewhat  extensive 
writer.

1770-75]  New  Zealand  Literature. 


Dalrymple,  Alexander — continued. 

Tasman's  voyage  is  taken  chiefly  from  Fran9ois  Valentijn  ("  The  Old 
and  New  East  Indies,"  1726),  collated  with  De  Hondt,  Rembrandt,  and 
others  {vide  Vol.  ii,  p.  66).  The  plates  relating  to  New  Zealand  are — 1.  The 
Continent  south  of  the  Rocky  Point  [the  Steeples] ;  2.  Staten  Landt,  or  the 
States  Land,  south  of  the  Rocky  Point  [Cape  Foulwind  and  Westport]  ; 
3,  View  of  Murderers'  Bay  [Golden,  or  Massacre,  Bay] ;  4.  Abel  Tasman's 
Bay  [Blind  Bay]  ;  5.  Three  Kings'  Island. 

The  author's  translation  of  these  Spanish  and  Dutch  voyages  is  ap- 
parently faithful,  and  is  accompanied  by  numerous  critical  remarks,  notes, 
and  references,  and  a  full  index.  The  work  was  published  before  Captain 
Cook's  return  from  his  first  voyage.  Alexander  Dalrymple  (1737-1808) 
was  hydrographer  to  the  Admiralty,  and  an  able  writer.  He  firmly  believed 
in  the  existence  of  that  geographical  will-o'-the-wisp,  a  southern  continent 
(the  terra  australis  incognita).  Chagrined  at  Cook's  appointment  to  the 
command  of  the  "  Endeavour,"  instead  of  himself,  he  put  trifling  value  on 
the  discoveries  of  that  great  sailor,  which  he  criticized  mercilessly,  reiterating 
his  old  theory. 


1771. 

Dalrymple,  A.,  and  Franklin,  B.  Scheme  of  a  Voyage  to  convey  the 
Conveniences  of  lafe.  Domestic  Animals,  Corn,  Iron,  etc.,  to  New  Zealand, 
with  Dr.  Benjamin  Franklin's  Sentiments  upon  the  Subject.  By  Alexander 
Dalrymple.     London,  Aug.  29th,  1771. 

Appears  in  "  Dodsley's  xinnual  Register  "  for  1779. 

So  keen  was  the  interest  excited  by  Cook's  account  of  New  Zealand 
that  this  odd  scheme  was  devised  to  help  the  poor  natives.  Perhaps  Dal- 
rymple's  jealousy  of  Cook  was  the  motif,  as  the  former  offered  and  expected 
to  command  the  expedition.  At  any  rate,  it  is  the  first  systematic  proposal 
to  connect  the  two  countries. 

Reprinted  in  New  Zealand  Journal,  1843,  p.  42;  also  byPetherick,  of 
London,  in  1882  (^0  printed).

Translated  into  French  in  1774  by  M.  Suard  ;  4to  ;  4  vols,  and  52^ 
plates  ;  Paris  ;  Saillant  et  Nyon.  Also  8vo  ;  4  vols.,  with  atlas  in  4to  ;

Translated  into  German  in  1774,  by  J.  F.  Schiller  ;  4to  ;  3  vols.,  with 
plates  and  charts  ;  Berlin  ;  Haude  und  Spener.  An  abridgment  of  this- 
in  4to.     Another  edition,  8vo ;    4  vols.  ;    Haude  und  Spener. 

Reviewed  in  the  Edinburgh  Magazine  and  Review  (Vol.  i),  1793. 
The  voyage  was  undertaken  at  the  special  request  of  the  Royal  Society 
to  observe  the  Transit  of  Venus  in  1769  at  Otaheite  (Tahiti).  Cook,  with  his 
companions  Banks  and  Solander,  left  Plymouth  Sound  August  26,  1768, 
and  returned  July  12,  1771,  from  Otaheite;  reached  New  Zealand  (Poverty 
Bay)  October  8,  1769,  and  there  remained  until  March  31,  1770,  when  he 
sailed  for  the  east  coast  of  New  Holland  (Botany  Bay).  Took  possession  of 
the  North  Island  November,  1769,  at  Mercury  Bay,  and  of  the  South  Island 
January  30,  1770,  at  Queen  Charlotte's  Sound.  Gives  a  general  account 
of  New  Zealand,  its  discovery,  climate,  and  productions  ;  the  natives,  their 
manner  of  life,  implements  and  weapons,  religion  and  language.  On  p.  474 
et  seq.  of  Vol.  iii  (1st  ed.)  is  some  comparison  of  the  dialects  of  the  North  and 
South  Islands  and  of  Otaheite,  and  reasons  against  the  existence  of  a  southern 
continent. 

Dr.  John  Hawkesworth  (1715-73),  an  able  literary  man,  was  appointed 
by  the  Admiralty,  through  the  influence  of  his  friend  Garrick,  the  actor^ 
to  compile  and  edit  these  voyages,  for  which  it  has  been  stated,  but  incor- 


1773]  New  Zealand  Literature.  U 


Cook's  First  Voysige— continued. 

rectly,  that  he  received  £6,000.  The  severe  criticisms  prssed  on  his  work 
contributed  to  his  death,  and  are  quite  undeserved.  The  voyage  of  the 
"  Endeavour  "  was  compiled  by  him  from  the  journals  of  Cook,  Banks, 
Solander,  and  others. 

In  1893  Cook's  original  journal  of  the  first  voyage  was  published  for  the 
first  time  :  Captain  Cook's  Journal  |  during  bis  |  First  Voyage  Round  the

by  I  Captain  W.  J.  L.  Wharton,  R.N.,  F.R.S.  |  Hydrographer  of  the  Ad- 
miralty I  Illustrated  by  Maps  and  Facsimiles  |  I^ondon  |  Elliot  Stock,  62 
Paternoster  Row  |  1898.  Roy.  8vo ;  pp.  Ivi  (preface,  contents,  sketch 
of  Capt.  Cook's  life,  and  list  of  persons  in  the  "  Endeavour"),  400;  Por- 
trait of  Cook.  3  folded  charts  are  in  pocket — 1,  Cook's  chart  of  New  Zealand  ; 
-2  and  3,  east  coast-line  of  Australia,  reduced  from  Cook's  originals  in  the 
British  Museum,  and  with  parallel  coast-lines  from  recent  surveys.  Dedi- 
cated to  Admiral  H.R.H.  the  Duke  of  Edinburgh.  Some  copies  are  in  large 
paper  and  bound  in  wood  taken  from  Capt.  Cook's  tree,  a  giant  elm  said 
to  have  been  planted  by  Cook,  which  was  blown  down  at  Clapham  Common 
fluring  a  gale  in  1892.  The  portrait  is  reproduced  from  Dance's  painting 
at  Greenwich  Hospital.  The  preface  gives  history  of  the  original  triplicate 
journals.  The  editor.  Rear- Admiral  Sir  W.  J.  L.  Wharton,  died  on  29th 
September,  1905,  at  Cape  Town,  during  the  meeting  there  of  the  Btitisji 
Association. 


Dalrymple,  Alexander.  A  |  Letter  |  from  |  Mr.  Dalrymple  |  to  |  Dr.  Hawkes- 
worth,  I  occasioned  by  |  some  groundless  and  illiberal  Imputations  |  in  his 
I  Account  of  the  late  Voyages  to  the  South.  |  London.  |  Printed  for  J. 
Nourse,  Bookseller  in  Ordinary  to  his  Majesty,  in  the  |  Strand ;  T.  Payne, 
near  the  Mews-Gate ;  Brotherton  and  |  Sewell,  Cornhill ;  B.  White,  Fleet- 
Street  ;  J.  Robson,  Old  |  Bond-Street ;  P.  Elmsly,  Strand ;  T.  Davies, 
Russell  Street,  Co  vent-  |  (harden  ;  and  .S.  Leacroft,  Charing  -  Cross.  | 
MDCCLXXIIT. 

Small  4to.  Pp.  35.  "  Chart  of  the  South  Pacifick  Ocean,  pointing  out 
the  Discoveries  made  therein  previous  to  1764." 

In  this  rare  "  tract  "  or  pamphlet  Dalrymple  bitterly  criticizes  Cook's 
voyage  in  the  "  Endeavour,"  as  edited  by  Ha wkes worth,  whom  he  accuses 
of  ignorance  and  unfitness  for  his  task.  He  asserts  that  the  reasons  adduced 
against  the  existence  of  a  southern  continent  are  quite  inconclusive,  and 
those  in  favour  ignored.  Dated  22nd  June,  1773,  when  Cook  had  been 
ten  months  on  his  second  voyage  ;  hence  from  him  there  could  be  no  reply ; 
Hawkesworth  replies  in  the  2nd  ed.  of  his  "  Account,"  &c. 


IJPitzgerald,  Prof.  (?)j     An  i  Epistle  1  from  |  Oberea,  Queen  of  Otaheite,  to  j 
Joseph  Banks,  Esq.  |  Translated  by  T.Q.Z.  Esq.  |  Professor  of  the  Otaheite

House,  in  Piccadilly.  |  MDCCLXXIV.  |  [Price  One  Shilling.] 

Sm.  4to,  7^  x  5.  Pp.15.  Paper  wrapper.  Introduction  dated  Dublin, 
20th  September,  1773,  date  of  1st  ed.     About  200  lines  of  rhyming  verse. 

Perhaps  this  and  succeeding  "  Epistles "  were  written  by  Professor 
Fitzgerald,  of  Trinity  College,  Dublin,  author  of  a  small  volume  of  poems. 
A  clever,  good-humoured  skit,  directed  against  Banks  and  his  editor,  Hawkes- 


12  Bibliography  op  [1773- 


[Fitzgerald,  Prof,  (^l)]— continued. 

worth,  and  pointed  in  the  footnotes  with  apposite  classical  verses.  Jocularly 
recounts  certain  amorous  incidents  of  the  voyage,  and  the  reader  may  parallel 
Banks  and  Oberea  with  Ulysses  and  Calypso.  Oberea  mourns  her  loss,  and 
wonders  whether  her  lover  will  return.

Collier  (p.  5),  repeating  James  Jackson  ("  Centenaire  de  la  Mort  de- 
Cook — Bibliographie,  No.  18"),  states  "that  another  edition  appeared  the 
same  year,"  &c.  I  think  this  is  a  mistake,  and  that  Jackson  has  con- 
founded together  the  two  issues  of  1773  and  1784,  as  here  given.  Jackson's- 
catalogue,  though  most  valuable,  is  often  faulty.

1773J  New  Zealand  Literature.  13 


Parkinson,  Sydney — continued. 

The  author,  a  clever  young  artist,  engaged  by  Mr.  Banks  at  £80  per 
annum  as  natural -history  draughtsman,  died  at  Batavia  of  dysentery  26th 
January,  1771,  on  the  homeward  voyage.  His  brother  Stanfield,  to  anti- 
cipate Ha wkes worth's  account  of  the  voyage,  hurriedly  published  this 
journal.  After  a  few  copies  had  appeared  the  further  issue  was  stopped  by  an 
injunction  in  Chancery,  on  the  score  of  infringement  of  Dr.  Hawkesworth's 
rights,  and  of  material  Iselonging  to  Mr.  Banks.  (For  details  of  this  vide  Trans.. 
N.Z.  Inst.,  Vol.  xxvii,  "  Earliest  Literature,  &c.,  of  New  Zealand,"  by  Dr.. 
Hocken.)  Dr.  Fothergill,  a  charitable  friend  of  the  Parkinsons,  afterwards 
bought  the  remainder,  which  appeared  in  1784  as  the  reissue,  with  the  added 
Appendix.     46  pages  relate  to  New  Zealand. 


1774.

Further  raillery,  under  the  guise  of  a  love- song  : — 

Alas  !  in  vain  I  write,  in  vain  I  sigh. 
My  life  is  torture,  for  thou  art  not  nigh. 


Otaheite  :  I  a  I  Poem.  |  Venient  annis     .     .     .     Seneca  in  Medea.  |  London :  f 
Printed  for  the  Author ;  1  and  sold  by  C.  Bathurst,  opposite  St.  Dun&t-an's

Sm.  4to,  7  X  4^.     Paper  wrapper.     Pp.  16. 

About  300  lines  of  poor  verse,  descriptive  of  the  explorer's  happy  re- 
turn, of  the  benefits  and  added  knowledge  he  confers,  and  of  the  countries 
visited— chiefly  Otaheite,  which  s^ems  to  have  been  viewed  as  a  very  Mecca. 


The  London  Magazine  for  August  1774,  p.  363,  contains  a  "  Genuine  Account 
of  Omiah,  a  Native  of  Otaheite,  a  new  discovered  Island  in  the  South-Seas,, 
lately  brought  over  to  England  by  Capt.  Fourneaux.  (With  an  elegant 
Engraving)."     Pp.  2. 

Omai,  a  native  of  Ulietea,  one  of  the  Society  or  Tahitian  Group,  was 
brought  to  England  in  the  "  Adventure  "  by  Capt.  Furneaux  in  July,  1774 — 
Capt.  Cook's  second  voyage.  He  remained  in  England  two  years,  and  re- 
turned in  1776  with  Cook,  in  the  "  Resolution."  Sir  Joshua  Reynolds  painted 
his  portrait,  which  was  engraved  by  Jacobi,  and  published  in  1780.  Aa 
account  of  him  is  also  given  in  G.  Forster  :   Preface,  p.  xiv.

SMara,  or  Marra,  John.  (?)]  Journal  \  of  the  |  Resolution's  Voyage,  1  in  1772, 
1773,  1774,  and  1775.  |  On  |  Discovery  to  the  Southern  .Hemisphere,  |  by 
which  I  the  Non-existence  of  an  undiscovered  Continent,  I  between  the 
Equator  and  the  50th  Degree  of  Southern  |  Latitude,  is  demonstratively 
proved.  |  Also  a  |  Journal  ]  of  the  |  Adventure's  Voyage,  |  in  the  years  1772, 
1773,  and  1774.  1  With  an  Account  of  the  Separation  of  the  two  Ships,  |  and 
the  most  remarkable  Incidents  that  befel  each.  |  Interspersed  with  |  Historical 
and  Geographical  Descriptions  of  |  the  Islands  and  Countries  discovered  in  the 
Course  |  of  their  respective  Voyages.  |  Illustrated  with  a  Chart,  |  in  which  the

Printed  for  F.  Newbery,  at  the  Corner  of  |  St.  Paul's  Church- Yard.  1  1775. 

8vo.  Pp.  xiii,  errata  and  directions  to  binder  (p.  1),  328.  Five  plates 
or  cuts  :  1.  View  of  harbour  and  plantations  in  the  Island  of  Rotterdam 
[Anamoka],  with  the  manner  of  the  inhabitants  sitting  and  standing  (copied 
from  "  Dalrymple's  Voyages  ").  2.  Fields  of  ice  by  which  their  passage 
was  obstructed  in  lat.  67  deg.  10  min.  S.  ;  3.  Landing  of  part  of  the  Ad- 
venture's crew  in  search  of  their  companions,  who  were  murdered  and  eaten 
by  the  savages  of  New-Zealand  ;  4.  View  of  two  burning  mountains  [Tonga 
Group]  ;   5.  Remarkable  high  mountain  [59°  S.,  Southern  Thule]. 

Forms  Vol.  5  of  "Voyages  Round  the  World,"  published  by  F.  New- 
bery. Contains  many  incidents,  &c.,  not  in  "  Cook's  Second  Voyage," 
and  published  a  year  before  it. 

This  is  the  very  rare  account  of  the  second  voyage,  published  surrepti- 
tiously and  anonymously  about  eighteen  months  before  Cook's  own  account. 
Probably  John  Marra,  one  of  the  gunner's  mates,  supplied  material  from  his 
private  journal,  which  was  put  into  shape  by  some  literary  person  in  the 
pay  of  Newbery.  Page  325  says,  "  Some  (officers)  who  had  kept  memorials 
by  way  of  exercise  .  .  .  reserved  their  labours  to  gratify  their  friends.  Of 
this  kind  is  the  journal  now  submitted  to  the  public."  The  preface  discloses 
considerable  knowledge  of  private  matters  connected  with  the  voyage,  and 
gives  an  account  of  the  causes  which  led  IVIr.  Banks  and  his  staff  to  with- 
•  draw  in  high  dudgeon  from  the  expedition  at  the  last  moment.  ( Vide  also 
a  letter  from  Capt.  Cook  in  Vol.  i,  part  1,  of  the  "  Historical  Records  of  New 
South  Wales,"  p.  383.) 

Many  of  the  particulars  of  the  voyage  are  additional.

linnsean  classification.  Descriptions  short,  and  confined  to  the  floral 
organs,  which  are  alone  delineated.  One  Cryptogam  {Aytonia)  only  described. 
Of  the  75  plants,  24  are  found  in  New  Zealand.  Dr.  Sparrman  described 
the  plants,  George  Forster  drew  them,  whilst  J.  R.  Forster  supervised  and 
revised  and  took  charge  of  the  zoological  collection.  ( Vide  Forster's  "  Pro- 
dromus.'')  The  name  is  perpetuated  in  the  small  genus  Forstera,  found  in 
New  Zealand,  Tasmania,  and  South  America. 

A  German  translation  appeared  at  Stuttgardt  in  1779,  by  Johann  Simon 
ICrener,  botanist  in  the  Ducal  Military  Academy  of  Wirtemberg :  Sm.  4to ; 
pp.  (33  n.p.),  160.  The  plates  of  the  original  are  compressed  into  17  folded

stanne"  {Leptosperrmim  and  Dacrydivm),  with  description.  Notes  also  on 
New  Zealand  flax  and  the  bread-fruit.     Idem,  Gottingen,  1776.

instead  of  dressing  him  out  in  a  bag  and  sword,  he  was  not  instructed  in

him  favour,  and  whom  he  regrets  to  leave.

Translated  into  French  by  A.  F.  L.  de  Freville  :  8vo  ;  Amsterdam, 
Paris ;  Pissot,  Nyon ;  1777.  Translated  by  M.  Suard :  4to ;  5  vols.  ; 
65  plates  (of  which  No.  10  is  double,  "  Trombes  de  Mer  aupr^s  de  la  Nouvelle 
Zelande  ")  ;  1778.  In  this  edition  Suard  has  supplemented  Cook's  narra- 
tive with  passages,  in  inverted  commas,  from  Forster.  Vol.  5  contains 
Forster's  "  Observations."  Another  edition  by  Suard  without  Forster's 
"  Observations  "  :    8vo  ;    6  vols.  ;    Paris  ;    1778(?). 

A  special  feature  of  the  second  voyage  was  to  decide  the  question  of 
an  antarctic  continent — the  terra  australis  incognita.  The  vessels  left 
Plymouth  July  13,  1772,  and  returned  July  30,  1775.  From  the  Cape,  reached 
Dusky  Bay  March  26,  1773,  remaining  until  May  11.  Corrected  longi- 
tude of  previous  voyage,  which  had  been  placed  40'  too  far  east  {vide  also 
Vol.  ii,  p.  161).  Then  to  Ship  Cove,  Queen  Charlotte's  Sound,  until  June  7, 
and  thence  to  Otaheite.  From  New  Amsterdam  (Tonga)  returned  to  Queen 
Charlotte's  Sound,  remaining  from  November  3  to  25.  Then  the  search  for 
a  southern  continent.  Made  Ship  Cove  again  October  19,  1774,  from  New 
Caledonia,  discovering  Norfolk  Island  on  the  way.  Left  for  Terra  del  Fuego 
November  10. 

Further  account  of  the  country  and  natives,  with  astronomical  observa- 
tions. Introduction  of  pigs,  sheep,  goats,  and  geese.  Distributed  to  the 
natives  visited  the  bronze  and  copper  medals  supplied  by  the  Admiralty — 
head  of  George  III,  surrounded  by  "  George  III,  King  of  Great  Britain, 
France,  and  Ireland "  ;  obverse,  representation  of  the  two  vessels,  with 
"  Resolution  and  Adventure  "  over  them,  and  below  "  Sailed  from  England, 
March  MDCCLXXII."  Contains  Captain  Furneaux's  narrative  of  his  sepa- 
ration. Introduction  gives  a  concise  but  excellent  account  of  previous 
voyages  in  the  Pacific.  The  "Voyage,"  though  nominally  by  Cook  himself, 
was  really  edited  by  the  Rev.  Dr.  Douglas,  afterwards  Bishop  of  Carlisle, 
and  then  of  Salisbury.

Forster,  George,  F.R.8. — continued. 

insere  U  relation  du  capitaine  Furneaux  et  celle  de  MM.  Forster,  traduit  de 
I'anglois  (dessins  par  M.  Hodges)."  Paris  ;  Hotel  de  Thou  ;  6  vols.  8vo, 
et  atlas  in  4to;  1778.  2nd  ed. :  Merigot  jeune;  1792.  Another  edition, 
"traduit  de  I'anglois  par  J.  B.  Ant.  Suard";  1778;  in  5  vols.  The  same, 
translated  into  Russian  by  D.  Gutuz,  of  5  vols.  ;  St.  Petersburg  ;  1796-99. 
The  above  contain  Cook's  second  voyage,  as  well  as  the  two  Forster s' 
works. 

It  was  understood  that  the  account  of  this,  Cook's  second  voyage,  should 
be  written  by  Forster  the  elder  (J.  R.),  naturalist  of  the  expedition.  This 
arrangement  failed,  being  virtually  forbidden  by  the  Admiralty.  Not  to 
be  foiled,  this  account  was  undertaken  by  his  son,  John  George  Adam 
(1754-93),  a  young  man  of  but  twenty- two,  "assisted"  by  his  father,  and 
it  anticipated  Cook's  narrative  by  three  or  four  months.  Whilst  condemn- 
ing the  method,  the  result  proved  a  valuable  possession.  The  same  veto 
doubtless  further  resulted  in  the  "  Observations,"  &c.,  of  J.  R.  Forster. 
The  illustrations,  being  the  joint  property  of  Captain  Cook  and  Forster,  are 
the  same  as  in  Cook. 

A  fluent  and  most  interesting  account  of  the  voyage,  with  much  new 
matter.  New  Zealand  visited  three  times.  It  begot  controversial  pamphlets 
from  Wales,  the  astronomer,  a  "  Reply,"  and  a  "  Letter  to  Lord  Sandwich  " 
iq.v.),  in  which  Forster  has  certainly  the  best  of  the  argument. 


W"ales,  W.,  F.R.8.,  and  Bayly,  W.  The  original  |  Astronomical  Observations, 
!  made  in  the  course  of  !  A  Voyage  towards  the  South  Pole,  ]  and  |  round  the

4to,  9  X  6^.  Pp.  Iv,  385.  4  plates.  Dedicated  to  Earl  of  Sandwich. 
Introduction  gives  account  of  new  instruments  used,  and  of  preceding  primi- 
tive ones,  in  navigating.  At  Queen  Charlotte's  Sound  the  clock  went  "  with 
greater  regularity  from  day  to  day  than  it  had  done  in  any  other  place." 
Philosophical  reasons  against  a  southern  continent,  complete  tables  and 
journals  —  nautical,  meteorological,  compass- variation,  &c.  Wales  died 
1798,  set.  64.  He  revised  and  pubHshed  in  1788  the  nautical  papers  of  Green, 
who  accompanied  Capt.  Cook  on  his  first  voyage  as  astronomer,  and  died 
after  leaving  Batavia  {q.v.).

Sm.  4to,  7i  X  5.  Pp.  ii,  25,  6.  Dated  "  Percy  St.,  1st  of  June,  1778." 
Very  rare. 

The  writer  and  his  father,  as  naturalists  of  Cook's  second  voyage,  were 
promised  valuable  emoluments  on  its  completion.  Written  in  eloquent 
and  piteous  style,  the  letter  lays  a  strong  indictment  of  injustice,  dishonesty.

and  broken  promises,  and  supplies  an  example  of  the  proud  man's  contumely 
and  insolence  of  oflfice.  Some  details  of  life  on  board  the  "  Resolution,'* 
and  of  the  publication  of  the  "  Voyage."  Soon  afterwards  father  and  son 
left  England  finally.

Here  Forster  the  younger  replies  to  and  animadverts  upon  Mr.  Wales's 
"  Remarks  "  in  a  much  more  polite  style  than  that  adopted  by  his  adversary, 
and  apparently  carries  off  the  honours  of  war. 


Porster,  John  Reinold,  LL.D.  Observations  [  made  during  a  j  Voyage  round 
the  World,  |  on  |  Physical  Geography,  |  Natural  History,  |  and  |  Ethic  Philo- 
sophy. I  Especially  on  |  1.  The  Earth  and  its  Strata,  |  2.  Water  and  the

4to.  Pp.  iii,  16,  649 ;  errata,  list  of  subscribers,  and  comparative 
table  of  46  words  in  15  languages  (chiefly  Polynesian).  Dedicated  to  the 
Royal  Society,  by  whom  he  was  recommended  as  naturalist  of  Cook's  second 
voyage,  vice  Banks,  who  had  retired  {vide  Journal  of  Sir  Jos.  Banks). 

An  excellent  account  of  the  countries  visited  in  1772-75,  prefaced  by 
short  journal  of  daily  events.  The  remarks  on  the  human  species  specially 
interesting  and  detailed.  Forster  was  accompanied  by  his  son,  John  George 
Adam,  a  youth  of  seventeen  years  of  age. 

A  French  translation  appeared  in  4to  ;  Paris ;  1778.  A  Dutch  trans- 
lation in  8vo  ;  Haarlem  ;  1778.  In  Swedish,  issued  in  parts  in  the  "  Biblio- 
th^que  Historique  "  ;  1775.  In  German  ;  8vo  ;  Berlin  ;  1783  and  1785  ; 
translated  by  his  son.     (Jackson,  "  Centenaire.") 

Johann  Reinhold  Forster  (1729-98)  was  a  Prussian  ;  resided  many  years 
in  England,  but  left  with  his  son  for  Germany  owing  to  disputes  connected 
with  the  voyage,  and  became  Professor  of  Natural  History  at  Halle.

walking  on  the  pavement,  whilst  a  third  is  floating  in  the  arms  of  a  flying

Some  raillery  respecting  Cook's  voyage,  with  light  satire  on  English 
customs  and  manners,  somewhat  after  Gulliver.  The  author's  "  dedication  " 
and  introductory  "  card  "  imitate  the  style  of  Dalrymple,  and  banter  his 
jealousy  of  Cook  and  his  fancied  southern  continent.

Neither  Dr.  Forster  nor  his  son  was  persona  grata  on  the  "  Resolution," 
and  their  joint  publication  intensified  the  bad  feeling,  because  of  many  observa- 
tions and  statements  it  contains.  These  Mr.  Wales  bitterly  criticizes  at  great 
length,  accusing  the  two,  in  no  measured  terms,  of  misstatement,  untruth, 
ineanness,  ignorance,  and  cow.irdice.

[Ledyard,  John.  (?)]  Journal  \  of  |  Captain  Cook's  |  last  1  Voyage  |  to  the  | 
Pacific  Ocean,  1  on  I  Discovery ;  |  performed  in  the  |  Years  177(),  1777,  1778, 
1779,  i  illustrated  with  |  Cuts,  and  a  Chart,  showing  the  Tracts  of  |  the 
Ships  employed  in  this  Expedition.  |  Faithfully  narrated  from  the  original 
MS.  I  London  :  |  Piinted  for  E.  Newbery,  at  the  corner  of  |  St.  Paul's  Church 
Yord.  I  MDCCLXXXI. 

8vo.  Pp.  xlvi,  396.  Chart  and  5  cuts,  one  representing  the  murder 
of  Capt.  Cook  at  Owhyee. 

The  introduction  gives  a  precis  of  South  Sea  discovery  and  of  attempts 
to  discover  a  North-west  Passage.  Gives  at  length  the  particulars  of  a  love 
episode  between  Gowhanne,  a  New  Zealand  girl,  and  one  of  the  sailors.  War 
and  antipathy  always  existed  between  the  natives  of  the  two  islands,  whilst 
those  of  the  North  were  friendly  with  each  other.  Details  of  Cook's  death 
differ  considerably  from  other  accounts. 

A  surreptitious  and  anonymous  publication,  anticipating  the  authorised 
account  by  more  than  two  years  ;  probably  written  by  John  Ledyard,  a 
sergeant  of  marines  on  the  "  Resolution,"  despite  the  statement  that  his 
private  journal  was  seized  {vide  his  "  Life,"  p.  36  :    Cambridge  ;    1829). 

It  was  translated  into  French  :  Troisi^me  j  Voyage  |  de  Cook  j  ou  Jour- 
nal d'une  expedition  faite  dans  |  la  Mer  Pacifique  du  Sud  at  du  Nord,  J  en 
1776-77-78-79  |  &  80.  |  Traduit  de  I'Anglois.  |  A  Paris,  |  Chez  Pissot,  pere 
et  fils,  Libraire,  quai  des  Augustins.  Laporte,  Libraire,  rue  des  Noyers.  | 
MDCCLXXXII.  j  Avec  Approbation  et  Privilege  du  Roi.  8vo;  pp.  v^ 
508  ;    map  and  illustration. 

On  Ledyard' s  return  to  America,  his  native  country,  he  published  an 
edition  with  almost  the  same  title  at  Hartford,  q.v.

A  running,  though  good,  history  of  the  voyage.  Five  pages  relate  to- 
New  Zealand.  The  best  descriptions  are  of  the  Friendly,  Society,  and  Sand- 
wich Islands,  King  George's  Sound  (north-west  coast  of  America)  with  vocabu- 
lary, and  Nootke ;    to  these  the  illustrations  refer.

Tract  iii,  "  Observations  on  the  Diseases  prevalent  in  the  South  Sea. 
Islands,  particularly  the  Lues  Venerea,  with  some  remarks  concerning  it& 
first  appearance  in  Europe." 

Discusses  the  question  in  connection  with  Cook's  voyages  and  those^ 
prior  to  them,  chiefly  the  French,  and  concludes,  contrary  to  Dr.  Forster 
(who  contends  that  the  disease  originated  from  libidinous  and  promiscuous, 
excesses,  and  was  not  imported),  that  it  was  first  communicated,  probably 
by  M.  Bougainville's  crew. 


*  Ledyard,  John.  A  journal  of  Capt.  Cook's  last  voyage  to  the  Pacific  Ocean 
and  in  ({uest  of  a  North- West  Passage,  performed  in  the  years  1776,  1777, 
1778,  and  1779,  illustrated  with  a  chart  showing  the  tracks  of  the  shipa 
employed  in  the  expedition.  Faithfvilly  narrated  from  the  original  MSS. 
of  Ml.  John  Ledyard.     Hartford,  Conn.  ;    Nathaniel  Patten  ;    1783, 

12mo.     Pp.  208. 

John  Ledyard  (1751-88)  was  an  adventurous  American  of  some  educa- 
tion. Visiting  England,  he  entered  as  corporal  of  marines  under  Captain. 
Cook,  then  about  to  sail  on  his  third  voyage.  Of  this  he  kept  a  journal,, 
which  was  claimed  by  the  Admiralty.  On  his  return  to  America  he  wrote 
another  account  from  memory,  as  above,  parts  of  which  differ  from  the 
ordinary  version  of  the  voyage,  especially  that  which  relates  to  Cook's  death. 
He  died  at  Cairo,  when  about  to  explore  in  Africa  on  behalf  of  the  African. 
Association.     (Vide  his  "  Life.") 


1783-1891.

Orozet'S  Voyage,  and  de  Surville's  Voyage — continued. 

Sm.  8vo.  Pp.  viii,  290.  7  plates  and  charts  :  1.  Four  small  insets 
— the  part  of  New  Zealand  traversed  by  Marion  (a  mistake ;  it  should 
be  by  de  Surville) ;  Aneres  Bay  ;  coast  of  Van  Diemen's  Land  traversed  by 
Marion ;  plan  of  Port  Marion  (Bay  of  Islands).  2.  Tacouri,  a  tatued 
native.     3.    New    Zealand    girl.      4.    A    native,    armed    and    in    full    dress. 

5.  New  Zealand  woman  and  child.  6.  A  cedar  (kauri).  7.  Sketch  of  land 
discovered  by  de  Surville,  called  Arsascides  (Solomon  Islands). 

Usually  known  as  "  Crozet's  Voyage." 

The  Abbe  Alexis  Rochon,  who  purposed  accompanying  the  ill-fated 
expedition,  drew  up  this  account.  The  "  Mascarin "  and  the  "  Marquis 
<le  Castries"  sailed  from  the  Isle  of  France  (Mauritius)  October,  1771,  the 
course  being  by  the  Cape,  Van  Diemen's  Land,  New  Zealand,  Guam,  Manila, 
and  home.  After  a  month's  stay  at  the  Bay  of  Islands  Marion  and  twenty-six 
of  his  crew  were  massacred  in  June,  1772,  when  the  command  was  assumed 
by  Crozet.  Excellent  description  of  New  Zealand,  productions,  natives, 
-and  their  habits.  De  Surville  anchored  at  Mongonui  in  the  "  Saint  Jean- 
Baptiste  "  in  December,  1769,  and  named  it  "  Lauriston  Bay  "  (Doubtless 
Bay,  of  Cook),  after  James  Francis  Law,  of  Lauriston,  in  Scotland,  Governor 
of  Pondicherry,  of  the  French  settlements  in  the  East,  created  Comte  de 
Tancarville  for  his  brilliant  services  in  India,  and  nephew  of  John  Law, 
Comptroller-General  of  French  Finance  and  author  of  variovis  wild  schemes. 
De  Surville  kidnapped  on  board  his  ship  the  chief  Naginoui,  and  to  this 
misconduct  Rochon  attributes  the  massacre  three  years  later

Cook's  Third  Voyage.  A  |  Voyage  |  to  the  !  Pacific  Ocean.  |  Undertaken, 
1  by  the  Command  of  his  Majesty,  |  for  making  |  Discoveries  in  the  Northern 
Hemisphere.  |  To  determine  I  the  Position  and  Extent  of  the  West  Side 
of  North  America  ;  |  its  Distance  from  Asia  ;  and  the  Practicability  of  a  | 
Northern  Passage  to  Europe.  |  Performed  under  the  Direction  of  |  Captains 
Cook,  Clerke,  and  Gore,  \  In  his  Majesty's  Ships  the  Resolution  and  Dis- 
covery. I  In  the  Years  1776,  1777,  1778,  1779,  and  1780.  |  In  Three  Volumes. 
I  Vol.  I  and  II  written  by  Captain  James  Cook,  F.R.S.  |  Vol.  Ill  by  Captain 
James  King,  LL.D  and  F.R.S.  f  Illustrated  with  Maps  and  Charts,  from  the

London  :  |  Printed  by  W.  and  A.  Strahan  :  !  For  G.  Nicol,  Bookseller  to  His 
Majesty,  in  the  Strand ;  |  and  T.  Cadell,  in  the  Strand.  |  MDCCI.XXXIV. 

4to.  3  vols,  and  imperial  folio  of  plates.  87  plates  and  charts.  Cook  » 
third  voyage. 

Vol.  i:  Contents  (n.p.),  6  pages;  introduction  and  eulogy  of  Cook, 
xcvi ;  421.  Vol.  ii :  Contents  (n.p.),  10  pages;  549.  Vol.  iii :  Contents 
(n.p.),  9  pages  ;  558.  Seven  appendixes.  The  atlas  has  63  plates,  one  only 
of  which,  "  The  Inside  of  a  Hippah,"  relates  to  New  Zealand.  Some  copies 
have  a  64th  plate,  engraved  by  Bartolozzi,  representing  the  death  of  Capt. 
Cook.  The  remaining  24  charts  and  land  -  views  are  distributed  through 
the  vols. 

2nd  ed.,  1785.  "  Printed  by  H.  Hughs  for  G.  Nicol."  Title-page 
much  abridged,  the  portions  "  To  determine  .  .  .  Europe,"  and  "  Illus- 
trated .  .  .  Artists  "  being  omitted.  On  that  of  Vol.  i  is,  engraved  by 
Hogg,  the  obverse  of  the  medal  struck  by  the  Royal  Society  in  1784 — Cook's 
head  in  profile  ;  round  it,  "  Jac.  Cook  Oceani  Investigator  Acerrimus  "  ;  and  on 
the  exergue,  "  Reg.  Soc.  Lond.  Socio  Suo."  This  vol.  has  a  frontispiece  of 
Capt.  Cook,  engraved  by  F.  Bartolozzi  from  the  painting  done  by  J.  Webber 
at  the  Cape  of  Good  Hope.  Contents  {n.p.),  8  pages;  xcvi,  421.  Vol.  ii: 
Title-page  has  reverse  of  Royal  Society  medal — Britannia  with  trident 
impaling  a  globe  ;  round  her,  "  Nil  Intentatum  nostri  Liqvere  "  ;  and  on 
the  exergue,  "  Auspiciis  Georgii  III."  Contents  {n.p.),  12  pages;  548. 
Vol.  iii :  Title-page  has  medallion  portrait  of  Capt.  King,  engraved  by  J. 
Hogg  from  S.  Shelley's  painting ;  round  it,  "  Jac.  King,  LL.D.,  F.R.S." 
This  vol.  has  frontispiece  of  Capt.  King,  engraved  by  Bartolozzi  from  J. 
Webber  s  painting.     Contents  (n.p.),  11  pages  ;    556. 

The  portraits  are  not  in  all  copies.  This  and  the  third  are  the  best 
editions,  letterpress  and  paper  being  superior.  The  1st  ed.  has  neither 
portraits  nor  medallions,  but  there  is  some  irregularity  in  the  editions  with 
regard  to  the  portraits. 

3rd  ed.,  1785.  Some  copies  of  all  the  editions  have  the  plates  inter- 
spersed, and  then  no  separate  atlas. 

In  the  Appendixes  are  "  Table  of  Numerals,  exhibiting  the  Affinity 
and  Extent  of  Language  which  is  found  to  prevail  in  all  the  Islands  of  the 
Eastern  Sea,  and  derived  from  that  spoken  on  the  Continent  of  Asia  in  the 
Country  of  the  Malayes  "  (42  specimens)  ;  "  Vocabulary  of  the  Language 
of  the  Friendly  Islands  "  (about  600  words) ;  "  Vocabulary  of  Language  at 
Nootka  "  (about  300  words)  ;  "  Vocabulary  of  the  Language  of  Atooi,  one 
of  the  Sandwich  Islands  "  (about  250  words) ;  "  Table  of  Affinity  between 
the  Languages  of  Nootka  and  Norton  Sound,  and  of  Greenlanders  and  Esqui- 
maux "  (about  50  words). 


Bibliography  of  [1784 


Cook's  Third  "VoySLge— continued. 

Translated  into  French  by  Demeunier ;  4to ;  4  vols.  (88  plates)  ; 
Paris ;  Pancoucke ;  1785 :  also  8vo ;  4  vols.,  with  altas  in  4to :  also 
18mo;  3  vols.;  Paris;  Moutard :  also  8vo ;  8  vols.;  Paris;  Hotel  de 
Thou:  also  4to ;  4  vols.;  Hotel  de  Thou :  all  published  in  1785.  Also 
other  editions  in  1785,  1816,  1819,  which  are  noted  in  the  "  Centenaire  de 
la  Mort  de  Cook,"  Jackson.  This  article,  published  in  the  "  Bulletin  de  la 
Societe  de  Geographic  "  for  May,  1879,  has  a  very  full  list  of  the  literature 
connected  with  Cook's  voyages,  including  Forster,  Wales,  and  other  com- 
panions. The  introduction  sketches  the  history  of  immediately  preceding 
discovery  in  the  South  Seas  and  the  efforts  to  find  a  North-west  Passage  ; 
and  gives  the  "  Secret  Instructions  "  to  Cook.  Dr.  John  Douglas  again 
edited  the  first  two  volumes  of  the  voyage  from  Cook's  manuscript.  King 
editing  his  own  (the  third). 

A  chief  object  of  this.  Cook's  last  voyage,  was  the  discovery  of  a  north- 
west passage  between  the  Pacific  and  Atlantic.  The  vessels  left  Plymouth 
12th  July,  1776,  and  returned  home  4th  October,  1780.  Refreshed  at  Ship 
Cove  from  12th  February,  1777  to  the  25th,  and  then  sailed  for  Otaheite.  Dr. 
Anderson,  the  surgeon,  gives  a  valuable  resume  of  his  observations  on  the 
natives  and  the  natural  history  of  New  Zealand,  and  a  comparative  list  of 
Otaheiti  and  New  Zealand  words  (43  in  number).  Capt.  Cook  was  killed 
at  Karakakooa,  Bay,  Owhyhee  (Hawaii),  on  14th  February,  1779,  Capt. 
King  then  assuming  the  command.  Most  of  the  interesting  collection  made 
in  the  three  voyages  was  variously  dispersed,  and  not  preserved  by  Great 
Britain.  It  was  chiefly  purchased  for  the  Vienna  Museum  in  1806,  by 
order  of  the  Emperor  Franz,  at  the  auction  of  the  Parkinson  Museum, 
previously  known  as  the  Lever  Museum.  In  the  New  Zealand  Journal  for 
1848,  p.  89,  is  a  daily  abstract  of  Cook's  sojourns  in  New  Zealand.

Thesis  for  his  M.D.  degree,  on  the  edible  plants  of  the  South  Pacific 
islands.  The  preface  of  20  pages,  in  simple  Latin,  gives  a  short  account 
of  the  characteristics  and  customs  of  the  various  islanders.  Describes  54 
plants  used  as  food,  26  of  which  are  new,  16  being  found  in  New  Zealand, 
and  all  described  at  some  length.

4to.     Pp.  (preface,  2)  34.     Very  rare. 

Confirms  and  adds  considerably  to  the  account  given  by  Capt.  King. 
Originally  appeared  in  Andrew  Kip|)is's  "  Biographia  Britannica,"  of  five 
huge  folio  vols.  ;  London  ;  1777-93.  Cook  was  first  clubbed,  then  stabbed 
with  one  of  the  iron  daggers  bartered  to  the  natives  a  few  days  before.  The 
conduct  of  the  lieutenant  severely  censured.  Gives  admirable  pen-and-ink 
sketch  of  Cook,  and  considers  Sherwin's  plate,  after  Dance,  most  excellent 
likeness,  "  the  only  one  I  have  seen  that  bears  any  resemblance  to  him." 
Cannot  say  when  or  whence  venereal  disease  was  introduced,  or  whether 
indigenous.  Concludes  it  was  introduced  "  before  we  discovered  the  islands." 
{Vide  Dr.  Martin  Wall's  "  Dissertations,"  &c.  ;    1783.) 


Sparrman,    X.,    M.D.      A  |  Voyage  I  to  the  |  Cape  of  Good  Hope,  |  towards 
the  I  Antarctic  Polar  Circle,  |  and  |  round  the  World  :  |  but  chiefly  into  the  | 
country  of  the  Hottentots  and  Caffres,  ]  from  the  year  1772  to  1776.  |  By 
Andrew  Sparrman,  M.D.,  |  Professor  of  Physic  at  Stockholm,  Fellow  of  the 
Royal   Academy  of  Sciences  |  in  Sweden,  and  Inspector  of  its  Cabinet  of 
Natm-al   History.  |  Translated   from  the  Swedish  Original.  |  With  Plates.  | 
In    two    Volumes.  |  Vol.    I.  |  The    second    edition,    corrected.  |  London :  | 
Printed  for  G.  G.  J.  and  J.  Rolnnson,  Pater-noster-Row.  |  MDCCLXXXVI. 

4to,  7|  X  5.  Vol.  i :  Pp.  xxviii,  368  ;  3  plates  and  map  of  Cape  of  Good 
Hope,  with  insets.     Vol.  ii :    pp.  viii,  356  ;    7  plates. 

On  Cook's  second  voyage,  joined  the  vessels  at  the  Cape,  at  Dr.  Forster's 
request  and  expense,  to  assist  in  the  naturalist's  work.  Meagre  account 
of  the  voyage,  in  20  pages.  Hairbreadth  escape  from  collision  of  the  two 
ships,  not  mentioned  elsewhere.  Shocking  details  as  to  the  food  on  board. 
Described  the  plants  which  George  Forster  drew.  Resumed  his  work  at  the 
Cape  on  return  of  the  expedition.  Book  interesting  and  amusing,  and 
descriptive  of  social  life  at  the  Cape.

1787. 

Catalogue  I  of  the  I  diiTerent  Specimens  of  Cloth  |  collected  in  the  three 
Voyages  of  |  Captain  Cook,  |  to  the  Southern  Hemisphere ;  |  with  a  par- 
ticular Account  I  of  the  |  Manner  of  the  Manufacturing  the  same  in  the 
various  islands  of  the  |  South  Seas ;  |  partly  extracted  from  |  Mr.  Ander- 
son and  Reinhold  Forster's  Observations,  |  and  the  verbal  Account  of  some 
of  the  most  knowing  of  the  Navigators :  |  with  |  some  Anecdotes  that  hap- 
pened to  them  among  |  the  Natives.  |  Now  properly  arraigned  and 
printed  |  For  Alexander  Shaw,  No.  379,  Strand,  London.  |  MDCCI.XXXVII. 

Very  rare.     No  evidence  of  authorship. 

Small  4to.  Pp.  8.  Then  follow  25  blank  sheets  of  paper,  between 
which  are  inserted  the  specimens  of  cloth  (39  in  number),  varying  in  size 
from  the  quarto  of  the  book  to  pieces  of  4  in.  or  5  in.  x  2  in.,  and  mostly 
with  some  pattern  and  dye,  and  similar  to  specimens  of  the  present  day. 

I  have  been  able  to  collate  six  or  eight  copies,  and  find  that  all  vary  in 
the  number  and  size  of  the  specimens — no  two  are  alike.  In  none  did  I  see 
No.  39,  "  a  fine  specimen  of  the  lace-bark,  from  Jamaica,  bought  at  the 
Duchess  of  Portland's  sale."  All  are  tapa  cloth,  chiefly  from  Otaheite, 
Owhyee,  and  Friendly  Isles,  None  from  New  Zealand,  though  Cook  ("First 
Voyage,"  Vol.  ii,  p.  367)  "  found  among  their  plantations  the  Morus  papy- 
rifera,  of  which  these  people,  as  well  as  those  of  Otaheite,  make  cloth."  But 
the  plant  seemed  rare,  and  the  cloth  but  worn  as  ornament.

The  logs  of  these  voyages  were  submitted  to  Mr.  Wales  for  revision 
and  calculation  of  their  data.  In  the  case  of  the  "  Endeavour  "  this  was 
specially  necessary,  owing  to  the  death  during  the  voyage  of  Mr.  Green,  the 
astronomer,  whose  papers  were  left  in  confusion,  and  imperfect.  The  work 
is  full  of  able  comment  and  criticism.

1788-1878]  New  Zealand  Literature.  27 


Kippis,  Andrew,  D.D. — continued. 

4to.  Pp.  xvi,  527.  Engraved  frontispiece  of  Capt.  Cook,  "  from  an 
original  picture  in  the  possession  of  Sir  Joseph  Banks,  Bart."  (Dance's  por- 
trait, engraved  by  Heath.) 

Admirable  precis  of  the  three  voyages,  with  valuable  information  from 
the  first  sources.  Introduces  most  of  Samwell's  "Narrative  of  the  Death," 
SiC,  q.v.  ;  also,  account  of  the  various  tributes  to  Cook's  memory  (Miss 
Helen  Maria  Williams's  "  The  Moral :  an  Ode,"  and  Miss  Seward's  "  Elegy,"" 
q.v.)  ;  biographies  of  Green  (the  astronomer),  Ledyard,  and  Capt.  Clerke,. 
and  notice  of  medals  struck.  Kippis,  a  dissenting  minister  and  litterateur,, 
editor  of  new  edition  of  "  Biographia  Britannica "  and  other  works,  was- 
born  at  Nottingham  in  1725  ;    died,  1795. 

A  reprint,  with  12  photographs  from  the  original  plates  of  the  voyage,, 
appeared  in  1878.     8vo  ;    London;   Bickers  and  Son. 

A  French  translation,  by  Jean  Castera  ;  2  vols.,  8vo  ;  Paris  ;  1788. 
The  same  in  4to  ;    1788.     The  same  in  4to  ;    1789. 

A  German  translation,  by  Albrecht  Wittenberg,  Hamburg.  2  vols. 
Svo  ;    1789  ;    with  portrait.     "  Centenaire,"  &c. 


1794. 

Portlock's  new  Collection  of  Voyages  and  Travels.     London.     Alex.  Hogg.     1794. 

FoHo.     Pp.  850.     Plates. 

This  popular  collection  contains  Cook's  voyages,  which  are  worth  re- 
vising with  other  accounts,  as  they  contain  some  additional  matter  derived 
from  the  officers.  Page  404  gives  a  fuller  list  of  the  officers  and  seamen 
of  the  third  voyage  than  is  found  in  the  authorised  account.  On  page  587 
the  date  of  Cook's  birth  is  given  as  3rd  February,  1*728,  on  the  authority 
of  the  Rev.  Mr.  Grenside,  "  whose  certificate,  taken  from  the  register  of  births 
in  his  parish,  is  now  in  the  possession  of  our  publisher."  Kippis  gives 
27th  October,  and  so  does  Besant ;  Wharton  says  27th  Februarj'. 


1796. 

Labillardiere.  In  the  "  Annale.-;  du  Museum  d'Histoire  Naturelle  "  for  1796, 
Paris,  is  "  Extrait  d'un  Memoire,  lu  a  la  classe  des  sciences  de  I'lnstitut 
national,  sur  la  force  du  lin  de  la  Nouvelle-Zelande,  comparee  a  celle  des 
filamens  de  1' aloes- pitte,  du  chanvre,  du  lin  et  de  la  soie,"  by  Labillardiere. 
Pages  474  to  484.  ....  J     : 

Labillardiere  brought  with  him  from  New  Zealand  plants  of  the  Phormium 
procured  in  exchange  with  the  natives.  His  is  the  first  scientific  account  of 
New  Zealand  flax  fibre.  He  describes  experiments  as  to  its  strength  and  its 
uses  amongst  the  natives.  In  the  same  volume  (p.  228  et  seq.)  is  a  "  Memoire 
sur  la  culture  du  lin  de  la  Nouvelle-Zelande,"  by  Thouin.

Governor    King.  I  By    David   Collins,    Esquire,  |  late  Judge   Advocate   and 


28  Bibliography  of  [1798 


•Collins,  David — continued. 

Secretary  of  the  Colony.  |  Illustrated  by  Engravings.  |  "  Many  might  be 
saved  who  "  .  .  .  Anon.  |  — London  :  |  Printed  for  T.  Cadell  Jun.  and 
W.  Da  vies,  in  the  Strand.  |  1798. 

4to.  Dedication  (to  Viscount  Sydney),  preface,  and  contents,  pp.  xx ; 
introduction,  xxxviii ;  617  ;  and  list  of  engravings,  1.  2  charts  and  22  plates. 
Forms  Vol.  i  of  the  work  completed  by  Vol.  ii  in  1802. 

Gives  the  most  complete  account  of  the  earliest  days  of  New  South 
Wales,  and  frequent  reference  to  whale-fishing  off  the  New  Zealand  coast, 
which  began  in  1794  ;  visits  to  Dusky  Bay  and  the  River  Thames,  for  spars  ; 
and  an  account  of  Hoodoo  and  Toogee  {Huru  and  Tuki),  two  New  Zealand 
natives  abducted  in  April,  1793,  from  Doubtless  Bay  by  Lieut.  Hanson,  in 
the  "  Daedalus,"  to  teach  the  art  of  dressing  flax  at  Norfolk  Island.  Fac- 
simile is  given  of  a  curious  chart  of  New  Zealand  drawn  on  a  floor  with  chalk 
by  Tuki,  accompanied  with  descriptive  matter,  which  greatly  resembles  the 
"  Ysles  de  Magna"  in  Jean  Rotz's  map  of  1542,  and  is  supposed  by  Major  to 
be  New  Zealand.     Vocabulary  of  over  400  New  Zealand  words. 

Vol.  ii,  1802.  Title-page  same,  with  additions,  "  From  its  first  Settle- 
ment, in  January,  1788,  to  August,  1801  "  ;  and  "  An  Account  of  a  Voyage 
performed  by  Captain  Flinders  and  Mr.  Bass,  by  which  the  existence  of  a 
;Strait  separating  Van  Dieman's  Land  from  the  Continent  of  New  Holland 
was  ascertained."  Dedication  (to  Lord  Hobart),  advertisement,  and  con- 
tents, pp.  xvi ;  336.  Chart  and  9  plates,  5  of  them  of  animals,  and  in  some 
copies  coloured.  Written  by  Collins  (now  described  on  title-page  as  "  Lieu- 
tenant-Colonel of  the  Royal  Marines  ")  after  his  return  to  England,  from 
information  transmitted  to  him  by  Governor  Hunter.  Mere  verbal  mention 
•of  New  Zealand.  The  voyage  of  Flinders  and  Bass  fortunately  inserted,  as 
the  original  was  afterwards  lost.     Reviewed  in  Edinburgh  Review,  April,  1803. 

2nd  ed.,  1804.  4to.  1  vol.  Same  title-page  as  in  Vol.  ii  of  1st  ed. 
Pp.  xvii  {n.p.,  2),  562.  Portrait  of  author  in  frontispiece,  2  charts  and 
31  plates.  A  much-abridged  edition.  Whilst  in  preparation  Collins  received 
the  appointment  of  Lieut. -Governor  of  Port  Phillip,  a  title  which  appears 
on  the  title-page.  Mrs.  Maria  Collins  thereupon  edited  it,  leaving  out  what 
"  was  now  no  longer  of  importance."  A  few  notes  and  official  returns  bring- 
ing up  information  to  1st  May,  1803. 

Collins  was  born  at  Exeter  in  1754,  fought  in  the  .American  revolutionary 
war,  and  died  at  Hobart  Town  in  1810.

chart ;    London  ;    B.  Uphill,  No.  30,  Brydges-street,  Covent  fJarden  ;    1800. 

Second  edition,  1802.  Vol.  i:  pp.  xii,  2,  464.  Vol.  ii  :  Pp.  viii,  423, 
43,  2. 

This  expedition,  under  the  command  of  Bruny  Dentrecasteaux,  of  the 
"  Recherche,"  and  Huon  Kermadec,  of  the  "  Esperance,"  failed  to  find 
La  Peronse,  whose  fate  was  discovered  thirty- five  years  later  by  Dillon  {q.v.). 
In  March,  1793,  a  few  days  were  spent  ofif  the  north  coast  of  New  Zealand 
in  intercourse  with  the  natives.  Labillardiere  brought  back  plants  of  the 
Phormium.  which  he  saccessfuUy  grew  and  described.  The  names  of  the 
Huon  pine.  Recherche  and  Kermadec  Islands,  &c.,  are  derived  from  thi? 
voyage. 


Proceedings  ]  of  the  |  Society  for  Missions  to  Africa  and  the  East,  1  insti- 
tuted by  1  Members  of  the  Established  Church  .  .  .  ]  London :  Printed 
by  C.  Whittingham,  Goswell  St.  1  Sold  by  L.  B.  Seeley,  169,  Fleet  St. ;  and 
J.  Hatchard,  |  Piccadilly :  Booksellers  to  the  Society :  |  and  by  W.  Johnston, 
Crow  St.,  Dublin. 

8vo. 

The  first  of  the  Society's  annual  volumes  ;  its  title  was  afterward? 
changed  to  "  Proceedings  of  the  Church  Missionary  Society,"  &c. 

Contains  considerable  reference  to  natives  and  early  New  Zealand ; 
foundation  of  mission  (8th  report) ;  Mr.  Marsden's  second  visit  (App.  xiv, 
1820-21) ;    and  journals  and  letters  from  missionaries. 

The  Society  finally  relinquished  its  mission  in  1004  to  the  care  of  the 
New  Zealand  Church  Board  of  Missions.  Other  publications  issued  by  it 
were  a  small  four-page  Quarterly  Paper,  begun  in  1816  ;  Monthly  Paper, 
enlarged  in  1830  to  Church  Missionary  Eecord  ;  Missionary  Gleaner,  1838  ; 
and  Church  Missionary  Juvenile  Instructor,  1839.  These  are  chiefly  selections 
from  the  Proceedings,  Register,  and  Church  Missionary  Intelligencer,  the 
three  important  publications  of  the  Society  {q.v.).

Vancouver,  Capt.  George — continued.  :  /.-l  -  .-ij:iA',iJ 

The  Original  edition  (1798)  was  x>"blisbed  in  3  vols.  4tOi  with  a  folio  vol. 
of  16  plates  (10  charts  and  6  views  of  headlands).  Thrmighont  the  'I  vols, 
are  interspersed  17  views,  which  in  the  "New  Edition"  are- of  the' same 
size,  but  folded.  The  advertisement  to  the  "  New  Edition  "  states^  that  the 
plates  of  the  10  charts  liad  been  stolen;  hence  2  verere-engrayed  (half - 
^'4i7.e)  for  insertion,  one  of  which  contains  the  Snares,  Chatham  Island,  and 
''''■'parti  of  the  south-west  coast  of  New  Holland.  The  original- has  considerable 
■,  •  additions  to  Dusky  Bay,  as  explored  by.  Cook.  Owing  to  Vancouver's  pre- 
mature death  (May,  1708),  the  work,  though  nearly  completed,  was. edited 
by  his.  brother  John,  Vancouver  discovered  King  George's  Sound,  and,  on 
the  24tb  November,  1791,  the  Snares,  Lieut<Wm.  Robert  Broughton,  in 
the  tender"  Chatham,"  discovered,  and  nam,ed  after  the  Earl,  Chatham 
Island,  on, November  29,  o_f  which  and  the  iiatives  (2,000  Morioris)  he,  gives 
an  interesting  account.  The  expedition  was  absent  five  years.  Dr.  Archi- 
bald Menzies,  surgeon  of  the  "  Discovery,"  contributed  much  to  the  New 
Zealand  Cryptogamia  (reproduced  in  Hooker's  "  Musci  Exotici  "  and  "  Icones 
Filicum").  The  "Daedalus,"  store-ship,  Lieut.  Hergest,  who  afterwards 
kidnapped  two  New  Zealand  natives  to  teach  flax-dressing  in  Norfolk 
Island,  was  also  in  the  expedition.  Vancouver  sailed  with  Cook  on  his 
second  voyage,  as  a  midshipman  in  the  "  Resolution." 

A  French  translation  was  issued  in  3  vols.  4to,  with  18  views  interspersed; 
and  an  atlas  of  16  charts,  at  Paris,  in  1798  (de  I'imprimerie  de  la  Republique, 
an.  VIII).

34  Bibliography  of  [1803 


Burney,   Cayt.  James — continued. 

Strait  of  Anian  by  Lorenzo  Ferrer  Maldonado  ;  3,  Condite  Head  Rock  ; 
4,  The  Cumbrian's  Reef  ;  5,  Caledonian  colony  in  Darien  ;  6,  On  passage 
to  South  Sea  by  the  south  of  America ;  Separation  necessary  between 
early  and  modern  discoveries. 

This  important  and  comprehensive  work  brings  the  history  of  Pacific 
discovery  down  to  1764,  when  Hawkesworth  continues  it.  It  must  always 
form  the  basis  of  historical  research  for  early  voyages  and  discoveries 
throughout  the  Pacific.  Vol.  iii  contains  the  translation,  by  Rev.  C.  G. 
Woide,  of  Tasman's  Journal,  with  woodcut  and  charts  of  New  Zealand.  The 
most  complete  until  that  of  Heeres,  q.v. 

Rear- Admiral  Burney  was  born  in  1751  and  died  in  1821.  Son  of  the 
musician,  and  lieutenant  with  Cook  on  his  last  two  voyages.  Many  references 
to  him  in  the  diary  and  letters  of  Mme.  D'Arblay,  who  was  his  sister. 


1807.

8vo.  Pp.  {n.p.,  4)  iv,  486.  Folded  chart,  showing  voyages  of  Rochon, 
Surville,  and  Marion  ;   and  nine  lunar  tables. 

In  1791  appeared  "  Voyage  a  Madagascar  et  aux  Indes  Orientales," 
1  vol.  8vo,  containing  an  account  of  Madagascar  and  of  Cochin  China  only. 
In  1803  appeared  "  Voyage  a  Madagascar,  a^Maroc,  et  aux  Indes  Orientales," 
3  vols.  8vo,  forming  the  complete  edition  "of  the  Abbe  Rochon's  voyages. 
This  was  followed  by  the  above  "  Nouvelle  Edition  "  of  1807,  which  includes 
an  account  of  the  expeditions  of  Marion  du  Fresne  and  of  de  Surville,  with 
the  circumstances  leading  to  the  enterprise,  and  which  is  very  similar  to 
that  given  in  the  "  Nouveau  Voyage  a  la  Mer  du  Sud,"  q.v. 

Alexis  Marie  Rochon,  astronomer  and  traveller,  was  born  at  Brest  in 
1741,  died  1837.

Savage,  John — continued. 

Gives  the  first  description  of  New  Zealand  after  Cook;  description  is  a^ 
good  one.  Savage  came  to  New  South  Walfcs  in  1803,  and  there  introdueed- 
vaccination ;  was  surgeon  of  a  convict-ship  which  visited  New  Zealand  for 
spars  in  Sei)rember  and  October  of  1805 ;  returned  to  England  in  1806,.  and 
then  entered  the  Honourable  East  India  Company's  service.  Moyhanger 
accompanied  him  to  England — being  the  first  New- Zealander  to  visit  those 
shores — and  after  a  few  weeks'  stay  returned  with  Capt.  Skelton,  of  the  whaler 
"Ferret";  he  is  afterwards  referred  to  in  Rev.  S.  Marsden'.s  journal,  and 
in  Nicholas.

12mo.     Pp.  54.     Bound  in  strong  brown  paper. 

The  first  book  printed  in  Sj'^dney  specially  connected  with  New  Zealand. 
Thomas  Kendall,  the  missionary,  wrote  it  as  a  primer  and  vocabulary. 
Pp.  1-3,  alphabets — Roman,  Enghsl ,  and  italics  {Na  letteree) — and  numerals 
to  20  {Na  figuree) ;  pp.  4-7,  syllables  of  two  and  three  letters;  pp.  7-21, 
vocabulary  and  short  sentences  ;  pp.  22-30,  longer  sentences  (Maori  and 
English);  pp.  40-51,  vocabulary  (Maori  and  English);  pp.  52-54,  speci- 
mens of  parts  of  speech  (Maori  and  English), 

The  sentences  in  Nicholas's  "  Voyage  "  are  the  same  (p.  342  et  seq.), 
but  his  list  of  words  or  vocabulary  is  much  more  numerous.  The  only  speci- 
men I  know — probablj^  the  only  one  now  extant — is  in  the  Auckland  Museum. 
It  is  referred  to  in  the  Missionary  Register  for  1817,  at  p.  515.

Mr.  Marsden. 

Nicholas  was  a  New  South  Wales  landholder,  but  finally  returned  to 
England,  and  gave  evidence  on  the  state  of  New  Zealand  before  the  Lords' 
Committee  in  1838.

Chapter  xvii  gives  an  account  of  the  New-Zealanders,  Tuki  and  Huru^ 
who  were  kidnapped  in  1794  to  teach  the  working  of  flax.  The  work  con- 
tains very  extensive  extracts  from  the  ^Sydney  Oazette  newspaper,  from 
March,  1806,  to  December,  1812,  in  which  are  references  to  New  Zealand, 
chiefs,  flax,  and  whaling-vessels.

Woodd,  Rev.  B.  Memoir  and  Obituary  ]  of  Mowhee,  a  young  New  Zea-
