Mowhee  came  to  England  as  a  sailor  in  1816,  and  remained  under  Mr. 
Woodd's  care  until  his  death  from  a  "  putrid  fever."  A  protegee  of  Mr. 
Marsden,  and  one  of  the  firstfruits  of  missionary  labour.

Lee,  Rev.  Samuel,  and  Kendall,  Thomas.     A  |  Grammar  |  and  |  Vocabulary  | 
of  the  I  Language  of  New  Zealand.  | |  Published  by  the  |  Church  Mis- 
sionary Society.  | 1  London :  |  Printed  by  R.  Watts,  |  and  sold  by  L.  B. 

Seeley,  Fleet  Street ;  and  |  John  Hatchard  and  Son,  Piccadilly.  | |  1820. 


1820]  New  Zealand  Literature.  .  39 


Xjee,  Rev.  Samuel,  and  Kendall,  Thomas — continued. 

8vo.     Pp.  230.     Preface,  &e.  {n.p.),  6  pages. 

Piepared  at  the  instance  of  the  Church  Missionary  Society  by  the  Rev. 
S.  Lee,  Professor  of  Oriental  Languages  at  Cambridge,  conjointly  with 
Thomas  Kendall,  a  New  Zealand  lay  missionary  who  visited  England  in 
1820  with  the  chiefs  Hongi  and  Waikato,  and  who  for  five  years  had  been 
■collecting  material  for  the  purpose.  A  vocabulary  is  included,  collected 
two  years  previously  from  Tool  and  Teeterree  (Tui  and  Titore),  who  were 
also  native  visitors  at  the  Church  Society's  House  in  London. 

The  Maori  language  is  here  reduced  for  the  first  time  to  fixed  principles, 
and  the  open  sound  of  the  vowels  used.  Contains »songs,  dialogues,  Lord's 
Prayer,  and  Creed.  Many  numbers  were  printed  on  strong,  coarse  paper  for 
native  use. 

Five  years  later  Mr.  Kendall  proposed  the  issue  of  another  edition,  but 
beyond  a  few  sheets  nothing  appeared.  He  was  dismissed  from  the  New 
Zealand  mission  in  1823;  afterwards  traded  in  timber  and  other  articles,  rnd 
was  drowned  with  his  crew  off  Port  Jackson  Heads  in  1832. 


1822. 

Instructions  of  the  Committee  of  the  Church  Missionary  Society,  delivered 
August  6,  1822,  to  the  Rev.  Henry  Williams,  proceeding  as  a  missionary 
to  New  Zealand  ;  with  ]Mr.  Williams's  reply,  and  an  address  by  the  Rev. 
Edward  Garrard  Marsh,  M.A.,  Mnister  of  Hampstead  Chapel,  and  Late 
Fellow  of  Oriel  College.  London :  Printed  for  the  Church  Missionary 
Society,  by  Richard  Watts.     1822. 

8vo.     Pp.  27. 

Interesting  history  connected  with  the  early  New  Zealand  mission. 
Mr.  (Archdeacon)  Williams  was  the  second  ordained  New  Zealand 
missionary,  the  Rev.  J.  G.  Butler  being  the  first.  He  arrived  at  the  Bay 
of  Islands  under  the  escort  of  Mr.  Marsden,  3rd  August,  1823.  Died  at 
Pakaraka,  16th  July,  1867,  set.  75. 


1823. 

Cruise,  Capt   R.   A.      Journal  |   of  a    |   Ten  Months'  Residence  |   in    |   New 
Zealand.  |  By  Richard  A.  Cruise,   Esq.  |  Captain  in  the  84th  Regt.   Foot. 

I I  London :  |  printed  for  Longman,  Hurst,  Rees,  Orme,  and  Brown,  I 

Paternoster-Row.  |  1823. 

8vo.     Pp.  iv,  321.     Frontispiece  of  Tetoro  (Titore),  chief  of  New  Zealand. 

The  2nd  ed.  appeared  in  1824.  Same  title-page,  substituting  "  Major  " 
for  "  Captain."  Pp.  vi,  327.  Contains  additional  14  pages  of  quite 
unimportant  matter,  and  a  weather-table  is  substituted  for  the  remarks 
upon  the  weather  which  in  the  first  edition  preface  the  journal  of  each  day. 

Reviewed  in  the  Quarterly  Review,  Ixi ;    also  in  the  Monthly  Review,  cv. 

Captain  Cniise  was  in  military  charge  of  H.M.S.  "  Dromedary  "  whilst 
procuring  kauri  spars  in  1820.  He  visited  the  Bay  of  Islands,  Whangaroa, 
Hokianga,  the  Waitemata,  and  the  Thames,  and  gives  an  account  of  the 
country  and  the  customs  of  the  New-Zealanders  ;  also  of  the  massacre  of 
the  "  Boyd,"  an  account  of  which  is  extracted  from  the  Sydney  Gazette, 
1st  September,  1810. 

In  the  manuscript  journal  of  Edward  Markham,  who  visited  the  Bay 
of  Islands  and  Hokianga  in  1834,  it  is  suggested  that  Cruise's  Joinnal  was 
written  by  a  subaltern  on  board  the  "  Dromedary."  If  so,  the  writer  must 
have  been  Lieut.  McCrae,  who  was  a  very  competent  young  officer.

Stated  to  be  the  first  book  printed  in  Australia.  Well  printed,  on 
thick  paper,  in  large  type,  and  fully  treats  the  subject.  The  author 
considered  viticulture  specially  adapted  to  the  young  colony  of  New  South 
Wales,  and  devoted  much  attention  to  the  introduction  of  this  industry.

index  (3).  The  price  was  3s.  6d.,  and  to  facilitate  its  distribution  in  the 
interior  copies  were  forwarded,  at  this  price,  to  district  constables.  For 
tying  up  vines,  and  for  many  other  similar  uses,  New  Zealand  flax  is  re- 
-commended  as  the  best  article. 

Mr.  Busby  was  born  at  Edinburgh  in  1801,  and  died  at  London  in  1871. 
In  February,  1831,  he  left  Sydney  on  a  visit  to  the  Home-country,  travelling 
through  the  vineyards  of  Spain  and  France,  and  returned  to  Sydney  in  1832 
with  the  appointment  of  British  Resident  in  New  Zealand,  entering  upon  his 
new  duties  in  1833,  which  he  discharged  until  superseded  by  Governor  Hob- 
son  in  1840.     He  published  Journal  |  of  a  recent  j  Visit  to  the  Vineyards  I  of

A  complete  and  learned  work  on  languages.  The  New  Zealand  belongs

Blosseville,  Jules  de.     Memoire  geographique  sur  la  Nouvelle-Zelande,  &c. 

In  "  Nouvelles  Annales  des  Voyages,"  torn.  xxix. 

In  the  "  Pieces  justificatives  "  of  D'UrviUe's  "  Voyage  de  1' Astrolabe.'* 
Vol.  iii,  pp.  692-700,  is  that  part  which  refers  to  the  manners  and  customs 
of  the  natives  {q.v.). 

M.  Blosseville  was  an  officer  in  the  "  Coquille,"  and  visited  the  Bay 
of  Islands  and  its  neighbourhood  in  April,  1824.  During  his  stay  there,  and 
previously  at  Sydney,  he  was  assiduous  in  collecting  from  whaling  captains, 
natives,  and  other  sources  any  information  regarding  New  Zealand,  and 
these  are  published  in  the  above  "  Memoire."  One  of  his  chief  informants 
was  Captain  Edwardson,  of  the  "  Snapper,"  who  was  sealing  for  many 
months  in  1822-23  in  Foveaux  Strait.  Reference  to  this  is  also  in  the 
Mevtte  de  Deux  Monde  s,  September,  1861.  .    t 


42  Bibliography  of  [1826 

Duperrey,  Capt.  L.  I.  Voyage  I  aiitoiu-  du  Monde,  |  Execute  par  Ordre  du 
Roi,  I  sur  I  La  Corvette  de  sa  Majeste,  |  La  Coquille,  |  pendant  les  Annees 
I  1822,  1823,  1824  et  1825,  1  Sous  le  Ministere  de  S.E.M.  le  Marquis  de 
Clerraont-Tonnerre,  |  et  publiee  |  sous  les  Auspices  de  Son  Excellence  M. 
Le  C*^  De  Chabrol,  Ministre  de  la  Marine  et  des  Colonies ;  |  par  L.  I. 
Duperrey,  1  Chevalier  de  St,  Louis  et  de  la  Legion  d'Honneur,  Capitaine 
de  Fregate,  |  Commandant  de  1' Expedition.  |  Paris,  |  Arthus  Bertrand,. 
Libraire-Editeur,  |  Rue  Hautefeuille  No.  23.  1  1826. 

This  splendid  work,  published  by  the  French  Government,  is  composed 
of — 1.  "Histoire  du  Voyage"  (et  Anthropologie) ;  1  vol.  4to  ;  pp.  xlv,  202, 
and  folio  atlas  of  60  plates,  often  coloured.  2.  "  Zoologie,"  by  Lesson  and 
Garnot ;  4  vols,  of  text,  and  atlas  of  157  coloured  plates.  3.  "  Botanique," 
by  D'Urville,  de  St.  Vincent,  and  Brogniart ;  2  vols,  of  text,  and  atlas  of  100 
plates,  of  which  25  are  coloured.  4.  "  Hydrographie  et  Physique,"  by 
Duperrey ;  2  vols,  in  4to  and  folio  of  52  plates  (48  being  charts  and  4  of 
canoes).  5.  "  Meraoire  "  ;  8vo ;  pp.  104 ;  on  the  geographical  work  of 
the  "  Coquille's  "  cruise. 

Duperrey  visited  and  remained  at  the  Bay  of  Islands  for  a  fortnight 
in  April,  1824,  bringing  with  him  from  Sydney,  at  the  request  of  the  Rev. 
S.  Marsden,  Mr.  George  Clarke,  who  was  just  commencing  his  missionary 
labours,  and  who,  in  1840,  was  appointed  Protector  of  Aborigines  by 
Governor  Hobson.  As  the  history  of  the  voyage  was  never  completed,  it 
does  not  contain  the  details  of  this  visit ;  liut  M.  de  Blosseville,  one  of 
the  officers,  has  published  some  particulars  of  it,  q.v.  iinder  his  name. 
D'Urville,  then  also  an  officer  on  the  "  Coquille,"  gives  an  account  in  his 
"  Voyage  of  the  Astrolabe,"  Vol.  iii,  p.  673  {q.v.).  The  atlas  has  8  plates- 
devoted  to  New  Zealand — implements  and  ornaments  of  the  natives,  the 
waterfall  and  residence  of  the  missionaries  at  Kerikeri,  natives,  and  canoes.

vernor Macquarie's  pamphlet,  |  and  the  |  Third  Edition  of  i  Mr.  Wentworth's

The  apostle  of  New  Zealand  was  grievously  vilified  by  numerous  enemies, 
who  accused  him  of  cruel  sentences  to  prisoners,  employing  labour  on  Sundays^ 
selling  liquor,  and  other  charges,  and  here  he  triumphantly  defends  himself.

The  Trial  |  of  |  Edward  Gibbon  Wakefield,  !  William  Wakefield,  |  and  |

Reviewed  in  the  Edinburgh  Review^  No.  xciii, 

E.  G.  Wakefield  (1796-1862)  was  sentenced  to  Newgate,  and  his  brother 
to  Lancaster  Gaol,  for  three  years.  Here,  contemplating  the  crime  and 
misery  around  him,  and  their  causes,  he  initiated  that  statesmanlike  career 
which  resulted  in  the  improvement  of  penal  laws,  of  emigration,  colonial  land- 
purchase,  and  that  extensive  system  of  colonisation  known  by  his  name. 
His  first  publication  was  "  ietters  from  Sydney,"  1829  {q.v.). 

In  John  Bull  newspaper  (1826  ?)  is  an  account,  in  several  close  columns, 
of  this  remarkable  episode  and  trial,  signed  by  himself.  This  is  availed  of  in 
the  Liverpool  Courier  of  25th  March,  1899.  In  Blackwood,  May,  1827,  is  a 
lengthy  discussion  upon  the  Gretna  Green  marriage  aspect.  Another  account 
is  in  the  Macclesfield  Courier,  and  is  copied  into  the  Hawkers  Bay  Herald 
of  27th  December,  1862. 

Miss  Turner,  restored  to  her  father,  maiTied  Mr.  Legh,  and  their  son 
afterwards  became  Lord  Newton.

A  French  translation  was  issued  o*f  this  work :  Voyage  |  aux  lies  de  la 
Mer  du  Sud,  |  en  1827  et  1828,  |  et  |  Relation  de  la  Decouverte  |  du  Sort 
de  la  Perouse.  |  Dedie  au  Roi,  par  le  Capitaine  Peter  Dillon,  Chevalier,  etc.

Gives  short  account  of  La  Perouse's  ill-fated  expedition  and  the  un- 
successful search  for  him  by  D'Entrecasteaux  and  Kermadec  {vide  Labil- 
lardiere).  Abounds  in  references  to  New  Zealand — Marion's  massacre, 
Dr.  Savage,  Moyhanger,  Capt.  Herd,  the  massacre  of  the  "  Boyd  "  (of  which, 
full  particulars,  derived  from  the  natives,  are  given). 

Following  up  a  slight  clue,  Dillon  discovered  in  1827  that  La  Perouse 
met  his  fate  on  Vanikoro,  one  of  the  Santa  Cruz  Group.  For  this  service 
he  was  awarded  a  life  annuity  of  4,000  francs  and  the  distinction  of  the 
Legion  of  Honoiu*. 

Peter  Dillon  (1785-1847),  a  splendid  specimen  of  a  wild  Irishman  and 
seaman.  Visited  New  Zealand  six  or  seven  times,  and  brought  down  the 
brig  "  Active  "  from  Sydney,  with  the  first  missionaries  (Kendall  and  Hall) 
on  board,  in  1814.  Much  reference  to  him  in  Bayly's  "  Sea  Life  Sixty  Years 
Ago"  (<7.v.).

Ellis,  Bev.  W. — continued. 

8vo.  Vol.  i :  pp.  xvi,  636 ;  maps,  4  plates,  and  10  wood  engravings. 
Vol.  ii :    pp.  viii,  576  ;   4  plates  and  6  wood  engravings. 

Minute  and  valuable  contribution  to  knowledge  of  Polynesia  by  this 
eminent  missionary,  who  commenced  his  labours  in  1816.  Describes  his 
visit  to  the  early  New  Zealand  mission  at  the  Bay  of  Islands,  Hongi,  tatu, 
the  war-dance,  implements,  and  much  reference  to  the  Rev.  S.  Marsden. 
A  repository  of  Polynesian  information. 

2nd  ed.  in  1831.  4  vols.  sm.  8vo.  "  During  a  residence  of  nearly  eight 
years  "  on  title-page. 

3rd  ed.  in  1853.     4  vols.  sm.  8vo.     Published  by  Bohn,  and  indexed. 

New  ed.  in  1859,  enlarged  and  improved. 


Hints  I  on  |  Emigration  |  to  the  |  new  Settlement  |  on  the  |  Swan  and  Canning 
Rivers,  |    on  the  |  West  Coast  of  |  Australia.   |  3rd  Edition.  |  London :  | 
J.  Cross,  18,  Holborn,  |  opposite  Furnival's  Inn.  |  MDCCCXXIX. 

8vo.     Pp.  70.     Map  of  the  world,  and  plan  of  settler's  cottage. 

Relates  to  the  j&rst  attempt  at  special  colonisation  in  Australasia,  made 
chiefly  through  the  efforts  of  Mr.  Thomas  Peel  and  Sir  Francis  Vincent. 
It  ended  disastrously,  and  supplied  E.  G.  Wakefield,  when  giving  evidence 
before  the  House  of  Lords  in  1837,  with  an  excellent  illustration  of  colonising 
on  wrong  principles.  Peel's  life  was  endangered  by  the  angry,  disappointed 
labourers  whom  he  took  out.  13  pages  contain  interesting  report  on  botanical 
productions,  &c.,  of  Swan  River,  by  Mr.  J.  Fraser,  Colonial  Botanist  at 
Sydney. 


New  South  Wales  and  Van  Diemen's  Land  almanacs  and  calendars  from 
this  date  contain  occasional  reference  to  New  Zealand,  such  as  arrival  and 
departure  of  vessels. 

New  Zealand  in  1829.  From  the  journal  of  an  officer  of  the  brig  "  Hawes,"^ 
describing  the  capture  of  that  vessel  by  the  natives,  and  the  cruelties  exercised 
towards  her  crew  ;  with  some  account  of  the  country. 

Article  in  the  United  Service  Journal,  No.  18,  June,  1830.  Pp.  10. 
Woodcut  of  the  chief  Enararo,  .  r  the  Lizard. 

The  "  Hawes,"  a  sealer,  was  seized  at  Whakatane  in  March,  1829; 
several  of  the  crew  murdered  and  eaten,  and  the  writer  made  prisoner. 
Amongst  much  other  information  is  described  the  mode  of  preserving  human 
heads. 


[Wakefield,   E.  G.]       A  |  Letter  from  Sydney,  |  the  I  principal  Town  |  of  ( 

Australasia.  |  Edited    by  |  Robert     Gouger.  | |  Together     with     the  | 

OutUne  of  a  System  of  Colonization.  |  London :  |  Joseph  Cross,  18,  Holborn ; 
I  Simpkin  and  Marshall,  Stationers'  Court,  and  |  Effingham  Wilson,  Royal 
Exchange.  |  MDCCCXXIX. 

8vo.     Pp.  X,  222,  xxiv.     Map  of  the  world  on  Mercator's  projection. 

Written  by  Edward  Gibbon  Wakefield  whilst  an  inmate  of  Newgate 
Gaol,  being  the  first  contribution  to  his  great  scheme  of  colonisation.  It 
first  appeared  in  the  form  of  letters  to  the  Morning  Chronicle ;  these  were 
collected  and  published  under  the  nam  of  "  Robert  Gouger,"  Wakefield's  in- 
timate friend,  and  afterwards  the  first  Colonial  Secretary  of  South  Australia, 
Written  in  fluent  and  interesting  style,  it  attracted  much  attention.  Word- 
pictures  of  freedom,  bright  skies,  currency  lasses,  emigration,  convicts, 
wool-growing,  &c.

A  reprint  appeared  with  title — The  |  New  Zealanders,  |  containing  |  a

Considerable  extracts  are  translated  into  French  in  D'Urville's  "  Voyage 
<le  r  Astrolabe  "  :    "Pieces  justificatives,"  pp.  711-92. 

A  German  translation — Die  Neuseelander,  nach  dem  Englischen.  Mit 
1  Karte  und  44  Abbildungen.     Klein  8vo.     Leipzig  ;    1833. 

Published  under  the  auspices  of  the  Society  for  the  Diffusion  of  Useful 
Knowledge.  Long  attributed  to  Lord  Brougham,  probably  for  no  stronger 
reason  than  that  he  was  president  of  the  society.  Now  known  to  be  the 
work  of  George  Lillie  Craik,  LL.D.  (1788-1866),  author,  and  Professor  of 
Enghsh  Literature  at  Belfast,  who  here  presents  a  most  valuable  compila- 
tion of  all  that  was  known  of  old  New  Zealand  and  its  inhabitants,  drawn 
chiefly  from  Cook  to  Cruise  and  the  Proceedings  of  the  Church  Missionary 
Society. 

John  Rutherford's  journal  of  his  ten  years'  exile  in  New  Zealand  is 
given  at  great  length.  In  Vol.  xxii,  1890,  Trans.  N.Z.  Inst.,  is  a  paper  by 
Bishop  W.  L.  Wilhams  casting  doubt  on  Rutherford's  narrative. 

The  i  Journal  ]  of    the  |  Royal    Geographical    Society.  | |  London:  |  John 

Murray,  Albemarle  Street. 

8vo. 

The  Journal  was  first  publishetl  in  this  year,  and  continued  until  1880; 
.50  vols.,  with  general  indexes  in  5  vols.,  and  a  volume  of  supplementary 
;  papers  (1882-85) — in  all  56  vols.  8vo.  In  1880  the  publication  ceased  in 
this  form,  but  was  continued  in  roy.  8vo  as  "  Proceedings  of  the  Royal 
Geographical  Society  and  Monthly  Record  of  Geography,"  Vols,  i-xiv, 
1879-92.  It  was  then  continued  to  the  present  date  as  the  "Geographical 
Journal,''  including  the  "  Proceedings  of  the  Royal  Geographical  Society," 
commencing  18th  January,  1893  (roy.  8vo),  and  pubUshed  monthly. 

The  Journal  contains  many  important  contributions  to  explorations  in 
New  Zealand,  Australasia,  and  the  Pacific  generally  —  Brunner,  Haast, 
Hector,  McKerrow,  and  others.  Many  early  and  important  explorations 
will  also  be  found  in  the  New  Zealand  Gazette  and  Wellington  Spectator, 
Nelson  Examiner,  &c.,  of  early  date.

A  '  Statement  |  of    the  |  Principles    and    Objects  |  of    a    proposed  |  National 
Society,  |  for    the  |  cure    and    prevention    of    pauperism,  |  by    means    of  |

An  early  contribution  to  the  new  art  of  colonisation.  Robert  Gouger's 
name  appears  as  secretary  of  the  committee.  Probably  E.  G.  Wakefield 
was  the  mover. 


Voyage  |  de  |  la  Corvette  |  L' Astrolabe  f  execute  par  Ordre  du  Roi,  |  pendant  les 
Annees  1826-1827-1828-1829,  1  sous  le  Commandement  1  de  M.  J.  Dumont 
D'Urville,  ]  Capitaine  de  Vaisseau.  |  Public  |  par  Ordonnance  de  sa  Majeste. 
I  Paris  I  J.  Tastu,  Editeur-Imprimeur,  |  No.  36,  Rue  de  Vaugirard.  |  1830. 

The  publication,  by  the  French  Government,  of  this  magnificent  work 
extended  over  a  period  of  six  years— from  1830  to  1835.  In  collating  and 
comparing  copies  it  must  be  remembered  that  the  original  issue  appeared 
in  parts,  in  paper  covers  and  boards,  which  were  afterwards  variously  bound. 
The  work  is  composed  of  five  divisions. 


PnEMifeRK  Division. 

"  Histoire  du  Voyage."  Edited  by  M.  D'Urville.  5  vols.  8vo,  with 
more  than  100  wood  or  line  engravings  ;  and  2*folio  atlases,  containing  a 
portrait  of  M.  D'Urville  and  243  lithograph  plates,  inclusive  of  8  charts. 
The  plates  of  the  first  atlas  are  numbered  1-115  ;   of  the  second,  116-243. 

Vol.  i :  Pp.  cxvi,  528  ;  and  table  of  contents,  1  page.  1830.  At  the 
end  are  8  engravings,  illustrative  of  the  customs  of  the  Austrahan  aborigines, 
reduced  from  CoUins's  "New  South  Wales,"  1798.  Chapters  1  to  11 — from 
Toulon  to  New  South  Wales. 

Vol.  ii :  Pp.  630,  and  table,  2  pages.  1830.  Chapters  12  to  20— from 
Port  Jackson  to  New  Zealand.     Also  "  Notice  sur  les  lies  du  Grand- Ocean." 

Vol.  iii :  Pp.  796.  "  Pieces  justificatives  siu-  la  Nouvelle-Zelande." 
{Corroborative  documents  or  proofs.)  This  volume  contains  enormous 
extracts  from,  and  often  entire  translations  of,  all  the  pubUcations  relative 
to  New  Zealand  from  Tasman's  "  Voyage,"  in  1642,  to  the  "  New  Zealanders,'* 
in  1830 — Cook,  Surville,  Marion,  Tumbull,  Missionary  Register,  Cruise, 
Marsden,  Nicholas,  and  others. 

Vol.  iv:  Pp.  760.  Chapters  21  to  30— from  New  Zealand  to  Tonga- 
Tabou,  Viti,  New  Britain,  New  Guinea,  Amboyna,  and  Van  Diemen's  Land. 
1832.  An  appendix  contains  that  part  of  Schouten  and  Le  Maire's  voyage 
relating  to  the  coast  of  New  Guinea.  It  was  written  in  Latin  by  Nicolas  de 
Wassenaer,  surgeon  of  the  expedition,  and  pubUshed  at  Amsterdam  in  1619. 

Vol.  V  :  Pp.  716.  Table  of  contents  and  list  of  woodcuts  throughout  the 
volumes.  1883.  Chapters  31  to  40 — from  Van  Diemen's  Land  to  Vanikoro, 
Guam,  Amboyna,  Batavia,  Isle  of  France,  Cape  of  Good  Hope,  and  France. 

Vols,  ii  and  iii  relate  wholly  to  New  Zealand,  and  give  a  minute  and  ex- 
haustive account  of  the  country,  the  natives  and  their  habits,  explorations, 
whalers,  missionaries,  &c.  Stores  of  information,  diffused  through  various 
works,  many  of  them  almost  imknown  or  forgotten,  are  here  brought  together. 
The  journals  of  the  Rev.  Samuel  Marsden  and  of  the  earliest  missionaries 
are  extracted  from  the  Missionary  Register  ;  the  voyages  of  Tasman,  Cook, 
Surville,  Marion,  Tumbull,  and  others  have  full  reference ;  also  the  valuable 
observations  of  M.  de  Blosseville  in  1824,  and  many  letters.  26  woodcuts 
relate  to  New  Zealand.  The  atlases  contain  30  plates  relating  to  New  Zea- 
land, with  many  figures,  and  also  2  charts,  of  which  one  is  of  the  North 
Island,  as  examined  by  M.  D'Urville,  and  the  other  of  the  northernmost 
part  of  the  island.  The  whole  is  enriched  by  extracts  from  the  journals  of 
the  officers  of  the  expedition.  Three  months — from  December,  1826,  to 
March,  1827— were  spent  in  New  Zealand.

the  plants,  other  than  those  of  New  Zealand,  collected  on  the  voyage.  In-

the  sertum.

Vivid  description  of  a  war-dance  at  CM;aheite  in  April,  1826,  by  some

The  Sydney  Herald. 

First  number  published  18th  April,  1831.  Has  in  this  and  later  years 
frequent  references  to  New  Zealand — shipping  and  whaling  news ;  baked 
heads  ;  White  Island  ;  a  New  Zealand  grammar  and  vocabulary — by  Kendall,

Tyerman,  L.,  and  Bennet,  G.,  and   Montgomery,  Jas.      Journal  | 
of  I  Voyages    and    Travels  \  by    the  |  Rev.    Daniel    Tyerman   and    George 
Bennet,  Esq.   |  Deputed   from  the  i  London  Missionary  Society   |  to    visit 
their    stations   |  in    the    South    Sea    Islands,  China,  India,   &c.,  |  between

Montgomery.  | |    "  Glorify    ye "      .       .       .       — Isaiah.   |  London :   | 

Frederick  Westley  and  A.  H.  Davis,  |  Booksellers  to  the  London  Missionary 
Society,  |  Stationers'  HaU  Court  and  Ave  Maria  Lane.  |  MDCCCXXXI. 

8vo.  2  vols.  Vol.  i :  pp.  xxiv,  566.  Vol.  ii :  pp.  viii,  568.  2  portraits 
and  12  plates — one,  "  Wesleyan  IMission  Station,  as  seen  June,  1824,  in 
Wesley  Dale  [Whangaroa]  in  the  Harbour  of  N.Z." 

Interesting  account  by  two  competent  observers.  60  pages  devoted 
to  Sydney  and  to  New  Zealand.  At  Whangaroa  the  vessel  was  seized  and  all 
were  put  in  peril  of  their  lives,  but  escaped  through  the  intervention  of  the 
missionaries,  who  themselves  two  years  and  a  half  later  narrowly  escaped 
massacre.  Montgomery,  the  editor,  was  the  poet,  and  took  a  keen  interest 
in  the  mission.  The  London  JMissionary  Society  was  estabhshed  in  1795, 
and  in  1796  sent  its  first  missionaries  to  Tahiti  and  Tonga,  in  the  historic 
ship  "  Duff."

Wakefield,  E.  G.  Swing  unmasked  ;  or,  the  Causes  of  Rural  Incendiarism. 
By  Edward  Gibbon  Wakefield,  Esq.  London  :  Effingham  Wilson,  88,  Royal 
Exchange.     1831. 

Ppt.     Price  Is. 

"  A  true  picture  of  the  state  of  things  in  the  rural  districts."— /S/jectetor, 
18th  December. 

To  the  pages  of  the  Spectator  Mr.  Wakefield  was  a  constant  contributor 
on  matters  of  reform,  national  policy,  and  his  special  theories,  which  he  was 
then  developing.  In  this  pamphlet  draws  attention  to  the  social  dangers 
of  the  day.  In  the  slang  of  the  day,  "  Captain  Swing  "  was  the  perpetrator 
of  these  secret  incendiarisms. 

This  and  the  two  preceding  were  further  outcomes  of  Mr.  W^akefield's 
experience  and  reflections  during  his  imprisonment  in  Newgate.

8vo.  Pp.  X,  371.  7  engravings :  1.  Aranghie,  the  tattooer  of  New

in  183L 

Reviewed  in  Quarterly  Review,  Vol.  xlviii,  1832;  Westminster  Review, 
Vol.  xvii,  1832;  Monthly  Review,  No.  cxxviii,  1832;  Edinburgh  Review, 
Vol.  Ivi,  1833  ;   dkud  Protestant  Journal,  1833,  in  bitter  terms. 

Part  was  translated  into  German  in  Hoffmann's  "  Jahrbuch  der  Reisen, 
erster  jahrgang,"  Stuttgart,  1833,  p.  204,  under  the  title,  "  Begegnisse  u. 
Beobachtungen  eines  Englandischen  Malers  auf  Tristan  d' Acunha  und  Neu- 
Seeland." 

The  author,  always  a  wanderer,  sailed  from  Sydney  to  Hokianga  in  the 
"  Governor  Macquarie,"  Captain  Kent,  in  company  wath  some  members 
of  the  Wesleyan  Mission.  Gives  a  general  account  of  the  natives,  the  Church 
missionaries.  Captain  Herd's  settlement  at  Hokianga  (1825),  and,  from  an 
actor  in  the  scenes,  of  the  massacre  of  Marion's  crew  and  that  of  the  "  Boyd." 
H.M.S.  "  Warspite  "  was  the  first  man-of-war  to  sail  through  Cook  Strait. 
Earle's  sketches  formed  the  subject  of  Burford's  panorama  [q.v.),  exhibited 
in  Leicester  Square  in  1831.

Both  Drs.  Good  and  Gregory  were  friends  and  correspondents  of  the

Hay,  Robert  William.     Notices  of  New  Zealand. 

A  paper  of  4  pages  and  chart  of  New  Zealand  in  Vol.  ii  of  Journal  of 
Royal  Geographical  Society,  1832.  "  From  original  documents  in  the  Colonial 
Office,  communicated  by  R.  W.  Hay,  Esq.     Read  the  9th  of  April,  1832." 

Chiefly  topographical  information.  "  In  the  year  1818,  60  tons  of  New 
Zealand  flax  were  sent  from  Sydney  to  England,  of  the  value  of  £2,600." 

Mr.  Hay  was  Under-Secretary  of  State  for  the  Colonies  from  1825  ta 
1836,  and  Vice-President  of  the  Royal  Geographical  Society. 

This  volume  also  contains  two  important  articles — "  Brief  View  of  the 
Progress  of  Interior  Discovery  in  New  South  Wales,"  by  Allan  Cunning- 
ham, Esq.,  and  "  Recent  Information  from  Australia." 

Morrell,  Capt.  B.  A  Narrative  |  of  |  Four  Voyages,  |  to  the  1  South  Sea, 
North  and  South  Pacific  Ocean,  |  Chinese  Sea,  |  Ethiopic  and  Southern  |

sold  by  the  Booksellers  generally  throughout  the  |  United  States.    |  1832. 

8vo.     Pp.  xxvii,  492.     Engraved  portrait  of  the  author. 

Full  of  interesting  remarks  and  observations.  17  pages  relate  to  New 
Zealand,  from  the  Auckland  Isles  to  the  Bay  of  Islands,  up  the  east  coast, 
which  were  visited  in  December  and  January,  1829-30.  Gives  a  glowing 
description  of  "  Auckland's  Island,"  which  "  is  one  of  the  finest  places  for  a 
small  settlement  that  can  be  found  6n  any  island  in  the  Southern  Hemi- 
sphere above  lat.  35°."  In  1823  the  schooner  "  Henry  "  took  from  these 
islands  13,000  "  of  as  good  fur-seal  skins  as  were  ever  brought  to  the  New 
York  market."     "  Scarcely  one  to  be  found  now."     Same  at  the  Snares. 

Richard,  A.     Essai  d'une  Flore  de  La  Nouvelle-Z61ande,  &c. 

Vide  "  Voyage  of  the  Astrolabe,  1830,"  of  which  it  forms  part. 

[Wakefield,  E.  G.  (?)]  Plan  of  a  Company  1  to  be  established  |  for  the  Pur-

Probably  wTitten  by  E.  G.  Wakefield.  A  practical  exposition  of  the

Donlan,  M.  J.  J.     Phormium  tenax.   |  Or  |  Neptune  new  rigg'd.  |  Statement 
of    Facts   relative   to   Experiments  |  made   upon  |  Phormium    tenax,  |  or  | 
New  Zealand  Flax.  |  By  |  M.  J.  J.   Donlan ;    proving  that  he   can  render 
Great  Britain  totally  independent  |  of  Russia,  with  regard  to  her  Supplies^ 
of  Hemp  and  Flax,  |  for  Naval  and  other  Purposes,  by  the  substitution  of 
I  Colonial  Produce  instead  of  Baltic.  |  Also  |  an  Account  of  the   Impedi- 
ments which  this  |  New  and  Improved  Manufacture  ha*  met  with   from  | 
most  of  the  Public  Officers  of  this  Country.  |  Sed  magna  est,  &c.  |  London.  | 
1833.  I  W.  Glindon,  Printer,  51,  Rupert  street,  Haymarket. 

8vo.  Pp.  X,  188;  index  (p.  1).  Paper  wrapper,  with  title  as  above 
and  frigate  in  full  sail. 

Mr.  Donlan  was  an  extensive  rope  and  sail  maker,  and  had  devoted 
twenty  years  to  experiment  on  the  best  mode  of  preparing  flax  and  hemp. 
New  Zealand  flax  he  considers  by  far  the  best.  "  Mine  are  the  first  and  only 
sail-cloths  ever  made  and  brought  into  actual  service  from  Phormium  tenax'"' 
He  angrily  describes  his  ineffectual  efforts  to  induce  the  Admiralty  Board 
to  use  his  New  Zealand  manufactures  and  so  save  thousands  a  year. "  Exten- 
sive correspondence  and  experiments  detailed. 


Goderich,  Lcyrd,  and  Busby,  J.  Letter  |  of  the  |  Right  Honourable  i  Lord 
Viscount  Goderich,  |  and  |  Address  |  of  |  James  Busby,  Esq.,  British  Resi- 
dent, I  to  the  I  Chiefs  of  New  Zealand.  | |  Ko  te  Pukapuka  |  o  te  tino- 

Rangatira  |  o  Waikauta  Koreriha,  |  me  te  Korero  |  o  te  Puhipi,  |  ki  nga 
Rangatira  i  o  Nu  Tirani.  |  Sydney :  |  Printed  at  the  Gazette  Office,  by- 
Anne  Howe.     [1833.] 

8vo.     Pp.  10  (in  wrapper).     Double  columns — English  and  Maori. 

Printed  for  distribution  amongst  the  chiefs,  13  in  number,  who  had 
addressed  a  letter  to  King  William,  dated  Kerikeri,  3rd  October,  1831,  and 
dehvered  by  the  Rev.  Wm.  Yate,  a  returned  missionary,  asking  His  Majesty 
to  be  their  friend  and  guardian,  having  heard  that  the  tribe  of  Marion  (th^ 
French)  was  coming  to  take  their  land  (see  this  letter,'  p.  7,  parliamentary 
papers,  13th  April,  1840).  In  reply.  Lord  Goderich— Colonial  Office,  14th 
June,  1832 — assures  them  of  Great  Britain's  interest  and  protection,  and 
introduces  Mr.  James  Busby  as  the  British  Resident.  Both  letters  are  found 
in  parliamentary  papers,  13th  April,  1840,  p.  7. 

In  his  address  Mr.  Busby  introduces  himself,  explains  his  position,  and 
invokes  the  assistance  and  friendship  of  the  chiefs.  Reprinted  in  Marshall's 
"  New  Zealand,"  1837.

History  of  the  voyage,  4  vols.  8vo,  with  general  chart  of  voyage, 
and  folio  atlas  of  72  plates  engraved  under  the  direction  of  M.  de  Sainson  ; 
hydrography,  folio  atlas  with  1 1  plans  and  charts ;  zoology,  by  Eydoux, 
Laurent,  and  P.  Gervais,  with  1  vol.  of  60  coloured  plates ;  and  a  supplement 
of  Lepidoptera  by  Feisthamel,  32  pages  and  10  plates. 

400  pages  and  5  plates  are  devoted  to  the  stay  in  Van  Diemen's  Land, 
Sydney,  and  the  Bay  of  Islands,  which  was  reached  on  the  4th  October, 
1831.  The  Kawa-kawa,  many  inlets,  Korororeka,  and  adjacent  country 
were  surveyed  and  mapped  with  accuracy  and  artistic  taste  during  a  week's 
stay.  Interesting  account  of  the  natives  and  missionaries,  and  of  the  mas- 
sacre of  Marion  and  his  crew  in  1772.  An  outcome  of  the  visit  was  the  inter- 
change of  letters  between  13  New  Zealand  chiefs  and  Lord  Goderich  (q.v.). 
1833. 

Reference  to  "La  Favorite"  in  "Life  of  Henrv  Williams,"  Vol.  i, 
p.  92. 


The  I  New  South  Wales  |  Magazine.  |  August  to  December,  !  1833.  |  Vol.  I. 
I  Sydney:  1  Ralph  Mansfield  (Sole  Proprietor),  Pitt  Street;  |  J.  M.  Richard- 
son, London  ;    and  |  Bell  and  Bradfute,  Edinburgh. 

8vo.     Pp.  xi,  328.     Plans  and  illustrations. 

Discussion  on  Mr.  Busby's  appointment  as  British  Resident  at  New- 
Zealand,  and  criticism  on  his  work  on  New  Zealand,  tlax,  trade,  and  shipping. 
Mansfield  was  previously  a  Wesleyan  minister,  and  became  a  politician  and 
writer. 

The  magazine  terminated  with  its  March  number  of  1834. 


{"Wakefield,  E.  G.]  England  |  and  1  America.  |  A  Comparison  of  the  social 
and  political  1  State  of  both  Nations.  |  In  Tmq  Volumes.  |  London :  |  Richard 
Bentley,  New  Burlington  Street.  |  1833. 

8vo.     2  vols.     Vol.  i :    pp.  xii,  331.     Vol.  ii :   pp.  341. 

The  author  of  this  work  was  E.  G.  Wakefield,  who  writes  in  a  brilliant, 
vigorous  style  upon  questions  of  social  economy  then  agitating  all  classes — 
the  increase  of  wealth  and  misery,  uneasiness  of  the  middle  classes,  free 
trade,  superabundant  population,  and  slavery  in  America.  The  2nd  volume 
is  chiefly  devoted  to  a  consideration  of  the  relief  of  these  conditions  by  means 
of  his  "  Art  of  Colonization,"  which  he  now  develops  at  some  length,  sum- 
marising the  growing  movement  on  the  subject,  and  giving  a  list  of  im- 
portant pamphlets,  &c.  The  appendix  contains  correspondence  between  the 
Government  and  some  persons  desirous  of  founding  a  colony  in  South 
Australia. 


1834*]  New  Zealand  Literature.  55 

1834. 

Bennett,  Dr.  G.  Wanderings  |  in  New  South  Wales,  |  Batavia,  Peclir  Coasts 
Singapore,  |  and  China ;  |  being  |  the  Journal  of  a  Naturalist  |  in  those 
Countries,  during  1832,  1833,  and  1834.  |  By  |  George  Bennett  Esq.,  F.L.S. 
I  Fellow  of  the  Royal  College  of  Surgeons,  &c.  |  In  Two  Volumes.  |  London  : 
I  Richard  Bentley,  New  Burlington  Street,  |  Publisher  in  Ordinary  to  his 
Majesty.  |  1834.  | 

8vo.     2  vols.     4  illustrations.     Vol.  i :  pp.  xv,  440.     Vol.  ii :  pp.  vii,  428. 

Charming  and  valuable  observations  in  natural  history.  Account  of 
the  preparation  of  flax  by  the  New- Zeal  anders,  and  its  manufacture  in  Sydney 
and  elsewhere  :  "  New-Zealanders  are  now  employed  at  Sydney  as  labourers, 
and  are  much  esteemed  for  their  steady  and  sober  habits,"  &c. 

Dr.  Bennett  visited  the  Bay  of  Islands  in  1829,  where  he  was  the  first 
to  find  the  Lihocedrus  doniana  (kawaka).  He  contributed  extensively  to 
natural  science.     Born  at  Plymouth  in  1804,  and  died  at  Sydney  in  1892.

general  des  Voyages  de  Decouvertes  |  de  Magellan,  Tasman,  Dampier,  Anson,

4to.  2  vols.  Double  columns.  Pp.  viii,  576,  and  584.  152  copper

An  imaginary  voyage,  in  which  D'Urville,  as  a  modern  Anacharsis, 
skilfully  weaves  into  his  own  pleasant  narration  extracts  from  the  voyages 
of  other  circumnavigators,  the  whole  forming  an  admirable  visum i.  The 
story  of  New  Zealand  is  depicted  in  76  pages  and  30  illustrations,  and  includes 
the  outlying  Macquarie,  Campbell,  Auckland,  Antipodes,  Bounty,  Chatham, 
and  Kermadec  Islands.  Excellent  account  of  manners,  customs,  natural 
history,  missionaries,  &c.  Extract  from  the  Rev.  H.  Williams's  journal  of 
his  journey  to  Tauranga  in  1832.

The  author  was  the  celebrated  blind  traveller.  His  account  of  New- 
Zealand  is  derived  from  the  information  of  a  friend,  the  captain  of  a  trading- 
vessel  to  that  country  from  Sydney,  who  describes,  as  from  personal  Imow- 
ledge,  the  massacre  at  Akaroa  in  1830  by  Rauparaha,  assisted  by  Stewart^ 
of  the  trader  "  Elizabeth."

Contends  that  the  Polynesians  are  of  Asiatic  origin,  proceeded  east- 
ward from  the  Indian  Archipelago,  across  the  Pacific,  and  then  discovered 
and  peopled  the  continent  of  America  somewhere  near  Copiapo.  Was  the 
author  of  this  theory,  and  bitterly  complains  of  Messrs.  Ellis's  and  Williams's 
plagiarism  in  the  "  Missionary  Enterprises."  Much  reference  to  the  natives 
of  New  Zealand.

In  I851(?)  appeared  a  further  modified  (but  the  best)  edition — "  The 
British  Colonies ;  their  History,  Extent,  Condition,  and  Resources :  by 
R.  Montgomery  Martin,  Esq.,  late  Treasurer  to  the  Queen  at  Hong  Kong ;

published  by  The  London  Printing  and  Publishing  Company,  London  and 
New  York."  4  vols.  Imp.  8vo ;  double  columns.  Numerous  maps  and 
portraits.  Vol.  iii  is  devoted  to  New  Zealand,  Tasmania,  Auckland  Islands, 
^c,  and  is  most  comprehensive. 

Excepting  that  of  1834,  the  editions  are  most  puzzling  to  collate.  Some 
are  undated,  and  were  issued  in  parts  of  32  pages  each,  afterwards  being 
bound  into  volumes  of  varying  thickness.  The  labour  entailed  in  them 
is  as  enormous  as  it  is  valuable,  and  the  author  keenly  felt  the  little  recogni- 
tion and  assistance  vouchsafed  by  the  Government.  He  was  born  in  1803, 
And  died  in

4to.  2  vols.  Pp.  384  and  381,  with  plates  and  portraits  of  celebrated

This  work  was  continued  in  the  "  Annals  of  Natural  History,"  &c., 
1838,  q.v. 

Vol.  ii,  1836,  contains  a  portrait  and  a  "  brief  biographical  sketch  of  the 
late  Richard  Cunningham,  Colonial  Botanist  in  New  South  Wales,"  giving 
an  account  of  his  botanical  explorations  in  New  Zealand  in  1833-34.  On 
his  return  to  New  South  Wales  he  accompanied  Major  Mitchell's  expedition 
into  the  interior.  Wandering  from  his  party,  he  was  murdered  by  the  blacks 
in  April,  1835,  having  just  attained  his  42nd  year.  His  brother  Allan,  who 
succeeded  him  as  Colonial  Botanist,  arranged  his  New  Zealand  notes  and 
collections  (together  with  those  of  his  own)  in  an  extensive  series  of  article*. 
The  first  three  appear  in  this  volume,  and  are  entitled  "  Florae  Insularum 
Novae  Zelandiae  Precursor ;  or  a  Specimen  of  the  Botany  of  the  Islands  of  New 
Zealand.  By  Allan  Cunningham,  Esq."  {vide  Cunningham,  1836).  The  suc- 
ceeding articles  appear  in  the  "  Annals  of  Natural  History,"  as  above  stated. 
In  a  synoptical  form  they  present  the  results  of  all  previous  botanical  ex- 
plorations in  New  Zealand.

a  Botanical  Appendix  by  Dr.  Hooker,  of  Glasgow.  "  London :    Cochrane. 

8vo.     Pp.  132.

Yate,  Rev.  Wm.  Ah  Account  |  of  New  Zealand  ;  |  and  of  the  |  Formation 
and  Progress  |  of  the  |  Church  Missionary  Society's  Mission  |  in  the  Northern 
Island.  I  By  the  Rev.  William  Yate,  |  Missionary  of  the  Church  Missionary 
Society.  |  London :  |  Seeley  and  Burnside.  |  Sold  by  Hatchard  and  Son, 
Piccadilly  ;  and  L.  and  J.  Seeley,  |  169  Fleet  Street.  |  MDCCCXXXV, 

8vo.  Pp.  310;  contents  and  index  {n.y.),  16  pages.  Portrait  of  Rev. 
S.  Marsden,  map  of  New  Zealand,  and  9  plates,  chiefly  of  the  Waimate. 

2nd  ed.  appeared  in  same  year,  being  rather  a  reprint  on  reissue.  The 
title-page  alone  differs — "  R.  B.  Seeley  and  W.  Burnside." 

From  a  copy  in  my  possession,  with  critical  notes,  it  is  plain  that  the 
author's  brethren  considered  many  of  his  statements  regarding  the  customs, 
(fcc,  of  the  natives  and  other  matters  as  quite  unreliable.  The  shells  col- 
lected by  him — 29,  many  quite  new — are  described  by  Dr.  Gray,  of  the 
British  Museum,  in  an  appendix.  From  the  Sydney  press  in  1830-33  was 
issued,  under  his  superintendence,  two  of  the  earliest  publications  in  the 
native  language — selections  from  Scripture,  liturgy,  catechism,  hymns,  &c. 
(which  will  be  elsewhere  described) ;  granting  of  national  flag,  with  illustra- 
tion. Mr.  Yate  arrived  at  the  Bay  of  Islands  in  1828,  remaining  nearly  seven 
years ;  he  then  went  Home  to  see  this  work  through  the  press ;  on  his  re- 
turn to  Sydney  in  1836  he  was  dismissed  from  the  mission  for  misconduct. 
He  was  afterwards  appointed  chaplain  to  the  Sailors'  Home  at  Dover.  Nume- 
rous references  to  him  and  extracts  from  his  journals  are  in  the  Missionary 
Register  and  Church  Missionary  Society^ s  Proceedings. 


1836. 

B  [ennet]  ,  G[eorge](?).     Letters  |  to  the  |  Rev.  William  Yate  |  from  Natives 
of  .New  Zealand  |  converted  to   Christianity,   |  with  an  1  Introduction  and 
Notes  1  by  I  G.    B.  |  "  Say  among    the    heathen "      .      .      .    |  London :   | 
James  Nisbet  and  Co.,  Berner's  Street.  |  1836. 

12mo.     Pp.  96.     Price  Is.  4d. 

These  pious  letters  from  converted  natives  appear  also  in  Yate's 
"  Account  of  New  Zealand."  In  this  reprint  the  editor  appends  a  commen- 
tary to  many.  Mr.  Yate  induced  the  natives  to  write  to  him  their  religious 
ideas  and  feelings,  and  from  the  mass  of  crude  correspondence  these  30 
letters  were  selected. 

Mr.  George  Bennet,  a  warm  supporter  of  missions,  visited  New  Zealand 
in  1824  with  the  Rev.  Daniel  Tyerman,  as  a  deputation  from  the  London 
Missionary  Society  to  their  missions. 

Cunningham,   Allan,   F.L.S.     Florae  Insular um  Novae  Zealandise  Precursor ; 
or  a  Si)ecimen  of  the  Botany  of  the  Islands  of  New  Zealand. 

This  important  monograph  on  the  botany  of  New  Zealand  appeared 
as  a  series  of  papers  in  the  "  Companion  to  the  Botanical  Magazine,"  edited 
by  Sir  W.  J.  Hooker,  beginning  with  Vol.  ii,  1836,  pp.  223-33,  327-36, 
358-78 ;  then  continued  in  the  "  Annals  of  Natural  History ;  a  Magazine 
of  Zoology,  Botany,  and  Geology,"  conducted  by  Sir  W.  Jardine,  P.  J.  Selby, 
Dr.  Johnston,  Sir  W.  J.  Hooker,  and  R.  Taylor— in  Vol.  i,  1838,  pp.  210-16, 
376-81,  455-62  ;  Vol.  ii,  44-52,  125-32,  205-14,  356-60 ;  Vol.  iii,  29-34, 
111-15,  244-50,  314-19  ;  Vol.  iv,  22-26,  106-11,  256-62.  They  comprise  the 
plants  discovered  by  Banks,  Solander,  the  Forsters,  and  Sparrman  during 
Cook's  voyages,  Allan  Cunningham  in  1826,  the  French  naturalists  attached  to 
the  voyages  of  "  La  Coquille"  and  "  L' Astrolabe"  {q.v.),  and  by  Richard  Cun- 
ningham in  1833-34,  and  are  arranged  and  edited  by  the  author.  A  good 
description  is  given  of  639  New  Zealand  species,  interspersed  with  native 
names,  customs,  &c.,  and  two  plates  of  Loxsoma. 

For  biographies  of  the  two  brothers,  vide  Hooker,  1836,  and  Heward,  1842.

Marshall,  Dr.  W.  B.  A  personal  Narrative  |  of  |  two  Visits  to  New 
Zealand,  |  in  His  Majesty's  sliip  Alligator,  |  A.D.  1834.  |  By  |  William 
Barrett  Marshall,  |  Surgeon — Assistant  Surgeon,  R.N.  |  London :  |  James 
Nisbet  and  Co.,  Berners  Street.  |  MDCCCXXXVI. 

8vo.  Pp.  xvi,  352,  and  page  of  errata.  Plate  (native  village  and  chapel). 
Dedicated  to  Lord  Glenelg,  Secretary  for  the  Colonies. 

H.M.S.  "Alligator,"  Captain  Lambert,  was  detached  from  the  East 
India  squadron  to  visit  Australia  and  Polynesia.  Spent  March  of  1834  in 
the  Bay  of  Islands  and  Whangaroa.  Enthusiastic  account  of  the  mission 
and  Mr.  Busby,  the  British  Resident.  Account  of  the  gift  of  a  national 
flag  to  the  New-Zealanders.  Baron  Hugel  was  a  passenger.  Second  visit. 
Despatched  from  Sydney  to  rescue  British  subjects  wTccked  in  the  "  Har- 
riet," and  made  captive  by  the  New-Zealanders  at  Taranaki.  Remained 
in  New  Zealand  September  and  October,  1834,  Good  description  of  native 
pa  and  huts  on  the  Sugar-loaves.  The  natives  treated  on  this  expedition 
with  cruel  severity  and  injustice.  Appendix  gives  Jackey  Guard's  account 
of  the  wTcck ;  contains  also  Lord  Goderich's  letter  to  the  chiefs,  and  Mr. 
Busby's  address  to  them.  See  also  pariiamentary  papers,  31st  August,  1835, 
No.  585. 

Dr.  Marshall  lost  his  hfe  in  the  Niger  expedition,  1841. 

Murray,  John.  An  Account  |  of  |  the  Phormium  tenax  |  or  |  New-Zealand 
Flax.  I  Printed  on  Paper,  made  from  its  Leaves.  |  With  |  a  Postcript  on 
Paper.  |  By  John  Murray,  F.S.A.,  F.L.S.,  &c.  |  London :  |  Henry  Renshaw, 
356  Strand.  I  1836. 

8vo.  Pp.  34,  inclusive  of  vii.  Frontispiece  of  New  Zealand  flax  plant. 
list  of  works  (17)  by  John  Murray,  with  general  reiharks  thereupon.  Dedi- 
cated to  Lord  John  Russell. 

Curious  and  rare  ;  the  paper  of  a  faint  bro^vn  or  cream  tinge,  and  as 
though  peppered.  Unrestricted  importation  of  flax  would  develop  inter- 
course with  and  civihsation  of  New-Zealanders.  Its  cultivation  in  Great 
Britain  would  prove  a  substitute  for  Russian  hemp.  Paper  made  from  it 
vastly  superior  to  the  ^Tetched  modern  paper,  which  threatens  to  sweep 
away  books  and  records.  Description  and  mode  of  preparation  by  the 
natives.  Is  reared  in  the  Botanical  Gardens  of  Edinburgh,  Glasgow,  Guernsey, 
Paris,  and  in  England.  Brought  to  Ireland  in  1798.  In  1831  Captain  Harris 
opened  a  manufactory  in  Grimsby.  Price  in  British  market,  £18  to  £25  per 
ton.  In  1828,  60  tons  imported  to  England  ;  1830,  841  tons ;  1831,  1,062 
tons;  1835,  300  tons.  Postcript  dwells  on  the  badness  and  adulteration  of 
modern  paper.     There  was  a  2nd  ed. 

The  author  was  a  prolific  lecturer  and  writer  on  scientific  subjects 
(1786-1851).

Report  of  Formation,  &c.,  of  New-Zealand  Temperance  Society — continued. 

The  first  English  book  (or  pamphlet)  printed  in  New  Zealand.  The 
first  Maori  book  was  the  Epistle  to  the  Ephesians  and  PhiUppians,  February 
1835 :  Ko  nga  |  Pukapuka  |  o  Paora  te  Apotoro  |  ki  te  hunga  o  |  Epeha, 
o  Piripai.  Both  were  printed  by  W.  Colenso  on  the  Church  Missionary 
Society  press,  which  was  landed  at  Paihia  on  the  30th  December,  1835.  The 
printed  notice  calling  the  temperance  meeting  was :  Temperance  Society.  | 
Wednesday,  the  11th  day  of  May,  inst.  |  A  |  Public  Meeting  |  will  be  held  in 
the  I  Church  at  Kororarika,  |  for  the  Purpose  of  EstabUshing  a  |  Tempe- 
rance Society.  |  The  attendance  of  all  Persons  desirous  of  promoting  |  Peace, 
Order,  and  Sobriety  is  most  earnestly  requested.  |  The  British  Resident  will 
take  the  Chair  at  12  o'clock.  |  Dated,  May  4th,  1836.  |  Paihia:  Printed  at 
the  Press  of  the  Church  Missionary  Society. 

Report  I  from  the  \  Select  Committee  |  on  |  the  Disposal  of  Lands  |  in  the

Fol.  Pp.  viii,  259.  Plan  of  Port  Phillip  and  Dutigalla  (Batman's  pur- 
chase). 

Almost  the  first  step  in  the  colonisation  of  New  Zealand  was  taken 
with  this  report.  E.  G.  Wakefield  was  the  principal  witness,  and  in  his 
reply  to  question  961  he  recommends  this  "  most  beautiful  country,  with 
the  finest  climate  and  most  productive  soil,"  as  being  the  fittest  in  the  world 
for  colonisation.  At  the  close  of  the  examination  Mr.  H.  G.  Ward,  the 
chairman,  promised  to  assist  Mr.  Wakefield  in  any  undertaking  of  the  sort. 

Yate,  Bev.  W.  Devotedness  to  God.  |  A  farewell  Sermon  |  preached  at 
St.  Swithin's  Church,  Cannon  Street,  January  24,  1836.  |  By  the  Rev. 
William  Yate,  |  previous  to  his  Departure  to  New  Zealand.  | 

Appears  in  the  Pulpit,  Vol.  xxvii.  No.  697.     Pp.  10. 

"  The  first  time  I  ever  addressed  a  Christian  assembly  was  from  this 
pulpit ;  the  last  sermon  when  going  to  New  Zealand  was  from  this  pulpit ; 
the  first  I  preached  on  my  return  was  here."  Credited  by  the  ancient  people 
at  the  Bay  of  Islands  as  an  eloquent  preacher.  On  his  return,  via  Sydney,  he 
was  temporarily  appointed  to  the  charge  of  St.  James's  Church,  vacant  by 
the  sudden  death  of  the  Rev.  Richard  Hill.

The  "  Annals  of  the  Propagation  of  the  Faith  "  were  published  annually, 
containing  several  monthly  numbers.  With  this,  the  10th  vol.,  commences 
the  history  of  the  Roman  Catholic  faith  in  New  Zealand,  under  Bishop  Pom- 
pallier.  Throughout  the  volumes  are  letters  from  the  Marist  fathers  who 
performed  the  missionary  work  of  the  order,  with  other  information.  They 
are  equivalent  to  the  "  Mission  Records  "  of  Protestants. 

Susby,    J.     Letter  from  James  Busby,  Esq.,   British  Resident  at  New  Zea- 
land, to  the  Honourable  the  Colonial  Secretary  of  New  South  Wales. 

Dated  16th  June,  1837. 

Describes  the  warlike  and  turbulent  state  of  the  natives,  which  requires 
the  control  of  a  paramount  authority,  supported  by  adequate  force.  The 
chiefs  should  be  induced  to  lend  their  aid,  under  the  guidance  of  the  mis- 
sionaries, and  80  form  a  government. 

This  and  other  oflficial  documents  from  Governor  Bourke,  Captain 
Hobson,  and  British  settlers  are  printed  in  parliamentary  papers,  7th  February, 
1838,  No.  122,  and  copied  bv  Dr.  Hinds  in  his  "  Latest  Oflficial  Documents," 
1838,  q.v.

In  1833,  1836,  and  1837  a  Committee  of  the  House  of  Commons  was 
appointed  to  report  on  r.  means  for  promoting  the  interests  of  savage  tribes- 
in  British  settlements,  q.v.     An  incomplete  report  was  issued  in  1836,  q.v. 

Further  evidence  a.nd  the  full  report  were  printed  in  1837. 

This  book  reprints  so  much  of  the  evidence  as  indicates  the  effectual 
means  to  be  the  Gospd  and  missionary  effort.  Dealing  chiefly  with  outrage 
and  the  disastrous  results  of  intercourse  between  the  New-Zealanders  and 
unprincipled  white  men,  it  rhay  be  viewed  as  the  gauntlet  thrown  down  in 
the  coming  fray  between  the  missionary  party  and  the  band  of  colonisers- 
under  Wakefield. 

Colonization  of  New  Zealand.     Blackwood's  Magazine,  xliii,  784. 

A  favourable  review  of  the  "  British  Colonization  of  New  Zealand  " 
and  of  the  Association's  plans.  >

Magazine,  xhi,  784. 

Describes  and  favours  the  New  Zealand  Association's  scheme:

"Wakefield,   E.  G.     Popular    Politics,  |  by  |  Edward    Gibbon  Wakefield,  Esq. 
i  London  :  |  (Charles  Knight  &  Co.,  22,  Ludgate  Street.  |  MDCCCXXXVII.

"  Consisting  of  extracts  from  various  publications,  by  the  same  author, 
all  of  which  are  out  of  print."  Rare.  Many  are  extracted  from  his  publi-

of  the  people,  universal  suffrage,  abolition  of  com  laws  and  punishment  by 
hanging,  &c. 

In  1836  Wakefield  unsuccessfully  contested  a  seat  for  Birmingham.

Fcap.  8vo.  Pp.  xvi,  422.  5  small  charts  of  New  Zealand  and  Polynesia, 
and  5  illustrations.     Dated  20th  October,  1837. 

Following  the  pamphlet  or  prospectus  issued  in  June  of  this  year,  this 
is  the  first  special  exposition  of  the  New  Zealand  Association  (afterwards  the 
New  Zealand  Company).  It  was  ably  written  by  E.  G.  Wakefield  and  by 
John  Ward,  the  secretary  of    the  Association.     It  proposes  the  principles 


64  Bibliography  of  [183T 


[Wakefield,  E.  G.,  and  Ward,  John]— continued. 

and  mode  of  colonisation  and  government  in  New  Zealand,  including  the  civil- 
isation of  the  natives.  A  description  of  New  Zealand  follows — topography,, 
native  race,  productions,  trade,  and  shipping — derived  from  many  sources, 
and  forming  an  interesting  summary.  The  appendix  has  an  essay,  with 
several  romantic  suggestions,  on  the  proper  conduct  of  Europeans  towards 
the  natives,  written  by  the  Rev.  M.  J.  Hawtrey,  q.v.  List  of  ten  works  on 
New  Zealand,  from  Cook,  in  1770,  to  "  Report  on  Aborigines,"  in  1837. 

As  the  first  work  of  importance  issued  by  the  Association,  this  book  is 
a  classic. 

W"ah:efleld,   E.   G.       IVIr.  Dandeson  Coates,  |  and  the  |  New  Zealand  Associa- 
tion ;    I    in   I    a     Letter  |    to   |    the     Right     Hon.     Lord     Glenelg,   |    by   | 
E.  G.  Wakefield,  Esq.  |  London :  |  Henry  Hooper,  13  Pall  Mall  East. 

8vo.     Pp.  26.     Dated  Hans  Place,  12th  December,  1837. 

A  smart  rejoinder  to  Mr.  Coates's  hostile  pamphlet.  Publishes  state- 
ments of  Mr.  Flatt,  a  returned  New  Zealand  catechist,  that  Church  mis- 
sionaries there  possess  large  tracts  of  land  and  stock  ;  that  expenditure  on 
mission  is  £13,000  a  year,  with  little  result ;  and  that  a  well-organized  civil 
and  religious  estabhshment  is  necessary  for  the  well-being  of  the  country. 

From  this  time  the  controversy  became  heated  and  widespread,  reviews 
and  newspapers  entering  the  lists.  Blackwood,  December,  1837,  very  favour- 
able  to  the  Association's  plans.     Eclectic  Review,  Ixvii. 


1838. 
Bannister,  Saxe. 

The  Journal  of  Statistical  Society  for  October  has  article,  "  Account  of 
the  Changes  and  Present  Condition  of  the  Population  of  New  Zealand." 

Attributes  disappearance  of  the  native  race  to  their  wars,  superstitions, 
bewitching,  degraded  state  of  women  and  polygamy,  want  of  food  o-wing 
to  constant  warfare,  and  introduced  diseases.

There  were  three  editions  or  reprints,  the  second  having  a  terminal 
note  on  the  Biighis  of  Celebes,  and  the  third  a  circular  letter  of  the  author's. 

An  attack  on  the  New  Zealand  Association's  proposals.  "  Our  colonies 
have  been  based  upon  a  principle  of  unrighteousness "  and  ursurpation, 
which  results  in  the  invariable  destruction  of  native  races.  The  recent 
proposals  of  the  New  Zealand  Association  would  have  the  same  result ; 
they  are  specious  and  insincere,  and  merit  condemnation. 

Mr.  Beecham  was  secretary  of  the  Wesleyan  Missionary  Society.

St.,  j  Hamilton,   Adams,    and   Co.,   and  John  Mason,    Paternoster  Row.  I

Concludes  that  the  plan  proposed  by  the  Association  is  incompetent  to 
remedy  the  ills  of  New  Zealand.  The  case  cannot  be  met  by  any  mercantile

officers.  The  postscript  rephes  to  Dr.  Hind's  "  Latest  Official  Documents," 
just  come  to  hand.     Excellently  argued. 

A  I  Bill  I  for  the  |  Provisional  Government  of  British  Settlements  |  in  the 
Islands  of  New  Zealand.     (1  June,  1838.— 1  Vict.) 

Parliamentary  papers,  No.  443.  Fol.  Pp.  25.  Prepared  and  brought 
in  by  Mr.  Francis  Baring  and  Sir  George  Sinclair,  19th  June,  1838. 

Embodies  the  views  of  the  Association,  as  modified  after  discussion 
with  Lord  Melbourne,  the  Prime  Minister,  and  with  Lord  Howick.  Leading 
features  are  cession  by  treaty  of  New  Zealand  to  the  Queen,  who  should  vest 
the  government  in  Commissioners ;  systematic  sale  of  land  for  public  pur- 
poses ;  and  beneficial  provision  for  the  natives.  Omng  to  violent  opposition 
of  the  missionary  party,  Parhament  threw  out  the  Bill,  with  disaster  to  the 
Association.  For  debate  vide  Hansard;  also  New  Zealand  Gazette  (newspaper). 
No.  1,  August  and  September,  1839.

Chapman,  H.  S.     The  Colonization  of  New  Zealand. 

Article  by  Henry  Samuel  (Mr.  Justice)  Chapman  in  the  Dvhlin  Review 
for  January. 

The  writer  was  a  friend  of  E.  G.  Wakefield,  and  an  ardent  promoter 
of  his  scheme,  and  here  points  out  his  method.

sidered, I  in  a  Letter  |  to  J.  P.  Plumptre,  Esq.,  M.P.  |  By  Dandeson

8vo.     Pp.  32.     Dated  1st  February. 

Valuable. 

Recapitulation  of  steps  taken  to  prevent  colonisation  by  the  New  Zea- 
land Association. 

Mr.  Plumptre  was  a  vice-president  of  the  Church  Missionary  Society, 
and  a  philanthropist. 

The  I  Colonial  Gazette.  |  A  Weekly  Journal.  |  "  Ships,  Colonies,  and  Com- 
merce." I  Volume  First,  j  1839.  |  London :  |  Published  by  Joseph  Clayton, 
at  9,  Wellington  Street,  Strand. 

Fol.  Pp.  908.  3  columns,  reduced  to  2  columns  with  the  37th  number. 
A  weekly  issue  of  16  pages,  upon  Saturdays,  changing  with  its  38th 
number  to  Wednesdays.  The  first  number  appeared  on  the  1st  December, 
1838. 

3 


66  Bibliography  op  [1838 


The  Colonial  Gazette — continued. 

This  important  journal  was  published  in  connection  with  the  Colonial 
Society,  which  was  formed  in  July,  1837,  for  the  purpose  of  watching  over 
the  interests  of  the  colonies  of  the  Empire.  In  August,  1839,  it  passed  into 
the  hands  of  the  conductors  of  the  Spectator,  notably  Mr,  Rintoul.  It  is 
replete  with  the  most  varied  and  special  information  regarding  all  the  British 
colonies — parliamentary  and  other  news,  trade,  shipping,  and  every  con- 
ceivable question  relating  to  them.  Its  details  connected  with  the  earhest 
steps  of  New  Zealand  colonisation  are  full  and  important,  and  the  advent 
of  the  New  Zealand  Journal  in  1840  by  no  means  detracted  from  its  special 
notices  of  the  young  colony.  In  1847  it  changed  its  title  to  that  of  West 
India  Mail  and  Colonial  Gazette. 


The  Colonization  of  New  Zealand. 

Article  in  Christian  Observer,  xxxviii,  131. 
Opposed  to  the  Association.

Oblong  folio.  Description  of  the  plates,  1  page.  10  coloured  litho- 
graphs, on  thick  cartridge-paper:  1.  Herald,  or  peace- maker — Awow ; 
2.  The  wounded  chief  Honghi  and  his  family ;  3.  Slaves  preparing  food ; 
4.  Village  of  Parkuni,  River  Hokianga ;  5.  Herald,  or  peace-maker,  and 
Bay  of  Islands  chief ;  6.  Kororadika  Beach,  Bay  of  Islands ;  7.  Native 
family ;  8.  Native  village  and  cowdie  forest ;  9.  War  speech  previous  to  a 
naval  expedition ;    10.  Amoko,  Eana,  Hepee. 

Very  rare. 

The  title-page  has  view  of  New  Zealand  scenery  and  imprint  of  the 
New  Zealand  Association's  device — two  shields,  one  of  the  British  arms,  the 
other  with  the  New  Zealand  flag  surmounted  by  the  British  Crown  and  sup- 
ported by  a  New-Zealander  and  a  British  seaman ;  motto,  "  Ships,  Colonies, 
and  Commerce."     Plates  faithful  and  well  executed. 


The  First  Annual  Report  of  the  Aborigines  Protection  Society,  presented  at 
the  meeting  in  Exeter  Hall,  May  16,  1838,  with  list  of  officers,  subscribers 
and  benefactors,  &c.  London :  Sold  by  W.  Ball,  Aldine  Chambers,  &c. 
1838. 

Svc.     Pp.  31. 

The  society  was  instituted  in  1836  to  assist  the  protection  and  promote 
the  advancement  of  uncivilised  tribes ;  T.  F.  Buxton,  Esq.,  president. 
The  House  of  Commons  "Report  on  Aborigines"  in  1836  gave  chief 
impulse  to  the  society.     There  were  annual  reports  from  this  date. 


1838]  New  Zealand  Literature.  67 


Hinds,     Rev.    Dr.      The    latest    |    Ofl&cial    Documents    |    relating    to    |    New 
Zealand;  |  with  |  introductory    observations,  |  by  |  Samuel    Hinds,    D.D.,  | 
Vicar  of  Yardley,  Herts :  |  and  one  of  the  Committee  of  the  New  Zealand 
Association.  |  London :  |  Jolm  W.  Parker,  West  Strand.  I  MDCCCXXXVIII. 

8vo.     Pp.  46. 

The  "  Official  Documents,"  of  which  the  originals  are  to  be  found  in 
parliamentary  papers — Correspondence,  1 3th  April,  1840,  p.  8  et  aeq.,  and 
7th  February,  1838,  No.  12,  are — (1)  Despatch  from  Governor  Bourke, 
Sydney,  9th  September,  1837,  stating  that  he  had  requested  Captain 
Hobson,  of  the  "  Rattlesnake,"  to  visit  New  Zealand,  and  to  suggest 
the  best  means  of  securing  the  common  interests  of  natives  and  settlers. 
This  encloses  (2)  Captain  Hobson's  reply,  dated  Port  Jackson,  8th  August, 
1837,  containing  details  of  observations,  and  suggesting  a  form  of  British 
jurisdiction.  It  also  encloses  (3)  a  long  letter  from  Mr.  Busby,  British 
Resident  at  New  Zealand,  dated  Bay  of  Islands,  16th  June,  1837,  epitomizing 
state  of  natives,  Europeans,  missionaries,  and  jurisdiction,  and  affirming 
the  advantages  of  established  authority.  (4.)  Petition  to  the  King  from 
200  British  settlers  in  New  Zealand  re  de  Thierry's  proposed  usurpation, 
and  asking  for  protection.  From  these  Dr.  Hinds,  in  his  "  Introductory 
Observations,"  supports  the  proposals  of  the  New  Zealand  Association, 
and  temperately  replies  to  Messrs.  Beecham  and  Coates. 

Dr.  Hindft  (1792-1872)  was  afterwards  Dean  of  Carlisle  and  Bishop  of 
Norwich.     A  firm  friend  of  New  Zealand  colonisation. 


House    of    Lords    Report.     (Brought    from    the    Lords,    7th    August,    1838.) 
Report  I  from  the  |  Select  Committee  of  the  House  of  Lords,  |  appointed  to 
inquire  into  |  the  present  State  |  of  |  the  Islands  of  New  Zealand,  |  and  ] 
the  Expediency  of  |  regulating  the  Settlement  of  British  |  Subjects  therein ; 
I  Avith  I  the   Minutes   of   Evidence  |  taken   before  the   Committee,  |  and  | 
an  Index  thereto.  |  Ordered,  by  the  House  of  Commons,  to  be  Printed,  | 
8th  August,  1838. 

Parliamentary  papers.  No.  680.     Fol.     Pp.  iii,  376.     Plan  of  Hokianga. 

This  Committee  was  appointed  on  the  motion  of  Lord  Devon,  a  supporter 
of  the  New  Zealand  Association,  and  sat  from  the  3rd  April  to  the  21st  May. 
The  evidence  abounds  in  history  and  information  regarding  the  country, 
natives,  missionaries,  traders,  and  proposed  colonisation.  The  witnesses 
were  J.  L.  Nicholas,  the  companion  of  Rev.  S.  Marsden  in  1814 ;  Watkins, 
surgeon,  1833-34 ;  Flatt,  catechist,  1834-37  ;  Montefiore,  Sydney  merchant, 
1830 ;  Enderby,  south  whale-fishery,  from  1794 ;  Polack,  trader,  1831-37  ; 
Rev.  F.  Wilkinson,  1837;  Tawell,  surgeon,  1837;  Nayti,  the  New-Zea- 
lander  who  returned  to  New  Zealand  in  the  "  Tory "  ;  Captain  Fitzroy, 
of  the  "  Beagle  "  ;  Rev.  J.  Beecham,  Coates,  and  Garratt,  of  the  missionary 
societies ;  EUiott,  Agent-General  for  Emigration  (gives  much  information 
on  the  subject) ;  and  Lord  Petre,  Hinds,  Baring,  and  Dr.  Evans,  members 
of  the  New  Zealand  Association. 

Because  of  the  opposition  to  the  Committee  and  to  the  scheme  the 
resolutions  were  vague — "  The  question  of  colonial  extension  was  one  of 
public  policy,  and  for  the  Government  to  decide ;  if  support  were  given,  it 
should  be  in  the  direction  which  had  already  advanced  the  native  welfare."

Marsh,  Bev.  E.  G.  An  Inquiry  |  into  the  |  Equity,  Practicability  and  Ex- 
pediency I  of  the  Proposal  for  |  colonizing  New  Zealand.  |  By  |  Edward 
Garrard  Marsh,  M.A.  |  Loudon :  Published  by  L.  &  G.  Seeley,  169  Fleet  St. 
1  MDCCCXXXVIII. 

8vo.     Pp.  23. 

Asserts,  as  result  of  past  experience,  that  the  scheme  of  the  New  Zealand 
Association  must  probably  fail.  It  lacks  justice  and  the  chance  of  success, 
and  would  injure  the  natives,  who  are  progressing  under  missionary  efforts, 
which  should  be  supported  by  some  interposition  of  the  British  Govern- 
ment. 

Mr.  Marsh  was  vicar  of  Aylesford  and  Canon  of  Southwell,  cousin  and 
brother-in-law  of  Archdeacon  Henry  Williams.  Reference  is  made  to  him 
in  Carleton's  "  Life  of  Henry  Williams." 

*  New  Zealand.     Penny  Magazine,  vii,  325,  417. 


New  Zealand  Colonization. 

The  Christian  Observer  for  January  and  February  sketches,  and  bitterly 
opposes,  the  New  Zealand  Association's  scheme,  and  is  especially  severe 
on  Mr.  E.  G.  Wakefield,  quoting  from  a  letter  written  in  the  Watchman  (the 
Wesleyan  newspaper)  of  the  17th  January  by  the  Rev.  William  White, 
an  ex- Wesleyan  missionary,  who  makes  disconcerting  remarks,  derived 
from  personal  knowledge  of  Mr.  Wakefield  and  some  of  his  friends.

Polack,  J.  S. — continued. 

8vo.  2  vols.  Pp.  xii,  403,  vi,  441.  Map  of  New  Zealand  from  Cook, 
D'Urville,  Duperrey,  and  Herd,  with  additions  by  J.  S.  Polack,  1838 ;  and 
insets  of  Bay  of  Islands,  Wangari  or  Bream  Bay,  and  East  Cape.  6  full- 
page  engravings  and  several  woodcuts  from  the  author's  drawings. 

Full  of  research  and  observation.  History,  topography,  and  natural 
products  of  New  Zealand ;  traditions  and  customs  of  the  natives ;  first 
pakehas  ;  missions  ;  and  Baron  de  Thierry's  pretensions.  Abounds  in  curious 
information  gathered  from  the  natives  whilst  travelling.  First  known  refer- 
ence to  the  moa  is  on  p.  303,  Vol.  i.  On  p.  282,  Vol.  ii,  is  a  list  of  numerals 
in  sixty  Pacific,  Indian,  and  African  languages.  The  notes  give  an  account 
of  the  discovery  and  productions  of  the  outlying  islands  of  New  Zealand 
and  the  whale-fishery. 

Josl  Samuel  Polack,  a  storekeeper  and  flax- trader,  resided  at  Kororareka 
(now  Russell).  Was  a  Londoner,  and  gav?  evidence  before  the  Lords  Com- 
mittee in  1838.  He  returned  to  New  Zealand  after  its  colonisation  and  lived 
in  Auckland.  Left  for  Calif ornian  gold-diggings  in  1849,  and  there  died.  By 
turns  had  been  an  artist  in  Europe,  a  ship-chandler  in  Australia,  in  the  Com- 
missariat and  Ordnance  Departments  in  Africa,  and  a  traveller  in  America. 

Reviewed  in  Eclectic,  Vols.  Ixx  and  xc,  and  Monthly,  Vol.  nxlvii.  The 
former  Eclectic  article  is  reprinted  in  John  Foster's  "  Fosteriana,"  London, 
1858,  pp.  538-57.  .^  ^

A  splendid  and  laborious  compilation  from  all  sources  of  voyages  and

of  the  author's  critical  notes  and  observations,  and  20  plates.  The  out- 
Ijnng  islands  are  also  described.

Alison,  A. — continued. 

Sm.  8vo.     Pp.  22. 

Addressed  to  the  first  body  of  New  Zealand  emigrants  from  the  Clyde, 
on  the  eve  of  their  departure  in  the  "  Bengal  Merchant."  An  elaborate 
speech,  with  statistics,  insisting  on  the  development  of  emigration  and  close 
relations  with  emigrants. 

The  writer  afterwards  became  Sir  Archibald  Alison. 


Beale,  Thomas.  The  |  Natural  History  |  of  |  the  Sperm  Whale :  |  its

8vo.  Pp.  393.  3  engravings  and  6  woodcuts.  The  2nd  ed.,  though  not 
so  indicated  on  title-page. 

Beale  cruised  in  the  Pacific  during  1831-32,  visiting  many  of  the  islands, 
to  which  he  makes  interesting  reference.  Recites  Polynesian  legend  of 
Kinau  and  Tuanoa.  Mr.  Enderbj'  in  1788  extended  the  sperm-whale  fishery 
to  the  Pacific.  "  In  the  year  1802  ships  were  sent  to  whale  off  the  island  of 
New  Zealand." 

Specially  full  articles,  with  notes,  on  the  history  of  the  whale  and  whaling 
are  in  Simmonds's  Colonial  Magazine  for  1844-45,  and  in  the  New  Zealand 
Gazette  for  1842.  They  were  written  by  "  W."  (Edward  Wakefield,  the 
father  of  Edward  Gibbon  Wakefield). 

*  The  Colonization  of  New  Zealand.     Monthly  Review,  cl.  38. 


Cunningliain,  A.,  F.L.S.  On  the  Habits  of  the  Apteryz  Australis,  a  Bird 
of  New  Zealand,  closely  allied  to  the  Struthionidce,  and  named  by  the  native

Paper  in  "  Annals  of  Natural  History  ;  or,  Magazine  of  Zoology,  Botany,

(by  aid  of  the  Missionaries)  on  the  Habits  of  the  Apierijx  Australis." 

Describes  its  egg,  nest,  and  habits,  and  mode  of  capture.

Contains  also  the  missionaries'  vindication  of  their  purchases  of  land  and 
their  views  respecting  the  New  Zealand  Association,  and  describes  the  for-

ment.  The  last  is  interesting,  giving  the  fifteen  resolutions  of  the  Kororarika 
Association,  formed  on  the  23rd  May,   1838,  by  the  residents  for  self-pro-

Confidential  Documents,  &c. — continued. 

tection  from  vicious  whites  and  troublesome  natives.  Every  member  to 
provide  himself  with  a  musket,  bayonet,  brace  of  pistols,  cutlass,  and  30 
rounds  of  ball  cartridge.     Fines  ranged  chiefly  from  £5  to  £10.

Durham,    Earl    of.       The    Report  |    and  1    Despatches  |    of  |    the    Earl   of

MDCCCXXXIX. 

8vo.     Pp.  xvi,  423. 

Lord  Durham  was  the  first  governor  of  the  New  Zealand  Company. 
On  the  occasion  of  his  visit  to  Canada  he  was  accompanied  by  E.  G.  Wake- 
field and  Charles  Buller,  who,  it  is  said,  had  considerable  part  in  the  prepara- 
tion of  this  report,  which  contains  the  scheme  for  granting  self-government 
to  the  colonies.     A  reprint  appeared  in  1905. 

Instructions    |    from    j    The   New   Zealand    Land    Company    I    to     |    Colonel 
Wakefield,  |  Principal  Agent  of  the  Company. 

Fcp.  8vo.     Pp.  23. 

Also  found  in  "  Information  relative  to  New  Zealand,"  and  in  "  Corre- 
spondence with  the  Secretarv  of  State  relative  tc  New  Zealand  "  ;  1840 ; 
pp.  22-27. 

Relate  to  purchase  of  lands,  acquisition  of  general  information  as  tc  the 
country,  and  preparation  for  the  formation  of  settlements  under  the  auspices 
of  the  company. 

"King,  Capt.  P.  Parker,  and  Pitzroy,  Capt.  Robert.  Narrative  |  of  the

Vol.  i.  —  Sub-title :  Proceedings  |  of  |  the  First  Expedition,  |  1826- 
1830,  I  under  the  Command  of  |  Captain  P.  Parker  King,  |  R.N.,  F.R.S.

-  72  Bibliography  of  [1839 


King,  Ca'pt  P.  Parker,  and  Pitzroy,  Capt.  ^ohett— continued. 

with  insets,  and  16  plates  (south-west  coasts  of  South  America).  Appendix 
contains  compass,  latitude  and  longitude,  tide,  and  height  tables ;  magnetic 
observations  ;  zoology,  &c.  The  expedition  left  Plymouth  for  Monte  Video 
on  the  22nd  May,  1826,  and  again  reached  Plymouth  on  the  14th  October, 

1830,  the  ships  being  there  paid  off.  Captain  King  was  the  son  of  Governor 
King,  and  was  the  well-known  Australian  explorer.  Ca2:)tain  Fitzroy  com- 
manded the  "  Beagle."  Mr.  (afterwards  Admiral)  John  Lort  Stokes,  who 
afterwards  surveyed  the  New  Zealand  coasts,  was  a  midshipman  on  this 
voyage. 

Vol.  ii. — Sub-title:  Proceedings  |  of  |  the  Second  Expedition,  |  1831- 
1836,  I  under  the  Command  of  |  Captain  Robert  Fitzroy,  R.N.  Pp.  xiv 
(1  page  of  directions  to  binder),  694  (1  page  addenda).  2  charts  and  25  plates 
(1  of  "New  Zealanders ").     The  "Beagle"  left   England   27th   December, 

1831,  and  completed  her  circumnavigation  2nd  October,  1836.  Mr.  Stokes  was 
mate  and  assistant  surveyor;  Augustus  Earle  {q.v.),  draughtsman;  Charles 
Darwin,  naturahst ;  Edward  Main  (Edmund  Mein)  Chaffers,  master  (after- 
wards, in  1839,  appointed  to  command  the  New  Zealand  Company's  first 
ship,  the  "Tory  ") ;  and  Richard  Matthews  {q.v.),  a  missionary  to  the  Fuegians, 
who  afterwards  re-embarked  and  remained  in  New  Zealand.  Chapters 
xxiv  to  XX vi  relate  to  the  Bay  of  Islands,  where  the  "  Beagle  "  remained 
for  the  last  ten  days  of  1835.  Short  account  of  the  natives,  missionaries,  and 
traders.  The  appendix  to  Vol.  ii  forms  a  separate  volume ;  pp.  viii,  352  ; 
2  charts,  with  insets,  diagrams,  and  plates  of  clouds.  Contains  various 
tables,  official  correspondence,  account  of  the  Fuegians,  and  a  vocabulary 
of  their  language ;  correspondence  from  and  to  Mr.  Busby,  British  Resident 
at  New  Zealand,  relating  to  Baron  de  Thierry  ;  declaration  of  independence  ; 
and  instructions  from  Governor  Bourke. 

Vol.  iii.— Sub-title :  Journal  and  Remarks.  |  1832-1836.  j  By  |  Charles 
Darwin,  Esq.,  M.A.  |  Sec.  Geol.  Soc.  |  Pp.  xiv,  629 ;  index,  7  pages  (wrongly 
paginated  609  to  615).  2  charts  (southern  portion  of  South  America,  and 
Keeling  Islands).  Forms  the  original  of  the  "  Naturalist's  Voyage  round 
the  World,"  which  in  the  2nd  (1845)  and  still  later  editions  was  recast. 
19  pages  refer  to  New  Zealand,  of  which  Mr.  Darwin  formed  no  favourable 
opinion.  The  work  was  published  under  the  superintendence  of  Captain 
Robert  Fitzroy,  afterwards  the  second  Governor  of  New  Zealand,  and  later 
known  as  Admiral  and  in  charge  of  the  Weather  Bureau. 


Lang,  Rev.  Dr.  J.  D.  New  Zealand  |  in  |  1839 :  |  or  |  Four  Letters,  I  to  I  The 
Right  Hon.  Earl  Durham,  |  Governor  of  the  New  Zealand  Land  Company, 
I  &c.  &c.  &c.  I  On  the  Colonization  of  that  Island,  |  and  |  on  the  present 
Condition  and  Prospects  of  its  |  Native  Inhabitants.  |  By  |  John  Dunmore 
Lang,  D.D.  |  Principal  of  the  Australian  College,  and  Senior  Minister  |  of 
the  Church  of  Scotland  in  New  South  Wales.  |  London  :  |  Smith,  Elder, 
and  Co.,  65,  Cornhill.  |  1839. 

8vo.     Pp.  iv,  120.     Appendix. 

Written  on  Dr.  Lang's  fifth  voyage  to  England,  in  the  "  Roshn  Castle," 
which  put  into  the  Bay  of  Islands  for  a  few  days  in  a  leaky  state  in  January, 
1839  :  Ist,  demoralised  Europeans  are  ruining  a  fine  native  race,  and  it  is 
imperative  Government  should  found  a  British  colony  on  Christian  principles ; 
2nd,  a  bitter  diatribe  on  the  Church  missionaries  and  their  mixed  plan  of 
evangelising ;  3rd,  the  high  suitability  of  New  Zealand  for  colonisation  ;  4th, 
the  best  means  of  colonising.  The  appendix,  "  South  Sea  Islands,"  gives  an 
account  of  the  treatment  of  the  Tahitians  in  1838  by  Du  Petit-Thouars,  captain 
of  the  French  frigate  "  La  Venus."  Contains  many  httle  pieces  of  history 
and  scandal,  list  of  vessels  visiting  the  Bay  of  Islands  in  1838,  and  10  pages 
on  the  origin  of  the  Polynesians.     {Vide  Taifs  Magazine,  n.s.,  vi,  611.) 

Republished  at  Sydney,  July,  1873,  "  With  a  preface  and  appendix  for 
1873."  Pp.  vii,  96.  Slightly  recast,  and  substituting  initials  for  names. 
List  of  vessels  (132),  Polynesian  origin,  and  appendix  omitted,  with  a  sub-

Lang,  Rev.  Dr.  J.  D. — continued. 

stitution  on  early  landsharking  in  New  Zealand.  In  October,  1873,  Mr. 
Edwin  Fairburn  replies  in  a  fly-sheet  to  Dr.  Lang's  strictures  upon  his  father, 
the  missionary. 

Dr.  Lang,  who  was  certainly  of  the  church  militant,  died  at  Sydney, 
8th  August,  1878,  aged  79. 

Lesson,  Rane  Primeverre.      Voyage  |  autour  |  Du  Monde  |  entrepris  par  ordre 
du  Gouvernement  |  sur  la  Corvette  La  Coquille ;  |  par  P.  Lesson,  |  Membre 

correspondant  de  I'lnstitut.  | |  Paris.  |  P.  Pourrat  Freres,  Editeurs,  | 

Rue  des  Petits  Augustins,  5,  |  Et  chez  les  Libraires  et  aux  Depots  de  Pitto-

Roy.  8vo.  2  vols.  Vol.  i:  pp.  511.  Vol.  ii :  pp.  519.  42  plates,  19  of 
which  are  coloured,  and  devoted  to  objects  of  natural  history. 

The  "  Coquille,"  under  the  command  of  Captain  Duperrey,  visited  the 
Bay  of  Islands  in  April,  1824,  remaining  fifteen  days.  The  official  history 
of  the  voyage  was  not  published,  and  the  only  contributions  to  it  are  in  these 
volumes  and  in  the  "  Observations  personelles "  of  M.  D'Urville,  then  an 
officer  of  the  vessel,  published  in  Vol.  iii,  p.  673,  of  the  "Voyage  of  the 
'  Astrolabe,'  "  q.v.  M.  Lesson  was  naturalist  of  the  "  CoquiUe."  His  observa- 
tions are  general,  being  almost  confined  to  an  account  of  the  natives  and 
the  language,  of  which  there  is  a  vocabulary,  chiefly  taken  from  Lee  and 
Kendall's  grammar,  which  had  been  published  shortly  before.

8vo.  Pp.  vi,  360.  Map  of  Australia,  New  Zealand,  and  the  adjacent

A  few  interesting  details  scattered  through  a  poor  book.  Account  of 
official  dealings  with  the  last  of  the  aboriginal  Tasmanians  in  1838.  The 
few  pages  on  New  Zealand  are  taken  chiefly  from  the  Rev.  W.  Yate,  whose 
proper  dismissal  from  the  New  Zealand  mission  was,  in  the  author's  opinion, 
the  result  of  black  conspiracy.
