Pompallier,  Bishop.      Early  History  |  of  |  The  Catholic  Church  |  in  [  Oceania 

I  By  the  |  Rt.   Rev.  Jean  Baptiste  Pran9ois  Pompallier  |  Vicar  Apostolic 

of  Western  Oceania.  |  With  Introduction  |  by  the  |  Rt.  Rev.  John  Edmund 

Luck,    O.S.B.,  I  Bishop    of    Auckland.  |  Auckland,    N.Z.  :  |  H.    Brett,   Star 

office.  1  MDCCCLXXXVIII. 

Large  8vo.     Pp.  84.     Portrait  of  Bishop  Pompallier. 

Bishop  Pompallier  left  Lyons  on  the  24th  December,  1836,  established 
various  Oceanic  missions,  and  landed  at  Hokianga  on  the  10th  January, 
1838,  with  Father  Servant,  the  first  priest.  Their  dangers  and  difficulties ; 
Captain  Cecile  and  the  "  Heroine  "  ;  the  natives  described ;  residence  at 
Kororareka ;  colonisation ;  account  of  signing  the  Treaty  of  Waitangi^ 
journeys  throughout  New  Zealand,  the  French  at  Akaroa,  Dumont  D'Ur- 
ville,  and  Bishop  Viard.  Left  New  Zealand  April,  1846.  Statistics  of  the 
Church,  1838-88.

Edwards,  Dunlop  &  Co.,  |  Melbourne,  &c.  |  1888.

discoinforts  and  dangers,  bushrangers,  and  notabilities.

in  1863.

A  brilliant  and  eloquent  argument  for  the  free  and  full  salvation  of  all,

Vol.  i  has  a  bibliography  of  Terra  Australis,  New  Holland,  and  New 
South  Wales,  to  1820,  and  the  administration  of  the  colonies  in  1787  ;  Secre-

1795,  with  a  list  of  Pitt's  Ministry. 

The  plan  upon  which  this  inestimable  work  was  conducted  was  changed 
whilst  the  2nd  vol.  was  in  progress,  and  the  ensuing  volumes  appeared  under 
an  altered  name  and  character  {vide  "  Bladen,"  1893).  Many  important 
references  to  New  Zealand  appear  throughout  the  volumes.

Contains  much  less  regarding  the  New  Zealand,  and  much  more  of  the 
Lichfield,  diocese  than  Mr.  Tucker's  standard  memoir.  Further  material,, 
however,  has  been  availed  of  —  incidents  of  leaving  the  Waimate  for- 
Auckland  ;  the  Canterbury  settlement ;  St.  John's  College  under  Mr.  Abra- 
ham ;  criticism  on  the  four  sermons  on  "  Christ  in  the  World  "  ;  final  de- 
parture from  Auckland.

.  21st  October,  1868.

Row."  Svo.  Pp.  (preface  n.'p.),  104.  Picture  of  the  author's  battleships 
on  the  cover.  A  clever  story,  culminating  in  a  catastrophe  resulting  from 
England's  unpreparedness  for  war.  Entirely  new  lines  are  suggested  for 
the  construction  of  battleships  which  curiously  foresiiadow  those  on  which 
modern  ironclads  are  built.  This  book,  though  printed,  was  not  published 
until  1884,  when  it  was  issued  with  a  Prologue  of  32  pp.,  wherein  the  author's- 
ideas  of  seventeen  years  previous  are  amplified  and  otherwise  modified. 
In  the  Epilogue  above  they  are  still  further  modified.  Mr.  Fairburn  was  a. 
son  of  one  of  the  earliest  New  Zealand  missionaries.

Descriptive  account  of  the  economic  trees  and  shrubs  of  New  Zealand, 
giving  historical  information  of  each  plant,  properties  and  uses,  value,  mode

technical description  of  flower,  fruit,  and  other  parts  ;  and  a  full  refer- 
ence to  the  plates. 

Appendix  has  a  catalogue  apportioning  the  special  suitability  of  the 
plants,  a  synopsis  of  orders  and  genera,  and  a  glossary.

Report  1  of  the   |   First  Meeting  |  of  the  j  Australasian  Association  |  for  the. 
I  Advancement    of    Science,  |  held    at  \  Sydney,  |  New    South    Wales,  |  In 
August  and  September,   1888,  |  Edited  by  |  A.  Liversedge,  M.A.,  F.R.S.  | 
Robt,   Etheridge,  Junr.  |  Published  bj^  the  Association.  |  Permanent  office 
of  the  Association  :  |  Royal  Society's  House,  5  Elizabeth  St.,  |  Sydney. 

8vo.     Pp.  xxxvi,  659,  vi  (index),  xviii  (list  of  members).     49  plates. 

"  The  Association  has  been  founded  upon  the  same  lines  as  the  British 
Association,  and  its  rules  are  practically  the  same."  The  succeeding  meetings 
were  held  at  the  following  dates  and  places,  each  issuing  its  report :  2nd  Janu- 
ary, 1890,  Melbourne  ;  3rd  January,  1891,  Christchurch  ;  4th  January,  1892, 
Hobart ;  6th  September,  1893,  Adelaide ;  6th  January,  1895,  Brisbane ; 
7th  January,  1898,  Sydney;  8th  January,  1900,  Melbourne  ;  9th  January,  1902, 
Hobart;  10th  January,  1904,  Dunedin  ;  11th  September,  1906,  Adelaide. 

These  volumes  contain  much  of  the  original  scientific  work  of  the  colonies, 
and  have  also  many  articles  on  exploration,  myths,  &c.,  reports  of  research 
committees,  and  lectures.

•Tregear,  E.      The   |  Maori-Polynesian  |   Comparative  Dictionary  |   By  |  Ed- 
ward Tregear  |  F.R.G.S.  ;    F.R.Hist.S.  ;    &c.   &c.  |  "  Manus  manum  lavat." 
I  Wellington,  N.Z.  :  |  Lyon  &  Blair,  Lambton  Quay  |  1891. 

Large  8vo.     Pp.  xxiv,  675.     Double  columns. 

Preface  gives  works  consulted — about  160.  Introduction  of  12  pages 
gives  letter- changes  taking  place  in  11  Polynesian  languages.  Dictionary 
proper  compares  with  Samoan,  Tahitian,  Hawaiian,  Tongan,  Rarotongan, 
Marquesan,  Mangarevan,  Paumotan,  Moriori,  and  extra- Polynesia,  such  as 
Fijian,  Malay,  and  Malagasy.  Gives  examples  of  a  word's  use,  and  contains 
much  mythological  reference.  At  the  end  is  a  partial  Anglo-Maori  vocabu- 
lary. A  work  of  great  research  and  labour,  though  the  philological  con- 
clusions are  not  always  trustworthy  or  justified.

Written  by  two  warm  admirers,  who  had  the  advantage  of  an  intimate

land.

The  1st  vol.  under  the  new  arrangement,  whereby  the  official  docn- 
ments  will  first  be  printed  consecutively  in  full,  and  then  followed  by  the 
historical  portion  based  upon  them  ;  exclusive  of  the  two  which  appea.redi 
under  the  old  arrangement.  7  vols,  have  been  issued  so  far,  the  last  in  1901^ 
coming  down  to  Bligh  and  Macquarie,  1809-10-11,  and  published  under 
the  editorship  of  Mr.  F.  M.  Bladen,  whose  name  first  appears  on  the  title-^ 
page  of  Vol.  ii. 

This  invaluable  work  has  many  fac-similes,  portraits,  illustrations,  and 
reproductions,  with  numerous  paragrajihs  relative  to  New  Zealand,  the  natives, 
whaling,  trade,  Rev.  S.  Marsden  and  the  missionaries,  shipping,  &c.  (Vide 
"  Barton,  G.  B.,"  1889.)

Seas.     By  Frederick  Revans  Chapman. 

8vo.     Pp.  446-459. 

Reprinted  from  the  "  Canadian  Record  of  Science,"  October,  1893. 
Incessant  and  reckless  slaughter  has  caused  their  disappearance.     Short 
account  of  the  seal  islands  and  the  seal  trade.

Jacobs,  Bev.  Dean.      A  Lay  of  the  Southern  Cross  |  and  Other  Poems.  |  By  |

Portrays,  in  prolonged  verse,  the  history  of  the  New  Zealand  mission 
from  its  introduction  by  Marsden  to  the  coming  of  Selwyn ;  sonnets  of  the 
old  pilgrim  days  ;  contributions  towards  a  "  New  Zealand  Christian  Year  "  ; 
a  cantata — "  Pentecost  "  ;   and  occasional  poems.

Much  information,  dating  from  the  Maori  history  written  by  the  Rev. 
J.  W.  Stack,  of  the  early  whalers,  French  settlers,  Hempleman's  purchase, 
the  various  bays,  old  settlers,  legends,  reminiscences,  &c.  Has  additional 
matter  to  that  of  the  1st  ed.,  of  1884  (pp.  vii,  200). 

Journal  of  the  Department  of  Labour.  A  digest  of  the  state  of  the  labour- 
market,  both  European  and  Colonial ;  Conciliation  and  Arbitration  Court 
awards  ;  legal  decisions  ;  Imperial  and  Colonial  notes  ;  and  a  general  bird's- 
eye  view  of  events,  legislative,  commercial,  &c.,  &c.,  in  relation  to  the  labour 
movement. 

8vo.  Published  monthly.  Now  (1908)  divided  into  two  parts,  the 
awards,  &c.,  being  published  separately.

Second  title-page :    "  Ko  nga  |  Waiata  Maori.   |  Na  te  Maori  o  Waikato  e 
tuhituhi,  I  Pepuere,   1864.  |  Na  Hone    Makareka,  |  e  Whakarite.  |  Akarana :

8vo.     Pp.  140. 

A  Supplement  No.  i  appeared  in  1898,  published  by  Champtaloup  and 
Cooper,  Auckland,  containing  about  sixty  additional  songs.     8vo.     Pp.  32. 

Supplement  No.  2  appeared  in  1903,  by  the  same  publishers,  with  sixty 
further  songs  and  many  proverbs.     8vo.     Pp.  33-64,  iii  (index). 

The  editor  was  one  of  the  guard  over  the  Maoris  of  Waikato  when  pri- 
soners of  war  on  the  hulk  "  Marion."  He  did  the  good  service  of  inducing 
them  to  write  these  songs  —  about  420  in  number.  A  few  had  previously 
been  published  by  Sir  George  Grey  and  Mr.  C.  O.  Davis.

New  Zealand  Department  of  Agricuture.  |  John  D.  Ritchie,  Secretary. 
The  I  First  Report  |  of  the  |  Department  of  Agriculture.  |  1893.  |  Welling-

From  this  date  the  Department  has  issued  annual  reports  of  great  value^ 
containing  extensive  information  in  connection  with  the  agricultural  and 
pastoral  industries,  and  including  the  biological  report,  papers  on  noxious 
weeds  and  insects,  butter,  cheese,  dairying,  fruit,  viticulture,  tree-planting, 
veterinary  reports,  soils  and  manures,  diseases,  horses,  sheep,  cattle,  and 
bees,  with  numerous  illustrations.  Written  by  the  accomplished  scientifie 
oflficials  of  the  Department.     The  volume  for  1907  numbers  450  pages.'

An  accomplished  essay,  written  to  excite  interest  in  the  subject  and  to 
induce  New  Zealand  colonists  to  realise  the  importance  of  preserving  their

Sha"W,  Miss  F.  L.     The  Australian  Outlook. 

Paper  read  by  Miss  Flora  L.  Shaw  at  the  Royal  Colonial  Institute,  on 
the  9th  January,  1894. 

A  picturesque  and  sensible  estimate.     Population  much  wanted. 

Miss  Shaw  visited  the  colonies  as  a  special  commissioner  for  the  London 
Times  to  write  up  Australasian  questions  of  a  social  and  political  kind,  amongst 
them  being  black  labour  in  connection  with  sugar-growing.  She  afterwards 
had  charge  of  the  colonial  department  of  The  Times.

Hamilton,  A.  New  Zealand  Institute.  |  The  Art  Workmanship  |  of  the 
Maori  Race  in  New  Zealand  :  |  a  Series  of  Illustrations  from  specially  taken 
Photographs,  |  with  Descriptive  Notes  and  Essays  on  the  |  Canoes,  Habita- 
tions, Weapons,  Ornaments,  and  Dress  |  of  the  Maoris,  |  together  with  j 
Lists  of  the  Words  in  the  Maori  Language  used  |  in  relation  to  the  Subjects. 
I  By  I  Augustus  Hamilton,  |  Registrar  of  the  University  of  Otago,  |  Dun- 
edin. I  1896.  1  Printed  and  published  for  the  Board  of  Governors  |  by  | 
Fergusson  and  Mitchell,  Printers  and  Manufacturing  Stationers,  Princes 
Street,  Dunedin,  N.Z. 

Large  4to.  Pp.  438,  vii.  65  plates,  containing  numerous  figures ;  and 
numerous  illustrations  throughout  the  text.     In  5  parts. 

By  far  the  most  complete  and  authoritative  work  on  the  subject.  The 
5th  part  was  issued  in  1900.  Part  i :  Canoes,  details  and  construction, 
canoe-words,  history  of  the  Maori  migration  to  New  Zealand  with  the  his- 
torical canoes.  Part  ii :  Habitations,  construction,  rafter- patterns,  sections  of 
fortified  pas,  and  words  relating  to  the  subjects.  Part  iii  :  Weapons,  im- 
plements of  agriculture  and  handicraft,  snares,  &c.,  used  in  hunting  rats 
-and  birds  for  food,  with  a  list  of  words  relative.  Part  iv  :  Dress,  personal 
ornaments,  &c.,  and  words  relative.  Part  v  :  Musical  instruments,  games 
and  amusements,  social  institutions,  and  words  relative. 

The  descriptions  and  illustrations  are  very  full,  and  include  much  more 
than  is  contained  in  the. above  list.     The  description  of  the  rafter  patterns

Pviblication  for  the  first  time  of  Banks's  complete  journal,  with  the 
exception  of  extracts  from  the  log  and  other  details  not  worth  reproduction, 
in  the  editor's  opinion.  Altered,  and  welded  with  the  journals  of  Cook  and 
Solander,  it  appeared  in  Ha wkes worth's  edition  of  "  Cook's  Voyages  "  in 
1773.  In  the  preface  a  brief  history  is  given  of  the  journal,  and  of  its  narrow 
escape  from  loss  ;  also  biographical  sketches  of  Banks,  Solander,  and  other 
naturalists  and  voyagers  mentioned  in  the  journal.  List  of  the  "  Endeavour's  " 
officers  and  of  Banks's  staff.

McLennan,    J.    F.      Studies    |    in    |    Ancient    History    |    The   Second   Series 
I    Comprising   an   inquiry  into   the    |    origin   of    Exogamy    |    By    the    late

8vo.     Pp.  xiv,  605. 

These  researches  relate  to  the  marriage  laws  of  the  natives  of  the  Pacific 
Islands,  Australia,  America,  and  Africa  ;  kinship,  totemism,  genealogies,  in- 
fanticide, and  similar  matters.  Sketches  the  structure  of  native  society  in 
New  Zealand,  and  considers  that  the  marriage  custom  there  has  always 
been  exogamous.

Robley,  General.     Moko ;  or  |  Maori  Tattooing  |  By  |  Major-General  Robley  | 
With    180  illustrations  from  drawings   by    the   |   author   and   from  photo- 
graphs I  London  :  Chapman  and  Hall,  Ltd.  |  1896. 

Large  4to.     Pp.  xxi,  216.     180  illustrations. 

Major  Robley  served  in  the  New  Zealand  war  in  1864-66,  and,  being  an 
accomplished  artist,  he  commenced  to  depict  these  representations  of  a  now 
neglected  art.  A  full  history  and  the  methods  of  tatu  are  given,  with  scores 
of  patterns  as  examples. 

It  is  probable  that  a  2nd  ed.  will  be  issued  in  1909,  with  very  considerable 
additions.

4to.  6  vols.  The  last  issued  in  1907.  Vol.  i  :  Wellington  ;  pp.  xxvii,

A  valuable  pictorial,  biographical,  and  historical  record.  The  illustra- 
tions are  by  the  hundred,  and  include  portraits  of  settlers  —  celebrated, 
notorious,  and  neither. 


Bgerton,   H.    E.      A   Short    History   of   |   British   Colonial   |   Policy  [    By   | 

Hugh   Edward   Egerton,   M.A.    |    Barrister-at-Law  | |    Methuen   &   Co. 

1  36  Essex  St.,  W.C.  1  London  |  1897. 

8vo.     Pp.  XV,  503. 

Traces  it  from  the  sixteenth  century  ;  thence  to  the  period  of  trade 
ascendancy,  of  systematic  colonisation  and  Responsible  Government,  and  of 
growing  indifferentism  between  the  Mother -country  and  the  colonies  ;  and 
thence  to  the  present  sentiment  of  Greater  Britain  and  Imperialism.  Has  an 
excellent  bibliograph}'^  of  works  relative  to  the  subject,  and  a  list  of  Secre- 
taries of  State  for  the  Colonies  from  1702.

Horsburgh,  Publisher,  George  St.  I  MDCCCXCVII. 

Sm.  8vo.     Pp.  vii,  232.     Illustrations  and  portrait. 
Descriptive,  heroic,  humorous,  ballad,  and  elegiac. 


Hocken.  Dr.    T.    M.      Old    Maori    Drawings    by    Tui    (Tooj)    and    Teeterree 
(Titiri). 

An  account  of  these  and  of  the  chiefs  themselves,  who  visited  England 
in  1818,  is  given  in  the  New  Zealand  Herald,  Auckland,  10th  April,  1897. 
The  drawings  are  now  deposited  in  the  Free  Public  Library,  Auckland. 
Small  oil  portraits  of  these  chiefs  were  painted  by  Mr.  Charles  Barry,  of 
London,  in  1818,  for  the  Church  Missionary  Society,  and  were  presented  to 
the  New  Zealand  Government  by  the  committee  in  November,  1906.  For 
three-quarters  of  a  century  these  remained  at  the  Church  Missionary  House 
imidentified.

Oolenso.  Rev.  W.  A  |  Maori-English  Lexicon  :  |  being  a  |  Comprehensive 
Dictionary  |  of  the  |  New  Zealand  Tongue  ;  |  including  |  mythical,  mytho- 
logical, "  taboo  "  or  sacred,  genealogical,  proverbial,  poetical,  |  tropological, 
sacerdotal,  incantatory,  natural-history,  idiomatical,  abbreviated,  |  tribal, 
and  other  names  and  terms  of  and  allusions  to  persons,  |  things,  acts,  and 
places  in  ancient  times  ;  |  also,  |  showing  their  affinities  with  cognate  Poly- 
nesian dialects   and  foreign  languages  ;  |  with  copious  pure  Maori  examples. 

I  Part  I.— Maori-English.  |  By  the  |  Rev.  W.  Colenso,  F.R.S.,  and  F.L.S.  ; 
&c.  I  Wellington.  |  By    Authority :  |  John    Mackay,     Government     Printer, 

I  1898. 

Roy.  8vo.     Pp.  xi,  111  ;    English-Maori  (Part  ii),  and  addendum,  pp.  21. 

Proceeds  as  far  as  the  word  "  Angutu."  The  English-Maori  part  gives 
less  than  100  disconnected  examples.  This  specimen  was  the  sole  one  issued, 
excepting  a  few  pages  in  G.-6,  Vol.  ii,  of  the  Appendix  to  the  House  of  Re- 
presentatives, 1880,  and  again  in  G.-2,  Vol.  ii,  1882. 

The  work  was  commenced  in  1865,  under  Government  auspices,  but 
was  resultless,  perhaps  owing  to  the  ponderous  method  adopted,,  which 
evidently  it  was  impossible  to  carry  out.  Hence  there  was  no  progress,  but 
much  strife,  of  which  the  preface  and  addendum  give  some  account. 


Garnett,  Dr.  Richard.  Edward  Gibbon  |  Wakefield  |  the  Colonization  of 
South  Australia  |  and  New  Zealand  |  By  |  R.  Garnett,  C.B.,  LL.D.  j  With 
Photogravure  Frontispiece  and  Maps  |  London  |  T.  Fisher  Unwin  |  Pater- 
noster Square  |  MDCCCXCVIII. 

8vo.     Pp.  xxvii,  386. 

A  fiiily  complete  biography  of  a  genius,  great  Empire- builder,  and 
coloniser  on  a  system  of  his  own  devising  and  known  by  his  name. 

Wakefield,  who  was  born  in  1796,  died  at  Wellington  on  the  16th  May, 
1862,  in  chagrin,  ill  health,  and  solitude,  after  a  life  of  conspicuous  activity. 


Hocken,   Dr.    T.    M.      Contributions   |   to   |   the    Early    History   |  of  |  New 
Zealand  |  [Settlement  of   Otago]  |  By  |  Thomas  Morland  Hccken,   M.R.C.S. 

Eng.,    F.L.S.,  I  Member    of    the    Otago    University    Council.  | |  With 

Portraits  and  other  Illustrations  |  London  |  Sampson  Low,  Marston  &  Co. 
I  &c.  I  1898. 

8vo.     Pp.  XV,  342.     7  appendices. 

Some  preliminary  and  concurrent  history  of  the  New  Zealand  Com- 
pany from  1839,  and  a  sketch  of  the  Middle  Island,  with  its  old  whalers, 
and  first  missionary,  Mr.  Watkin ;  struggles  of  early  settlement.  Brings 
the  history  down  to  the  time  when  gold-discov  ries  and  an  influx  of  popula- 
tion changed  the  old  life.  The  diary  and  journal  of  Mr.  Tuckett  and  of 
Dr.  (Sir  David)  Monro  in  1844  ;  official  correspondence  of  Colonel  Wakefield 
and  the  New  Zealand  Company  in  1844  ;  list  of  vessels  and  emigrants  from 
1847  to  1850;  and  a  precis  of  principal  events  from  1840  to  1897.  Por- 
traits of  principal  leaders  of  the  settlement,  and  several  very  early  views.

Morris,  Professor  E.  E.  Austral  English  |  a  Dictionary  of  |  Australasian 
Words  I  Phrases  and  Usages  |  With  those  Aboriginal- Australian  and  Maori 
Words  which  |  have  become  incorporated  in  the  language  and  the  |  com- 
moner Scientific  Words  that  have  had   |   their  origin  in  Australasia   |    By 

I   Edward  E.   Morris   |   M.A.,   Oxon.    |    Professor  of  English,  French,  and

London   |    Macmillan  and  Co.  Ltd.   |   New  York :    The  Macmillan  Company 

1  1898. 

8vo.     Pp.  xxiv,  525.     Double  columns. 

By  no  means  a  dictionary  of  slang,  but  a  description  of  the  new  words 
and  the  new  uses  of  old  words  developed  amongst  colonists,  whose  surround- 
ings are  new.  Gives  their  origin  and  derivation,  accompanied  by  examples 
taken  from  well-known  writers.  Many  such  words  and  phrases  are  peculiar 
to  New  Zealand.

Smith,   S.  p.      Hawaiki:  |  the  Whence  of  the  Maori;  |  with  a  Sketch  of  Poly- 
nesian History ;  j  being  an  Introduction  to  the  Native  |  History  of  Rarotonga. 
I  He  ahunga  mai,  &c.  I  By  S.  Percy  Smith,  F.R.G.S.  ]  (From  the  Journal  of 
the  Polynesian  Society.)  |  Whitcombe  &  Tombs  Ltd.  |  &c.  |  1898. 

Roy.  8vo.     Pp.  128. 

A  scholarly  examination  of  the  question,  with  an  account  of  the  author's 
researches  at  Rarotonga,  his  conclusion  being  that  the  last  Maori  migration 
(nearly  600  years  ago)  was  from  Tahiti,  with  a  prolonged  interval  by  the  way 
at  Rarotonga.  There  were  previous  migrations,  and  it  seems  probable  that 
earlier  still  the  Polynesian  race  originated  in  the  Gangetic  basin,  at  intervals 
migrating  eastward. 

2nd  ed.  in  1904  [q.v.). 

Published  originally  in  Vols,  vii  and  viii  of  the  "  Polynesian  Journal." 


Smith,  S.  p.  The  Peopling  of  the  North  :  |  Notes  on  the  |  Ancient  Maori

Society.  |  Whitcombe  &  Tombs  Ltd.  |  Wellington,  &c.  |  1898. 

Roy.  8vo.     Pp.  108. 

The  north  of  New  Zealand  was  first  peopled  by  the  Polynesian,  but  of 
this  migration  little  is  known.  Many  traditions  and  much  interesting  old 
history  are  giver.

How,  F.  D.  Bishop  John  Selwyn  |  A  Memoir  ]  By  |  F.  D.  How  [  Author 
of  I  "  Bishop  Walsham  How :  a  Memoir  "  |  etc.  |  London  |  Isbister  &  Co. 
Ltd.,  &c.  1  1899. 

8vo.     Pp.  X,  268.     Portrait. 

Life  at  Norfolk  Island  and  amongst  the  islands ;  visit  to  England  and 
return  to  duties  in  the  islands ;  missionary  duties  there. 

John  Selwyn  was  the  younger  son  of  Bishop  G.  A.  Selwyn,  and  was  born 
at  the  Waimate,  Bay  of  Islands,  in  May,  1844.  He  devoted  himself  in  1873 
to  the  Melanesian  mission,  and  was  consecrated  Bishop  of  Melanesia  in  1877 
at  Nelson.  He  subsequently  paid  a  visit  to  England,  returning  after  some 
time  to  his  duties  amongst  the  islands,  but,  his  health  breaking  down,  he  was 
again  compelled  to  return  home.  He  was  appointed  master  of  Selwyn  College 
in  1893,  and  died  at  Pau  in  February,  1898,  set.  53.

Arnold,  T.      Passages   in  a  |   Wandering  Life   |   By  |   Thomas  Arnold,  M.A. 
I  of    Univ.    Coll.,    Oxford  :    Fellow  of  the  Royal  University  of   Ireland  j 
London  |  Edward  Arnold  1  37,  Bedford  St.  [  1900. 

8vo.     Pp.  ix,  268.     Portrait. 

Reminiscences  of  Domett,  Weld,  Dillon  Bell,  Major  Richmond,  Colonel 
Wakefield,  and  the  early  life  of  the  colonists. 

The  writer  was  a  son  of  Dr.  Arnold,  of  Rugby,  who  was  a  New  Zealand 
land  purchaser.  He  came  to  Otago  in  the  "  John  Wickliffe  "  with  the  first 
of  the  Scotch  settlement  in  1848,  and  describes  his  fellow- passengers.  He 
remained  eighteen  months  in  the  colony,  chiefly  at  Wellington  and  Nelson. 
He  finally  settled  in  Tasmania.

Elephant  fol.     Pp.  31  (Latin  description  of  the  plates).     100  plates.

final  title-page,  introduction,  &c.  The  printer  has  blundered  over  the  pagi- 
nation. 

These  volumes  contain  the  reproductions  made,  by  order  of  the  Trustees 
of  the  British  Museum,  from  the  plates,  engraved  at  Sir  Joseph  Banks's  ex- 
pense, of  the  beautiful  drawings  of  plants  made  during  Cook's  First  Voyage. 
The  descriptions  are  printed  verbatim  from  the  original  manuscripts,  and  to 
them  Mr.  Britten  has  added  such  notes  and  an  introduction  as  add  to  the 
value  and  interest  of  this  important  work,  and  bring  it  up  to  the  requirements 
of  present  botanical  knowledge.

Outcome  of  a  discussion  in  the  Otago  Daily  Times  with  Bishop  Nevill. 
From  the  Rev.  J.  Watkin's  arrival  in  1840,  with  an  account  of  Tamihana 
te  Rauparaha.     Extracts  from  Bishop  Selwyn.     Baptismal -register  epitome. 


Gravelius,  Professor  Dr.  H.     F.  von  Bellingshausen's  |  Forschungsfahrten  |  im 
Siidlichen    Eismeer   |    1819-1821.    |   Auf    Grund    des    russischen    Original- 
werks  |  herausgegeben  vom  |  Verein  fiir  Erdkunde  zu  Dresden.  |  Leipzig  | 
Verlag  von  S.  Hirzel  |  1902. 

8vo.     Pp.  viii,  203. 

This  is  an  abridged  translation  into  German  by  Professor  Dr.  H. 
Gravelius  from  the  Russian  of  Bellingshausen's  Voyage  to  the  South  Frozen 
Sea  in  1819-21.  The  original  was  published  at  St.  Petersburg  in  1831,  in 
2  vols.  4to,  and  is  as  rare  as  it  is  important.  After  a  stay  at  Sydney,  the 
expedition  left  on  the  8th  May,  1820,  for  New  Zealand,  entering  Queen 
Charlotte's  Sound  via  the  west  coast,  where  they  remained  for  nearly  a 
fortnight,  thence  sailing  for  Tahiti.  Returning  to  Sydney,  they  proceeded 
south  on  the  31st  October,  sighting  Macquarie  Island  on  the  17th  November, 
remaining  there  three  days  making  scientific  observations  and  collections. 
Sailing  still  south,  they  advanced  to  the  ice-wall  and  discovered  land,  their 
furthest  latitude  being  69**  S.  Interesting  references  to  Sydney,  the  Rev.  S. 
Marsden,  the  stay  at  New  Zealand,  and  a  good  account  of  Macquarie  Island 
and  the  fisheries.  A  translation  of  the  last  into  English  is  in  McNab's 
"  Murihuku,"  p.  190,  q.v.,  1907.  A  translation  of  the  whole  of  Gravelius's 
abridgment  is  in  preparation. 


Hadfield,  Bishop.  Maoris  of  By-  |  gone  Days.  ]  By  the  Most  Rev. 
Octavius  Hadfield.  |  Printed  by  H.  W.  Williams,  Te  Rau  Press,  Gisborne, 
1902. 

8vo.     Pp.  24. 

Sketches  of  Matenga  te  Matia,    Te  Rauparaha,  Te   Ruru,  Rev.   Riwai 
te  Ahu ;   building  of  Otaki  Church ;   meeting  of  natives  at  Otaki,  &c.

Irvine,  R.  F.,  and  Alpers.   0.   T.   J.      The   Progress   |  of   New  Zealand  | 
in  the  Century  |  By  |  R.  F.  Irvine,  M.A.,  and  0.   T.   J.   Alpers,  M.A.  |  The

bers, Limited  |  London  and  Edinburgh  |  1902. 

8vo.     Pp.  xvii,  460.     The  Nineteenth  Century  series. 

Traces  the  history  from  the  landing  of  the  Maoris,  through  colonisation, 
the  romance  of  early  settlement,  and  the  wars  between  the  two  races,  to  1870, 
when  the  public-works  policy  effected  a  marvellous  change  in  the  progress 
of  the  country.  An  excellent  account  of  the  great  legislative  changes,  the 
new  democracy,  labour  laws,  widespread  education,  and  other  measures  of 
advancement.

Reeves,   W.    P.      State   Experiments   |  in   |   Australia   and  New   Zealand   | 
By  I  William  Pember  Reeves  |  Author  of  "  The  Long  White  Cloud  "  |  In

A  dispassionate  recital  of  the  democratic  progress  of  the  previous  twenty- 
five  years,  given  in  a  lucid  and  spirited  style,  with  lists  of  authorities.  ,A 
portion  previously  appeared  in  various  magazines  and  other  publications. 
Women's  franchise  ;  federation  ;  land  and  labour  questions  ;  old-age  pen- 
sions ;  liquor  laws ;  the  exclusion  of  aliens ;  and  the  general  progressive 
•    movement.     A  valuable  compendium  on  the  subject.

Clarke,  Rev.  G.  Notes  |  on  |  Early  Life  in  New  Zealand.  |  By  |  George 
Clarke.  |  Printed  for  Private  Circulation.  |  Hobart :  |  J.  Walch  and  Sons,^ 
Printers,  42  Macquarie  St.  |  1903. 

8vo.     Pp.  106.     Portrait  of  author  and  4  photos  of  native  warriors. 

The  writer,  a  son  of  George  Clarke,  the  New  Zealand  missionary  who  was 
appointed  in  1840  Chief  Protector  of  Aborigines,  was  born  at  Parramatta 
on  the  29th  June,  1823,  and  became  Sub-Protector  under  his  father.  He 
assisted,  in  1844,  in  the  purchase  of  the  Otago  Block,  and  was  present 
throughout  Heke's  war  and  during  the  beginning  of  the  Taranaki  war.  In 
1853  he  left  for  Hobart,  and  was  ordained  minister  in  charge  of  the  Inde- 
pendents.    He  is  Chancellor  of  the  University  of  Tasmania. 

Fifty  Years'  Retrospect  of  Church  Life  in  Wanganui. 

8vo.  Pp.  45.  Numerous  portraits  and  illustrations.  Wanganui  Herald 
Newspaper  Company,  Limited,  printers. 

Relates  to  the  Presbyterian  Church,  and  is  contained  in  the  annual 
report  of  St.  Paul's  Presbyterian  Church,  30th  June,  1903.

JM[oleS"WOrth,   Sir   W.      Selected    Speeches  of  ]  Sir   William  Molesworth,   | 
Bart.,  P.O.,  M.P.,  |  on  Questions  relating  to  Colonial  Policy.  |  Edited,  with 
an  Introduction,  by  [  Hugh  Edward  Egerton,  M.A.,  |  author  of    "A  Short

I  John  Murray,  Albemarle  St.,  W.  |  1903. 

8vo.     Pp.  xxvii,  520.     Portrait. 

Important  speeches  connected  with  the  foundation,  progress,  and  re- 
quirements of  the  Australasian  colonies. 

Sir  William  took  a  life-long  interest  in  colonial  reform  and  the  new 
democratic  school  of  politicians.  He  was  a  friend  and  fellow-worker  with 
E.  G.  Wakefield,  Buller,  and  others,  and  aided  the  colonisation  of  New  Zea- 
land and  the  granting  of  self-government  to  the  colonies.  He  died  October 
22nd,  1855. 


H'orton,    Lord.     Imperial   Fellowship  |  of  |   Self -governed    British  |  Colonies  | 
By  I  Lord  Norton  |  Rivingtons  |  34  King  Street,  Covent   Garden  |  London 
I  1903. 

Svo.     Pp.  67.     Dedicated  to  the  Right  Hon.  Joseph  Chamberlain. 

Written  in  a  fine  spirit  of  Imperialism,  pointing  out  the  advantages 
and  necessity  of  patriotic  union  throughout  the  Empire.  Gives  much 
colonising  history  and  an  account  of  the  Society  for  the  Reform  of  Colonial 
Government,  of  which,  as  Mr.  Adderley,  the  writer  was  secretary. 

Lord  Norton  was  one  of  the  principal  promoters  of  New  Zealand  colonisa- 
tion, and  died  in  March,  1905,  set.  90.

8vo.     Pp.  vii,  451.     Illustrations. 

The  writer  has  dressed  up  the  ancient  native  traditions  in  the  garb  of 
modern  fiction,  with  imaginary  conversations — often  in  very  common  ver- 
nacular— descriptive  passages,  and  reflections.  The  opening  chapter,  on 
the  Wharekura,  varied  from  John  White's  description,  is  the  best.  There 
are  four  hitherto- unpublished  legends  collected  by  Mr.  Jury,  of  Greytown.

material  for  a  bibliography  of  Australasian  poetry.

Considerable  additions  to  the  edition  of  1898  {q.v.),  forming  the  most

Smitll,  S.  P.  Wars  I  of  the  Northern  against  the  Southern  |  Tribes  of  New

Tombs,  Ltd.  |  1904. 

Roy.  8vo.     Pp.  245. 

Valuable  contribution  to  old  native  history,  commencing  about  1795, 
with  wars  on  the  borderland  between  Nga-puhi  and  Ngati-whatua,  down  to 
1836.  Extensive  extracts  are  made  from  the  Missionary  Register,  which 
refer  to  the  massacre  of  the  "  Boyd,"  the  Rev.  S.  Marsden,  Hongi,  Patuone, 
and  other  celebrated  warriors. 

Stone      H.      The    I    Timbers    of    Commerce    |    and    their    Identification    |   By 
j    Herbert    Stone,     F.L.S.,    F.R.C.I.    |   Illustrated    with     186    photo-micro- 
graphs I  prepared     by  |  Arthur    Deane  |  London   |  William     Rider     &    Son^ 
Ltd.  I  Manchester  House,  164  Aldersgate  St.,  &c.  |  1904. 

8vo.     Pp.  xxxviii,  310. 

Includes  description  of  many  New  Zealand  timbers — Plagianthus,  Ho- 
heria  (now  Gaya),  Elseocarpus,  Sophora,  kamahi,  rata,  maire,  puriri,  birch, 
beech,  and  the  various  pines.  Gives  the  physical  characters,  uses,  authorities, 
botanical  and  native  names,  and  microscopic  appearances  of  the  various 
woods.     A  valuable  book  of  reference. 


Terry,   L.      The    Shadow.    |    By    |    Lionel     Terry.    |    (Author's    Copyright.)    [ 
A  Prayer.  |  When  the  Great  Gold  God  advancing,  &c.  |  Auckland.  |  Wilson 
and  Horton,  Printers,  Queen  and  Wyndham  Streets.  ]  1904. 

8vo.  Pp.  30.  Black- edged  wrapper,  with  design  of  a  ferocious  Tartar 
face,  and  uplifted  arm  brandishing  a  scimitar,  emerging  from  a  black  cloud, 
and  threatening  Great  Britain,  as  typified  by  a  portion  of  St.  Paul's.  Dedi- 
cated "  To  my  Brother  Britons." 

A  fierce,  stirring  invective,  in  prose  and  verse,  against  the  degeneracy  of 
the  British  policy,  as  evidenced  by  avarice,  capitalism,  selfishness,  and  alien 
labour,  wdth  a  prophecy  of  results.  The  writer,  a  monomaniac,  was  after- 
wards tried  for  shooting  and  kiPing  an  unoffending  Chinaman,  and  was  com- 
mitted to  a  lunatic  asylum.

Maori-Polynesian  Comparative  Dictionary,"  "  A  Dictionary  of  the  Pau-

An  admirably  condensed  and  comprehensive  account  of  native  customs 
and  characteristics,  implements,  weapons,  food,  dress,  witchcraft,  and  cos- 
mogony, brought  together  from  numerous  sources,  and  supplying  a  much- 
felt  want.     The  native  names  are  everywhere  given.

The  standard  work  on  New  Zealand  botany,  following  on  the  general 
plan  adopted  in  Sir  J.  L.  Hooker's  "  Handbook,"  which  it  supersedes.  In- 
cludes the  outlying  islands.  History  of  botanical  discovery ;  a  synoptical 
key  of  the  orders ;  list  of  plants  naturalised,  arranged  in  their  natural 
orders ;  list  of  Maori  names  ;  and  glossary.  A  work  of  faithful  and  exten- 
sive labour.

Svo.     Pp.  X,  327.     Ma; . 

The  author  visited  Australia  and  New  Zealand  under  a  commission  from

creeds.  He  parallels  the  movement  in  the  two  countries,  apparently  pro- 
nouncing in  favour  of  that  in  Australia.

Gives  a  history  of  the  Geological  Survey  of  New  Zealand  under  the 
Directorship  of  Sir  J.  Hector,  from  1869  to  1903,  when  it  was  remodelled

of  the  New  Zealand  Institute  and  its  Transactions  ;  present  status  of  the 
Colonial  Museum ;  a  list  of  the  marine  MoUusca  of  New  Zealand,  with  short 
notices  of  the  contributors ;  an  account  of  New  Zealand  tokens  ;  and  photo- 
graphs of  some  fine  Maori  carvings.

Lord  Durham  was  associated  with  the  early  fortunes  of  the  New  Zealand 
Company,  was  its  first  gov^ernor,  and  was  thus  in  close  communication  with 
E.  G.  Wakefield  upon  its  affairs,  as  well  as  those  of  Canada.  He  died  in  July,. 
1840.     ( F*(/f  "  Durham,"  1839).

Stephen,  Miss  C.  E. — continued. 

8vo.     Pp.  X,  298;   4  (w.j?.),  note.     Portrait. 

Whilst  these  epistolary  fragments  throw  some  light  on  Mr.  Stephen's 
personality  as  the  all-powerful  Under-Secretary,  they  throw  none  on  his 
attitude  towards  the  New  Zealand  Company,  which  was  that  of  a  bitter 
and  uncompromising  adversary.  He  was  Under-Secretary  from  1836  to  1847, 
and  died  in  1859.

Wellington.     1907. 

8vo.  Pp.  380.  A  most  complete  and  valuable  work.  24  folded  plana 
of  New  Zealand  harbours  and  wharves ;  3  coloured  maps  of  New  Zealand, 
illustrating  the  different  kinds  of  coast  lighthouses,  and  railway  and  steamer 
communication ;  inset  with  international  signal  code  flags  and  diagrams  of 
semaphore  and  flag  signals ;  information  regarding  fires  on  ships,  prevention 
of  collisions,  and  courses  given  for  distant  sailing.  Issued  annually  since  the 
first  number,  in  1902.  Since  the  edition  of  1905  the  azimuth  tables  have  been 
enlarged  and  issued  separately.

Pearce,   T.  D.,  and  Pulton,  Dr.     Otago  High  School  |  Old  Boys'  Register.

Early  history  of  the  school  and  of  contemporary  private  ones.  List  of

Price-list  |  of  |  Public  Acts  |  and  |  Official  Publications  [  on  sale  at  the  j  Go- 
vernment Stationery  Office,  |  Wellington,  N.Z.  |  Wellington.  |  By  autho- 
rity :   John  Mackay,  Government  Printer.  |  1907. 

8vo.     Pp.  32. 

Such  of  the  official  publications  which  appeared  to  be  of  special  interest 
and  importance  have  been  noted  throughout  this  bibliography  at  length. 
Others  which  are  chiefly  of  a  purely  governmental  character  have  not  always 
been  noted :  such  are  the  volumes  of  Statistics,  Statutes,  Parliamentary 
Debates,  Parliamentary  Papers,  various  Acts,  regulations,  and  rules,  and 
others  of  a  similar  class,  all  of  which  will  be  found  in  the  above  list.  It 
should  be  mentioned  that  the  Statistics  of  the  colony  first  appeared  in  1858, 
in  a  volume  for  the  years  1853  to  1856.  Previous  partial  ones  are  noted 
in  this  bibliography.  The  Parliamentary  Debates  from  1854  to  1866  were  com- 
piled by  Mr.  Maurice  FitzGerald,  who  gathered  them  from  newspapers  and 
original  sources,  and  they  were  published  in  1885-87.  The  Blue-books,  or 
Appendices  to  the  Votes  and  Proceedings  of  the  House  of  Representatives 
and  to  the  Legislative  Council,  have  been  published  from  the  first  session  in 
1854.

^  The  first  of  what  will  evidently  be  a  valuable  series  of  records,  and  framed 

on  the  lines  of  the  "  Historical  Records  of  New  South  Wales  "  {vide  "  Bladen," 
1893).  Begins  with  a  letter,  dated  1770,  from  Cook  after  his  discovery  of  New 
Zealand,  and  ends  with  the  correspondence  of  August,  1839,  regarding  Cap- 
tain Hobson's  appointment  as  Consul  of  New  Zealand.  Much  has  been 
properly  taken  from  the  New  South  Wales  Records,  as  being  common  to  both 
countries.     Numerous  excellent  footnotes.

Mr.  Adderley  was  closely  connected  with  colonial  reform,  the  New  Zealand

Cleary,  Rev  Dr.  H.  W.  An  Impeached  Nation.  |  Being  a  Study  of  Irish

8vo.     Pp.  viii,  417. 

Defends  the  Irish  people  from  the  charges  under  which  they  lie,  and 
contends  that  the  "  outrages "  paraded  in  the  newspapers  are  not  only 
exaggerated,  but  are  the  inevitable  outcome  of  injustice  and  oppression.

Smith,  S.  P. — contimied. 

Numerous  maps  and  illustrations. 

The  west  coast  of  the  North  Island,  extending  from  Kawhia  to  Welling-

the  number  for  September,  1907.

Rossignol,  Professor  of  Economics  in  the  Denver  University,  author  of 
"  Orthodox  Soeialism,"  &o. 

Treats  upon  wages  and  the  cost  of  living,  wealth  and  its  distribution, 
rating  on  unimproved  values,  the  labour  movement,  the  land  question, 
municipal  enterprise.  State  fire  insurance,  and  compulsory  arbitration.

McDonnell,  Lieut.  Extracts  |  from  |  Mr.  McDonnell's  MS.  Journal,  j  con- 
taining I  Observations  |  on  New  Zealand.  |  London  :  (  Printed  by  |  James

Addenda.  489 


McDonnell,  Lieut. — continued. 

8vo.     Pp.  24. 

Originally  read  before  the  Royal  Geographical  Society,  10th  March, 
1834. 

Lieut.  McDonnell  was  a  settler  on  the  Hokianga,  and  was  appointed 
Assistant-Resident  at  New  Zealand  under  Mr.  Busby.  He  published  a  very 
incorrect  chart  of  that  river,  now  of  great  rarity,  whereby  one  or  two  vessels 
were  wrecked. 


1842. 

IHalse,  William  and  Henry.     Letter  |  from  |  William  and  Henry  Halse,  |  dated 
on  board  the  "  Amelia  Thompson,"  |  off  the  coast  of  |  New  Zealand,  15th 
July,  1841.  I  London  :  |  Printed  by  T.  Brettell,  Rupert  Street,  Haymarket.  | 
1842. 

8vo.     Pp.  30. 

A  journal  of  the  voyage,  kept  by  two  well-known  New  Plymouth  settlers. 


1846. 

[Dix,  William  D.]  Wreck  of  the  Glide  |  with  an  Account  of  |  Life  and  Manners 
I  at  the  I  Fiji  Islands.  |  Boston  :  |  William  D.  Ticknor  &  Co.  |  No.  135 
Washington  Street.  |  1846. 

12mo.     Pp.  122. 

The  "  Glide  "  was  owned  by  Joseph  Peabody,  and  the  captain  was 
Henry  Archer.  Sailing  from  Salem,  she  touched  at  the  Cape  of  Good  Hope, 
and  reached  the  Bay  of  Islands  on  the  17th  September,  1829.  She  remained 
there  a  week  and  shipped  six  Maoris,  then  sailing  for  the  Friendly  Islands, 
Fiji,  Manila,  Samoa,  &c.,  and  meeting  Captain  Morrell,  of  the  "  Antarctic," 
at  the  Sandwich  Islands. 


1852. 

[Kennedy,  A.]  Notes  |  of  a  |  Short  Tour  into  the  Interior  |  of  the  |  Northern 
Colony  of  New  Zealand,  |  in  March  and  April,  1851.  |  Auckland  :  |  Printed 
by  Williamson  and  Wilson,  |  1852. 

8vo.     Pp.  30. 

An  interesting  canoe  journey  up  the  Waikato  and  Waipa  Rivers  as  far 
as  Otawhao,  and  returning  by  the  Horotiu  to  Waiuku  and  Onehunga. 

[Martin,  W.,  C.J.,  and  Chapman,  H.  S.,  J.]  Supreme  Court  Procedure 
Commission.  |  First  Report  |  of  the  |  Commissioners  ]  appointed  by  (  His 
Excellency  the  Governor-in-Chief,  |  &c.  &c.  &c.  |  to  inquire  into  and  report 
upon  I  a  System  of  Procedure  |  suited  to  |  the  Supreme  Court  of  New  Zea- 
land. I  Ordered  by  His  Excellency  to  be  printed  |  19th  January,  1852.  |  New 
Zealand  :  |  Printed  by  R.  Stokes,  at  the  office  of  the  "  New  Zealand  j  Spec- 
tator," Wellington.  ]  1852. 

8vo.     Pp.  iv,  139,  and  errata  (p.  1).     Two  appendices. 
An  important  document,  recommending  certain  forms  of  pleading  and 
procedure  for  the  New  Zealand  Supreme  Court.

yet  his  right  was  denied,  and  he  was  succeeded  by  Bishop  Nevill.

An  interesting  and  complicated  native-land  case  in  which  Sir  Walter 
Buller  was  involved.  He  was  summoned  before  the  bar  of  the  House,  a 
Royal  Commission  was  set  up,  lasting  for  many  weeks,  and  there  was  much 
newspaper  discussion.  Sir  Walter  j)ublished  in  1896  his  address  to  the 
Commission  as  counsel  for  Major  Kemp  (Te  Rangihiwinui)  and  the  Muaupoko 
Tribe  in  an  8vo  pamphlet  of  33  pages,  followed  by  another  containing  his 
"  Objections  to  the  Report  of  the  Commission."  An  opposite  statement 
of  the  case  is  given  at  great  length  in  the  Manawatu  Farmer  and  Horowhenua 
County  Chronicle  of  the  4th  March,  1896,  stated  to  be  written  by  Sir  William

MAORI   BIBLIOGRAPHY. 


Great  difficulty  attends  the  classification  and  bibliography  of  the 
Maori  section. 

Many  pubhcations  were  issued  without  title,  date,  or  imprint ; 
leaflets  of  different  size  and  date  and  on  diverse  subjects  were  often 
bound  together,  and  sometimes  even  those  on  the  same  subject  were 
printed  in  parts  and  at  distant  intervals. 

The  best  method  of  grouping  seems  to  the  author  to  begin  with 
Scripture  translation  and  rehgious  matter  generally,  as  being  the 
first  in  point  of  time  ;  and  then  to  follow  with  educational  issues, 
general  literature,  and  much  that  is  difficult  to  define.  Overlap  is 
unavoidable,  and  must  often  necessitate  the  student's  scrutiny. 

A  method  of  identification  resorted  to  in  the  case  of  many  tiresome 
publications  is  the  use  of  millimetric  measurement  (/x).  This  is  applied 
solely  to  the  area  of  the  printed  matter — the  perpendicular  measure 
including  the  page-number,  and  the  horizontal  a  complete-line  length. 

As  also  of  some  assistance,  the  author  suggests  the  perusal  of  his 
paper  on  "  Some  Account  of  the  Beginnings  of  Literature  in  New  Zea- 
land "  (Trans.  N.Z.  Inst.,  Vol.  xxxiii,  p.  472),  and  also  a  paper  by  him 
presented  to  the  Maori  Congress  in  July,  1908  (and  printed  in  their 
Proceedings),  on  "  The  Maori  Newspaper  Literature  of  New  Zealand." 

From  the  first,  and  especially  after  the  date  of  colonisation,  earnest 
efforts  were  made  to  cultivate  in  the  native  a  taste  for  reading  and 
knowledge. 

For  the  advantage  of  those  unacquainted  with  the  Maori  tongue 
EngHsh  translations  are  appended  to  most  of  the  original  titles. 

A. list  hkely  to  be  useful  is  given  of  the  Maorified  names,  with  their 
English  equivalents,  of  places  of  pubhcation.

The  flat  land  inland  from  Napier.

Hobart  Town. 

England.

Napier. 

New  Zealand. 

Port  Nicholson,  or  Wellington. 


Purewa  is  at  the  head  of  St.  John's  College  Creek,  and  less  than  a  mile  below 
the  college  (Te  Kareti).  In  1846,  when  Bishop  Selwyn  removed  his 
press  and  a  number  of  scholars  from  the  Waimate,  and  during  the  erec- 
tion of  the  college,  he  fixed  his  temporary  quarters  at  Purewa,  where 
supplies  were  readily  landed  at  high  water,  and  where  much  of  his  early 
printing  was  done. 

Ranana        . .  . .  . .  . .     London. 

Rione  . .  . .  . .  . .     Lyons. 

Te  Kareti    . .  . .  . .  . .     The  College  (St.  John's  College). 

Werengitana,  or  Weretana       . .  . .     Wellington. 

Hiperu  =  Hebrew ;  Kariki  =  Greek.

The  Epistles  to  the  Ephesians  and  to  the  Philippians. 

The  first-fruits  of  the  Church  Missionary  press  which  was  landed  at 
Paihia  on  the  30th  December,  1834.  Printed  by  William  Colenso,  who

of  the  press,  but  speedily  relinquished  his  duties  for  those  of  a  catechist. 
Adopting  Baptist  views  he  left  for  Hobart  in  1842,  assuming  charge  of 
the  Baptist  Church  there.  {Vide  Wade,  p.  107.)  In  1842  John  Telford  was 
sent  out  as  printer,  superseding  Colenso,  who  was  ordained  deacon  in  1844.

In  1840  portions  were  bound  up  with  the  title-page,  "  Ko  tetahi  wa- 
hanga  |  o  te  Kawenata  Hou  |  o  |  to  tatou  Ariki  te  kai  wakaora  |  a  Ihu 
Karaiti.  |  He  mea  wakamaori  i  te  reo  Kariki.  |  Paihia  :  |  He  mea  ta  i,  &c.  | 
1840."  8vo.  Contains  Maka,  pp.  47-73  ;  Ruka,  74-120  ;  Koriniti,  221-52  ; 
Karatia,  253-59;  Epeha,  260-66  ;  Piripai,  267-71;  and  Korohe,  272-76. 
Despite  the  title-page,  these  portions  of  the  New  Testament  are  uniform 
with  the  1837  ed.  There  is  a  trifling  difference  in  the  disposition  of  the 
type  in  Epeha.  Probably  this  selection  was  made  up  from  odd  remnants 
and  so  utilised  for  distribution  amongst  the  natives.

Bishop  Broughton's  sermon  or  address  to  the  natives  at  the  first  con- 
firmation,, in  New  Zealand.  A  translation  of  this  is  in  the  Missionary 
Register  for  December,  1839,  p.  551 ;  also  in  "  Missions  of  the  Church  Mis- 
sionary Society,"  &e.,  1840  {vide  p.  82).  It  was  translated  by  the  Rev.  W. 
Williams. 

The  Bishop  arrived  at  the  Bay  of  Islands  on  the  21st  December,  1838, 
in  H.M.S.  "  Pelorus,"  Captain  Harding,  accompanied  by  the  Rev.  0.  Had- 
field.  He  consecrated  a  burial-ground,  confirmed  44  natives  and  20  whites 
(mostly  children  of  missionaries),  and  on  the  6th  January  ordained  Mr.  Had- 
field  to  priest's  orders.  On  the  7th  he  sailed  for  the  Thames,  baptised 
three  of  Mr.  Fairburn's  children,  and  left  for  Norfolk  Island  on  the  12th.

1847. 

[Probationary  copies  (so  called)  of  the  following  portions  of  the  New  Testa-^ 
ment  Scripture  were  distributed  to  the  missionaries,  who  were  expected  to  revise- 
and  perfect  the  translation  in  their  converse  with  the  natives.  There  is  no- 
indication  of  date  or  place  of  printing,  but  these  would  probably  be  about 
1846-47,  at  St.  John's  College,  Tamaki.  They  were  8vo.  ;  ^t  160  x  60  ;  blank 
interleaved ;    and   bound   in   linen.      On   the  outside   a  label   was   pasted   with 

"  St. .      Probationary  Copy.      To  be  returned  to before ."      And 

on  the  inside,  "  Resolutions  passed  at  the  Central  Committee  of  the  Church 
Mission,  held  at  St.  John's  College,  September  14th,  1847. — (1.)  That  the- 
Bishop,  the  Rev.  G.  A.  Kissling,  and  the  Rev.  R.  Maunsell  be  a  sub-committee 
for  managing  the  details  of  the  Printing  Department.  (2.)  Probationary  copies 
of  all  new  translations  to  be  printed  in  sufficient  numbers  to  allow  of  copies 
being  sent  to  every  member  of  the  Church  Mission  and  of  the  Wesleyan  Mission. 
(3.)  That  from  the  time  of  the  printing  of  any  probationary  copy  of  a  trans- 
lation of  any  portion  of  the  Holy  Scriptures  a  space  of  twelve  months  be 
allowed  to  enable  the  Archdeacons  and  Rev.  Mr.  Maunsell  to  collect  the 
suggestions  of  the  members  of  both  missions,  and  that  at  the  expiration  of 
that  time  the  sub-syndicate,  on  application  of  the  Venerable  Archdeacon 
W.  Williams  and  Rev.  Mr.  Maunsell  conjointly,  be  empowered  to  print  a 
sufficient  number  of  copies  for  the  supply  of  the  two  missions.  As  a  general 
rule,  all  books  are  to  be  issued  in  proportion  to  the  scale  of  population  a& 
determined  by  census.  The  members  of  the  syndicate  most  earnestly  request 
the  members  of  both  missions  to  give  full  effect  to  the  above  resolutions  by 
collecting  and  forwarding  to  them  all  such  remarks  and  suggestions  as  may 
tend  to  make  the  version  of  the  New  Zealand  Bible  as  correct  as  possible. — 
(Signed)  G.  A.  New  Zealand,  G.  A.  Kisslestg,  R.  Maunsell." 

The  following  formed  the  probationary  copies  : — 
Ko  te  rongo  pai  a  Matiu  [Matthew].     Pp.  5-67. 
Ko  te  rongo  pai  a  Maka  [Mark].     Pp.  68-106. 
Ko  te  rongo  pai  a  Ruka  [Luke].     Pp.  107-73. 
Ko  te  rongo  pai  a  Hoani  [John].     Pp.  175-226. 
Ko  nga  Mahi  o  nga  Apotoro  [Acts  of  the  Apostles].     Pp.  227-289.] 


THE  OLD  TESTAMENT  (Te  Kawenata  Tawhito). 

[The  definite  translation  of  the  Old  Testament  was  commenced  in  1839,. 
the  Rev.  Robert  Maunsell  being  the  chief  translator  and  authority.  Owing 
to  the  frequent  absence  of  title-page  and  date,  and  irregular  sequence  in  the 
translation,  the  collation  is  very  difficult.  But  little  work  was  done  at  Paihia, 
and  this  was  covered  by  a  few  chapters  from  Exodus,  Deuteronomy,  Joshua, 
and  Isaiah  by  Mr.  Maunsell,  and  a  few  from  Daniel  and  Jonah  by  Mr.  Colenso. 
In  1845  the  Church  Missionary  press  was  removed  to  Purewa,  and  thence  to 
St.  John's  College,  and  the  work  proceeded  uninterruptedly.  Every  few 
chapters,  or  a  complete  book,  were  sewn  or  jacketed  in  brown-paper  wrappers,. 
specimens  of  which  are  still  occasionally  seen.  \^Tien  these  were  numerous 
enough  they  were  roughly  bound  and  interleaved  for  distribution  and  emen- 
dation. Three  of  such  sets  made  up  the  completed  Old  Testament,  and  were 
forwarded  to  London  for  reprint  by  the  British  and  Foreign  Bible  Society,  the 
first  section  appearing  in  1848.  The  completed  and  perfect  Bible  (Te  Paipera 
Tapu)  was  first  issued  at  London  in  1868.  This  introduction  will  simplify  the 
following  details.] 


504  Bibliography  of  New  Zealand  Literature.

This  forms  the  second  instalment  or  volume  of  the  Old  Testament  published

[1853-56.] 

[The  remaining  books  of  the  Old  Testament,  still  without  date  and  title- 
page,  here  numbered  from  27  to  46  (Proverbs  to  Malachi),  were,  as  before,  the 
Rev.  Robert  Maunsell's  translation  almost  entirely.  They  appeared  in  two  parts 
or  volumes,  sm.  8vo,  paginated  from  1  to  576,  and  were  printed  at  St.  John's 
College  in  1856.  Some  were  interleaved  and  distributed  to  secure  further 
correction. 

To  the  first  part — Proverbs  to  Lamentations  (Ko  Whakatauki-Ko  nga  Tangi 
a  Heremaia),  pp.  1  to  329 — the  following  notice  was  prefixed  :  "  The  printing  of 
the  translation  of  the  portion  of  the  Holy  Scriptures  not  yet  published  in  the 
Maori  language  having  proceeded  so  far  from  the  Book  of  Proverbs  to  the  Pro- 
phecies of  Ezekiel,  the  committee  charged  with  the  present  superintendence  of 
the  undertaking  have  resolved  to  distribute  the  portion  now  in  print  amongst 
those  whose  aid  in  the  revision  of  the  translation  is  anticipated,  without  waiting 
for  the  completion  of  the  whole  of  the  Old  Testament.  Thus  the  revision  can  go 
forward  simultaneously  with  the  printing  of  the  remainder,  which  it  is  expected 
will  be  completed  in  a  few  months.  The  concluding  portion,  to  the  end  of  Malachi, 
will,  when  ready,  be  similarly  circulated  ;  and  on  the  receipt  of  the  emendations 
which  may  be  sent  in,  the  plan  as  originally  proposed  will  be  carried  out  by  placing 
the  Rev.  R.  Maunsell's  translation  with  the  suggested  emendations  in  the  hands 
of  a  committee  of  final  revision  to  be  appointed  by  the  recognised  local  authorities 
of  the  two  missionary  societies  acting  conjointly.  It  is  earnestly  requested 
that  the  ministers  and  lay  friends  to  whom  the  books  are  now  forwarded  will 
direct  their  attention  without  delay  to  the  subject ;  and  that  the  books,  with 
such  emendations  of  the  translation  as  they  may  see  fit  to  propose,  shall  be  returned 


Maori.  507 


to  the  Secretary,  at  Auckland,  if  possible,  not  later  than  the  end  of  the  present 
year.  On  behalf  of  the  Maori  Bible  Translation  Committee,  John  B.  Bennett, 
Secretary. — Auckland,  May  1,  1856."]

Note. — In  1840 was  printed"  Kotetahi  wahi  |  o  te  |  Pukapuka  a  Ihaia  | 
a  te  Poropiti."     Sm.  8vo.     Pp.  12,    fx  155  x  80.    On  the  last  page,  "  Heoi 
ano.     No  Paihia  :  He  mea  ta  i  te  perehi  o  nga  raihanere."     Contains  chapters 
49  to  55  of  Isaiah.     By  Rev.  R.  Maunsell.

Pp.  192-318. 

32.  Ko  nga  Tangi  a  Heremaia  [Lamentations  of  Jeremiah]. 
Pp.  318-29. 

[To  the  second  part — Ezekiel  to  Malachi  (Ko  Ehekiere-Ko  Maraki),  pp.  331 
to  576 — the  following  notice  was  prefixed  :  "  The  Maori  Bible  Translation  Com- 
mittee have  now  the  pleasure  of  placing  the  concluding  portion  of  the  Rev.  R. 
Maunsell's  translation  in  the  hands  of  the  missionaries  and  other  friends,  whose 
aid  in  the  revision  of  the  work  is  anticipated.  Upon  the  promptitude  with  which 
they  render  their  assistance,  by  suggesting  any  emendations  which  they  may 
judge  necessary,  must  greatly  depend  the  time  at  which  the  entire  Word  of  God 
can  be  given  in  the  native  language  to  the  aboriginal  population  of  New  Zealand. 
This  solemn  consideration  will,  it  is  confidently  hoped,  stimulate  to  earnest- 
ness and  diligence  in  this  part  of  the  undertaking.  The  31st  of  March,  1857, 
has  been  fixed  on  by  the  committee  as  the  date  at  which  at  the  latest  they  expect 
the  books  now  issued  to  be  retutned.  It  is  hoped,  however,  that  the  portion  of 
the  translation  which  was  put  in  a  siinilar  course  of  circulation  some  months 
since  will  be  returned  much  sooner,  so  as  to  enable  the  committee  of  final  revision 
to  prosecute  their  work  with  the  least  possible  delay.  On  behalf  of  the  com- 
mittee, John  B.  Bennett,  Secretary. — Auckland,  October  24th,  1857."]

piti i  nga  iwi.  |  Ihikiera,  37."  Sm.  8vo.  Pp.  2,  (j.  144  x  76.  No  title-

amongst  the  bones. )

CHURCH  SERVICES,  CATECHISMS,  HYMNS,  ETC. 
Church  of  England  Mission. 

(Chiefly  earliest  selections  from  Scripture,  inclusive  of  the  complete  New  Testa- 
ment— Te  Kawenata  Hou.) 

1839. 

Ko  te  Pukapuka  |  o  nga  Inoinga,  |  me  te  |  Minititanga  [  o  nga  Hakarameta  |  ko 
I  era  tikanga  hoki  o  te  Hahi,  |  ki  te  ritenga  |  o  te  Hahi  o  Ingarani.  |  Paihia  : 
I  he  mea  ta  i  te  perehi  o  nga  mihanere  o  te  |  Hahi  o  Ingarani.  |  1839. 

Sm.  8vo.  Pp.  36.  fx  148  x  85.  Double  columns.  Title  with  black- 
line  border. 

The  first  prayer-book  of  the  New  Zealand  press,  of  which  4,000  copies 
were  printed.  It  contains  the  Morning  and  Evening  Prayer,  the  Litany 
shortened  and  adapted.  Prayer  for  all  Conditions,  General  Thanksgiving, 
and  at  the  end  two  pages  of  other  prayers,  inclusive  of  those  for  the  chiefs 
and  their  families  (pp.  35-36).  The  hymns  (pp.  23-34)  are  42  in  number. 
Sewn  in  brown- paper  wrapper. 

{Vide  1830  and  1833  for  earlier  services.) 

In  1840  there  was  another  issue,  with  the  same  title-page  as  that  of  1839. 
Sm.  8vo.  Pp.  218'.  jj.  150  x  88.  Double  columns.  Contains  the  com- 
plete Church  service,  excepting  the  forms  for  ordination,  &c.,  of  bishops, 
priests,  and  deacons,  and  the  prayers  for  the  Queen  and  Royal  Family.  For 
the  latter  are  substituted  prayers  for  the  Governor,  the  chiefs,  and  their 
families.  Bound  with  the  volume  are  the  Psalms — "  Ko  |  nga  Waiata  |  a 
Rawiri.  |  Katahi  ka  taia  ki  te  reo  Maori.  |  No  Paihia  :  |  he  mea  ta  i  te  perehi 
o  nga  mihanere  o  te  |  Hahi  o  Ingarani.  |  1840"  (pp.  125  and  blank  page); 
then  the  39  Articles  (Ko  nga  j  Korero  ki  te  Wakapono  \  a  te  Hahi  o  Inga- 
rani) and  Table  of  Kindred  (He  Tepara  mo  nga  Huanga),  (pp.  127-41).  On 
the  last  page  is  "  Ko  te  wakamutunga  tenei.  Nowema,  1841."  At  the  end 
are  42  hymns  (Ko  nga  Himene),  (pp.  13).  The  Psalms  were  translated  by 
Mr.  Puckey. 

In  1842  there  was  a  similar  issue  to  the  preceding.  Also  one  on  thin 
paper,  "  Ko  nga  Inoi  o  te  Ata,  ko  te  Ritani,  ko  nga  Inoi  o  te  Ahiahi "  (The 
Order  for  Morning  and  Evening  Prayer  and  the  Litany).  8vo.  Pp.  32. 
fx  157  X  85.  The  42  hymns  follow  (Ko  nga  Himene).  Some  copies  have 
prefixed  a  Table  of  Lessons.  The  prayers  for  the  Queen,  Prince  Albert, 
the  Prince  of  Wales,  and  all  the  Royal  Family  now  first  appear. 

1843. 

Ko  te  I  Ritenga  me  te  Tikanga  |  o  te  Waka  Rikona. 

At  the  end,  "Paihia:  He  mea  ta  i  te  perihi  o  nga  Mihanere  o  te  Hahi 
o  Ingarani.

Ko  te  Ritenga — continued. 

(The  manner  and  rule  of  ordering  deacons.) 

Translation  into  Maori,  so  that  the  natives  might  understand  Bishop 
Selwyn's  procedure  at  the  ordination  service. 

An  Order  of  Confirmation  (sm.  8vo  ;  pp.  4)  and  an  Order  of  Conse- 
cration of  a  Burial-ground  (sm.  8vo  ;  pp.  4)  were  printed  in  Maori  about 
the  same  time.

He  Kupu  ma  te  ngakau  inoi.  |  "  Kei  whakamutua  te  inoi."     1  Teha.,  5,  17.  | 
At  Purewa  :  |  Printed  at  te  perehi  o  nga  Mihanere.  |  M.DCCC.XLV. 

Sm.  8vo.     Pp.  37. 

(Prayers  and  sentences  from  the  heart.) 

Translated  from  Bishop  Andrews  by  the  Rev.  R.  Maunsell.

perehi,  &c.  |  1847."     Sm.  8vo.     Pp.  34.     (x  145  x  84.

1848. 

Sm.  8vo.  Pp.  ii  (n.p.),  20,  321.  fx  152  x  80.  W.  Watts,  Crown  Court, 
Temple  Bar,  printer. 

The  first  Maori  prayer-book  printed  in  England  ;  issued  by  the  S.P.C.K. 
In  it  the  Epistles  and  Gospels  for  the  day  are  not  printed,  but  merely  indi- 
cated by  the  textual  reference.  The  service  for  the  making  of  deacons  is 
included. 

In  1852  the  Epistles  and  Gospels  were  printed,  but  otherwise  the  issue 
is  identical.     Pp.  432. 

In  1859  there  was  another  issue,  which  was  identical  with  the  previous 
one. 

From  this  date  a  numerous  variety  has  been  issued.

1840. 

Ko  I  nga  Katikihama  [  ewa  :  |  ka  oti  nei  te  wakamaori  ki  te  reo  |  o  Nu 
Tireni.  |  Paihia  :  |  he  mea  ta  i  te  perehi  o  nga  mihanere  o  te  |  Hahi  o  Inga- 
rani.  1  1840. 

Sm.  8vo.     Pp.  28.     fi  145  x  85. 

These  four  catechisms  were  the  earUest  from  the  New  Zealand  press. 
The  first  has  41  questions  and  answers,  the  second  24,  the  third  70,  and  the 
fourth  25. 

In  1842  there  was  another  edition,  with  the  same  title  as  the  preceding, 
*  but  the  contents  vary  from  it  in  composition  and  the  use  of  fresh  words  and 
matter.  The  catechisms  have  respectively  42,  44,  71,  and  25  questions  and 
answers.     Sm.  8vo.     Pp.  35. 


1845. 

Ko  I  te  whakangawhatanga  o  te  Katikihama  o  te  |  Hahi  o  Ingarangi  |  Ho 
mea  penei  kia  matau  ai  te  tangata  i  te  |  mea  e  whakahuatia  nei  te  katiki- 
hama. 1  I  taia  ki  te  Perehi  a  nga  Mihanare  o  j  Kaitaia,  1845. 

Sm.  8vo.     Pp.  49,  and  2  n.p.     ^^  140  x  78. 

4  chapters  (upoko).  "  Ko  nga  ingoa  o  Ihu  Kariti  "  (The  names  of  Jesus 
Christ). 

The  Kaitaia  press  was  a  small  hand-machine,  run  at  first  by  Mr.  Puckey, 
the  senior  catechist,  who  trained  a  Maori,  Ropata  Taki,  to  its  use.  On  it 
were  printed  alphabets,  school  lessons,  and  skeleton  sermons  for  Maori  lay 
readers  prepared  on  slips  of  paper  by  the  Rev.  Joseph  Matthews.  It  was 
probably  taken  possession  of  by  Mr.  John  Telford,  who  succeded  Mr.  Colenso 
as  printer  on  the  Church  Missionary  Press.

respectively.

Neither  date  nor  place  of  printing,  but  Paihia,  1839.

There  was  another  edition  in  1840,  but  better  printed,  and  bound  in  with 
the  prayer-book  of  this  year. 

In  the  year  1852  the  hymns  had  increased  in  number  to  56,  and  were 
usually  bound  up  with  the  prayer-books.

Tunes  for  different  metres. 

Sm.  8vo.  Pp.  40  (n.p.).  fi  142  x  85.  No  title-page,  pagination,  or  im-

A  figure  notation  for  the  use  of  St.  John's  College  singing  classes.  The 
Maori  syllables  ta,  ru,  to,  wha,  ma,  no,  tu,  iva  are  substituted  for  do,  re,  mi, 
&c. 

Written  by  Dr.  Arthur  Guyon  Purchas,  a  medical  man,  and  ordained  a 
•deacon  of  St.  John's  College,  September,  1847. 


Wesley  AN  Mission. 

[The  Wesleyan  Mission  issued  various  publications  from  their  press  at  Ma- 
ngunu,  on  the  Hokianga  River,  from  1835 — primers,  catechisms,  hymns  on  sheets 
and  in  pamphlet  form,  and  portions  of  Scripture.  In  1836  a  Harmony  of  the 
Gospels  was  issued  in  Maori.  The  Rev.  William  Woon  was  printer,  and  was 
aided  by  the  Rev.  John  Hobbs,  who  for  this  purpose  learnt  the  art  of  printing.]

8vo.     Pp.  44. 

The  Roman  Catholic  doctrine. 

Pompallier,  Bishop. 

In  this  year,  and  again  in  1848,  the  Bishop  addressed  two  letters  to 
Hone  Heke  and  his  followers,  exhorting  them  to  cease  warfare  and  observe 
peace.  They  were  printed  in  double  columns  on  fcp.  fol.,  in  Maori.  They 
have  been  mislaid  in  my  collection,  and  search  for  them  has  failed.  In  1856, 
1861,  and  1863  he  wrote  to  other  natives  in  a  similar  strain  regarding  the 
Waitara  and  Waikato  wars  {vide  p.  226). 

The  Bishop  returned  to  France  in  February,  1868,  and  died  on  the  20th 
December,  1870.

518  Bibliography  of  New  Zealand  Literatuue. 


[jColensO,  William] — continued. 

(The  second  of  the  descriptive  books ;  making  known  the  errors  of  the 
Church  of  Rome.) 

Three  conversations  between  Aroha  Pono  (Truelove),  Rapupono  (Earnest 
Seeker),  and  Takataka  (Waverer),  in  which  are  discussed  errors  (henga) 
numbered  from  7  to  12. 

Mr.  Busby  (the  British  Resident)  and  others  outside  the  mission  ob- 
jected to  this  being  printed  at  the  Paihia  press.  Mr.  Colenso  thereupon 
sent  it  to  Hobart  Town  (Hopataone)  and  Sydney,  where  8,000  copies  were 
printed. 

The  first  of  the  descriptive  books  (Ko  te  tuatahi  o  nga  pukapuka 
waki)  was  printed  at  Sydney  in  1840,  in  which  were  discussed  errors  from 
1  to  6. 

These  wer;  reprinted  in  1852,  as  follows  :   "  He  |  pukapuka  waki ;  |  hei 

wakakite  atu  i  nga  he  o  te  |  Hahi  o  Roma.  |  Na  te  Aroha  Pono,  |  ki  ana 

hoa,  ki  te  hunga  o  Nu  Tireni.  |  .     .     .     Mat.  xxiv,  4.  |  .     .     .     Karatia  i, 

7,  8.  I  .     .     .     2  Koriniti  x,  4,  5.  |  Poneki ;  |  he  mea  ta  i  te  perehi  o  te  Toki. 

I  1852."     Sm.  8vo.     Pp.  24. 

The  resuscitation  of  these  old  pamphlets  was  the  outcome  of  some 
heated  correspondence  between  Mr.  Colenso  and  the  Rev.  Father  Lampila,  a 
Roman  Catholic  priest,  of  Pakowhai,  Hawke's  Bay.

(On  the  sacrament  of  baptism.) 

All  /A  142  X  78.  Type  and  paper,  &c.,  indicate  that  the  above  8  tracts 
form  a  series  of  short  sermons.  They  were  written  by  the  Rev.  Robert 
Maunsell,  and  all  bound  together  in  glazed -calico  cover. 

There  is  another  edition,  in  which  the  sermons  are  differently  arranged, 
and  of  which  the  title  is,  "  Seven  Maori  Sermons.— Contents  :  1.  Ko  te  Tino 
Tohimga  Nui.  2.  Ko  te  Whakapono.  3.  Ko  te  Iriiri  Tapu.  4.  Ko  te  Hapa 
Tapu.  5.  Ko  nga  Mahi  Hou.'*f|6.  Ko  te  Inoi  a  te  Tahae.  7.  Ko  te  Mana- 
wanui o  Hopa.     Purewa  :    Printed  at  the  Church  Mission  Press.— 1846." 

Some  copies  of  these  sermons  'are  incomplete,  containing  a  fewer 
number  than  8 — generally  5.

After  Mr.  Colenso's  ordination  as  deacon  in  1844,  and  departure  forhia 
new  duties  in  the  Ahuriri  district,  he  secured  a  small  press,  from  which  he 
issued  his  numerous  little  publications,  generally  with  the  imprint  of  "  Perehi 

0  Te  Koreneho."     It  will   be  observed  that  he  never  aspirates  the  letter 
"  w  "  in  any  of  his  earlier  printing.

SCHOOL-BOOKS  AND  BOOKS  OF  INSTRUCTION. 
1833. 

Sheets  (22  in.  x  17  in.),  with — (1)  Maori  alphabet,  numerals,  signs  of 
punctuation,  &c. ;  (2)  Scripture  texts  in  IVIaori  syllabified ;  (3)  a  simple 
morning  and  evening  prayer  for  children,  in  English;  (4)  a  notification: 
"  The  Order  for  this  School  is,  A  Place  for  Everything  and  Everything  in 
its  Place." 

Two  of  these  are  printed  by  "  Jones,  Bridge  St.,  Sydney  "  ;  probably 
they  were  all  printed  there.     No  date.     [1833  (?).] 

Loose  sheets  (10  in.  x  8  in.),  12  in  number,  from  "  Ratari  1"  to  "  Ra- 
tari  12  "  (?  Lessons  1  to  12),  proceeding  from  words  of  one  syllable  to  those 
of  four  syllables. 

No  date.     [1833  (?).]

I  ki  te  perehi  o  te  Kuini.  j  Na  te  Kepa  i  whakamaori,  |  Aperira,  1852. 

8vo.  Pp.  157.  A  short  preface  in  English  and  Maori.  4  full-page 
woodcuts,  by  Dr.  Thomas  Shearman  Ralph,  who  practised  in  Wellington, 
and  was  one  of  the  promoters  and  secretary  of  the  New  Zealand  Society 
(1851) ;  afterwards  lived  in  Kew,  Victoria.  Translated  by  the  well-known 
interpreter  and  Native  Secretary,  Henry  Tacy  Kemp. 

One  example  of  the  efforts  made  by  Sir  George  Grey  during  his  Go- 
vernorship to  interest  and  instruct  the  Maoris. 

Mr.  Kemp  told  me  in  1901  that  he  desired  to  bring  out  another  edition, 
but  received  no  encouragement.

Sm.  4to.     Pp.  12.     Double  columns. 

(The  first  Maori  parliament,  or  runanga,  at  the  Bay  of  Islands.) 

A  reprint  of  No.  10,  Vol.  ii,  23rd  May,  1862,  of  Te  Karere  Maori,  issued 
thus  separately  for  wider  distribution  amongst  the  natives.  In  the  Karere 
Maori  the  English  translation  follows  (pp.  13-22). 

An  account  of  the  proceedings  at  the  first  district  runanga  held  by  the 
Ngapuhi  on  Sir  George  Grey's  plan  for  Maori  government,  devised  by  him 
shortly  after  he  assumed  the  Governorship.

VOCABULARIES. 

1773. 
Cook's  First  Voyage  ( Ha wkes worth).     Vol.  iii,  p.  474. 

About  45  words  given  and  compared  with  those  of  Otaheite. 

Parkinson's  (S.)  Voyage  in  the  "  Endeavour."     P.  126, 
Gives  67  words  and  vocabularies  from  other  islands. 

Banks's  Journal,  edited  by  Sir  Joseph  Hooker  in  1896.     P.  253. 

42  words  compared  with  those  of  Otehite  and  other  vocabularies. 

1777. 

Cook's  Second  Voyage.     Vol.  ii,  p.  319. 

An  excellent  vocabulary  of  the  Society  Islands,  of  about  1,000  words, 
and  a  comparative  table  of  40  words  from  8  of  the  South  Sea  languages.  A 
preface  treats  on  pronunciation. 

1778. 
Porster's  Observations.     P.  284. 

A  comparative  table  of  46  words  in  15  languages.

Cook's  Third  Voyage. 

Vol.  i :   pp.  164-77,  comparative  tables  ;   pp.  369-417,  words  of  Friendly 
Isles.     Vol.  iii  (1785) :    p.  528,  comparative  table  ;    pp.  529  et  seq..  Friendly 
,      and  Sandwich  Isles,  Nootka,  &c.
