NEWSPAPERS  AND  PERIODICALS. 

1842-63. 
Te  Karere  o  Nui  Tireni.     (1842-46.) 
Fcp.  fol. 

The  Maori  Messenger.  [  Ko  te  Karere  Maori.     (1849-54). 
Demy  fol. 

The  Maori  Messenger.  |  Te  Karere  Maori.     (1855-61.) 
Sm.  4to. 

Te  Manuhiri  Tuarangi  |  and  |  Maori  Intelligencer.     (1861.) 
Sm.  4to. 

Te  Karere  Maori ;    or,  |  Maori  Intelligencer.     (1861-63.) 

Sm.  4to. 

For  collation  of  the  very  important  papers  given  above,  vide  p.  95.  It  may 
be  here  added  that  the  chief  editors  and  contributors  were  Dr.  Shortland,  Messrs. 
T.  S.  Forsaith,  C.  0.  Davis,  Walter  Buller,  Judges  W.  H.  Smith,  F.  D.  Fenton,. 
and  H.  A.  H.  Monro.  Mr.  David  Burn  supplied  the  shipping  news  and  market 
prices.  Mr.  Buller,  upon  taking  charge  of  the  paper  in  1861,  changed  its  name 
to  "  Te  Manuhiri  Tuarangi  "  (The  Visitor  from  Afar),  but  in  less  than  a  year  it 
reverted  to  its  former  title. 

1849. 

Ko  te  Ao  Mararaa.     [The  New  World.] 

The  first  number  was  published  at  Wellington,  on  the  20th  September,. 
1849.     Issued  fortnightly.     Subscription,  6s.  per  annum. 

Its  aim  was  to  foster  a  spirit  of  industry  amongst  the  natives,  and  to 
acquaint  them  with  the  customs  of  their  white  neighbours. 


1859. 

Davis,   C.  0.     Te  Waka  o  te  Iwi.     [The  Canoe  of  the  Tribes.]     [1859  (?).] 
Two  or  three  numbers  were  issued. 

Davis,  C.  0.     Te  Whetu  o  te  Tau.     [The  Star  of  the  Year.]     [1859  (?).] 
Two  or  three  numbers  were  issued. 

Te  Haeta.  I  "  Tatou  kahaere  i  to  Ihowa  Maramatanga  "  [  Te  utu  mo  te  tau  |  ko- 
tahi  28.  6d.  |  Te  utu  mo  te  puka-  |  puka  kotahi  3d.  |  Vol.  I.]  Akarana,. 
Apereira  1,  1859.  [No.  1.  [The  Dawn  of  day."  "  Walk  in  the  light  of 
God's  countenance."] 

4to.  Pp.  4.  Triple  columns.  Imprint :  "  Printed  by  W.  C.  Wilson,^ 
at  the  '  New  Zealander '  Printing  Office,  Shortland  Street." 

Issued  monthlv  for  three  years  (1859-60),  the  last  number  being  Maehe 
(March),  1862  (No.  "12). 

A  religious  paper,  chiefly  issued  by  the  Wesleyan  Methodists. 


Maori.  543 


1861. 

[Davis,  C.  0.]  Ko  Aotearoa,  |  or  the  ]  Maori  Recorder.  |  Hanuere.  Janu- 
ary.  |  E  matau  haere  .  .  .  |  Akarana  :  |  he  mea  ta  i  te  perehi  o  nga  iwi 
Maori.  |  1861. 

8vo.     Pp.  24.     Maori  and  English.     Double  paper  wrappers. 

The  second  (and  last)  number  appeared  in  January,  1862  (pp.  32),  and 
was  called  the  Recorder  and  Aotearoa.  Mr.  Davis  was  editor  and  proprietor. 
As  an  ardent  philo-Maori  he  involved  himself  in  disputes  on  native  matters, 
and  was  finally  charged  with  treason. 

The  numbers  refer  largely  to  the  Waitara  trouble,  and  to  subjects  of 
general  interest  to  the  natives. 

Te  Hokioi,  o  Nui-Tireni,  e  rere  atuna.  [The  Far-flying  Hokioi  of  New  Zea- 
land.] 

Sm.  4to.  Usually  pp.  4 ;  but  often  poorly  printed  scraps  of  paper  ap- 
peared, and  the  whole  irregularly,  and  at  long  intervals.     Not  numbered. 

Patara,  cousin  of  the  King  JMatutaera  Potatau,  was  editor,  and  the 
paper  was  printed  at  Ngaruawahia.  It  was  the  organ  of  the  King  move- 
ment, and  contained  letters  from  seditious  natives,  notices,  &c.  It  ceased 
publication  immediately  after  the  extinction  of  the  Pihoihoi,  the  last  number 
being  that  of  the  24th  March,  1863. 

The  hokioi  was  a  mythical  bird  of  dire  omen. 

Psalm  ii,  v.  10,  supplied  an  occasional  motto,  which  was  placed  under  the 
title:  "Be  wise  now  therefore,  O  ye  kings;  be  instructed,  ye  judges  of  the 


earth. 


1863. 

CrOrst,  J.  E.  Te  Pihoihoi  Mokemoke  ]  I  runga  i  te  tuanui.  |  Number  1.  |  Pe- 
puere  2,  1863.  [The  Lonely  Ground-lark  perched  on  the  Roof.J  "The 
sparrow  that  sitteth  alone  upon  the  housetop  "  (Psalm  cii,  7.) 

Sm.  4to.  Pp.  4.  Double  columns  chiefly.  Imprint  (in  Maori) : 
"  Otawhao  : — i  taia  ki  te  perehi  o  te  kura." 

Edited  and  published  by  Mr.  John  Eldon  (Sir  J.  E.)  Gorst,  Native  Com- 
missioner in  the  Upper  Waikato,  to  counteract  the  mischievous  native 
newspaper,  Te  Hokioi,  of  the  Maori  King,  which  was  printed  at  Ngarua- 
wahia. The  paper  had  a  dramatic  ending  when  its  fifth  and  last  number, 
of  the  23rd  March,  and  the  press  were  seized  and  removed  by  the  angry 
natives,  for  a  full  and  interesting  account  of  which  vide  Appsndix  to  Journals 
of  House  of  Representatives,  E.-l,  1863. 

For  reference  to  and  translation  of  articles  vide  Karere  Maori,  30th  March, 
1863. 

Te  I  Waka  Maori  o  Ahuriri.  |  No.  1]    Nepia,  Hatarei,  Hune  13,  1863.     [Vol.  I. 

Demy  fol.  Usually  pp.  4.  Double  columns.  Imprint :  "  He  mea 
ta  na  Hemi  Wuru  [James  Wood],  i  te  whare  ta  o  te  Haaki  Pei  Herara 
[Hawke's  Bay  Herald],  ki  Nepia,  i  te  Haterei  i  nga  rua  wiki  katoa — tena  rua 
wiki,  tena  rua  wiki." 

With  the  third  number  was  added  to  the  title,  "  Ko  te  tika,  ko  te  pono, 
ko  te  aroha  "  (For  justice,  truth,  and  love),  being  the  motto  adopted  by 
Potatau,  the  first  Maori  King. 

Printed  in  Maori,  with  no  Enorlish  translation  until  1873. 

Vol.  i :  From  No.  1  (June  13,  1863)  to  No.  26  (May  28,  1864). 

Vol.  ii :  From  No.  27  (June  11,  1864)  to  No.  53  (June  10,  1865). 


544  Bibliography  of  New  Zealand  Literature. 


Te  Waka  Maori  o  Ahuriri — continued. 

Vol.  iii :  From  No.  54  (July  1.  1865)  to  No.  78  (November  10,  1866). 
Nos.  54  and  55  were  4to  size. 

Vol.  iv:  From  No.  1  (March  1,  1867)  to  No.  21  (February  20,  1868). 
From  Nos.  13  to  19  the  vol.  is  misprinted  "  v  "  for  "  iv,*'  and  from  Nos.  18 
to  21  the  year  is  misprinted  "  1867  "  for  "  1868." 

Vol.  v:  From  No.  1  (March  5,  1868)  to  No.  13  (May  27,  1869).  In 
No.  1  the  year  is  misprinted  "  1867  "  for  "  1868.".  " 

Vol.  vi :  From  No.  1  (July  17,  1869)  to  No.  18  (December  26,  1870). 

Vol.  vii :  From  No.  1  (January  19,  1871)  to  No.  6  (June  21,  1871). 

The  publication  was  then  removed  to  Wellington,  placed  entirely  under 
Government  control,  and  printed  by  George  Didsbury,  with  the  Royal  Arms 
on  the  title-page.  The  title  was,  Te  Waka  Maori  [  o  I^iu  Tirani.  |  "  Ko  te 
tika,  ko  te  pono,  ko  te  aroha."  |  Vol.  I.]  Poneke,  Turei,  Oketopa  24,  1871. 
[No.  1. 

Vol.  8:  From  No.  1  (October  24,  1871)  to  No.  20  (December  24,  1872). 
On  the  title-page  of  the  first  number  "  Vol.  I  "  is  misprinted  for  "  Vol.  8." 

Vol.  9 :  From  No.  1  (January  8,  1873)  to  No.  20  (December  24,  1873). 
Translation  into  Enelish  commenced  with  this  vol. 

Vol.  10  :   From^No.  1  (January  13,  1874)  to  No.  25  (December  15,  1874). 

Vol.  11  :  From  No.  1  (January  12,  1875)  to  No.  25  (December  28,  1875). 
From  No.  19  to  No.  23  the  vols,  are  misnumbered  "  12." 

Vol.  12  :   From  No.  1  (January  11,  1876)  to  No.  23  (December  19,  1876). 

Vol.  13  :  From  No.  1  (January  9,  1877)  to  No.  12  (July  17,  1877). 

An  angry  Parliament  here  terminated  the  paper's  existence  as  a  Go- 
vernment publication,  on  the  ground  of  political  misuse,  and  its  place  was 
supplied  by  the  Kahiti  o  Niu  Tireni  {New  Zealand  Gazette),  the  first  number 
of  which  was  issued  in  January,  1877.  The  paper  was,  however,  resuscitated 
by  a  small  company  at  Gisborne,  under  its  former  editor,  Mr.  James 
Grindell,  with  its  former  title,  the  word  "  Niu "  being  substituted  for 
"  Nui."  The  first  number  was  issued  at  Turanga  on  the  21st  August,  1878, 
for  the  "  Gisborne  Maori  Newspaper  Company,  Limited,"  and  usually  weekly 
thereafter.  With  the  5th  number  an  illustration  was  added  of  a  Maori  canoe 
filled  with  warriors  paddling  at  high  speed,  and  the  words  "  Hoea  te  waka, 
ha!"  (Pull  the  canoe!)  underneath,  the  words  "q  Niu  Tirani"  no  longer 
appearing  under  the  title.  After  the  31st  number  (24th  May,  1879)  the  paper 
was  removed  to  Napier,  and  there  continued  until  the  42nd  and  last  number 
of  the  series  (25th  October,  1879). 

The  paper  then  had  a  further  short  existence  in  1884,  again  at  Gis- 
borne, under  the  editorship  of  Mr.  George  H.  Wilson,  author  of  "  Ena,  or  the 
Ancient  Maori."  The  title  was  the  same  as  that  of  the  preceding,  with  the 
addition  of  "  0  Aotearoa."  It  was  published  fortnightly,  in  Maori,  without 
English  translation,  at  Turanganui,  Kihipane.  The  only  numbers  known 
to  me  are  No.  1  (March  7,  1884)  to  No.  16  (October  17,  1884). 

This  important  paper  had  thus  a  chequered  career,  and  suffered  at 
least  two  actions  for  libel— one  against  Mr.  Henry  Russell,  the  other  against 
Mr.  Sheehan  —  which  cost  the  country  more  than  £6,000.  It  is  full 
of  native  history  and  transactions,  letters  from  natives,  reports  of  their 
meetings,  and  other  important  matters. 


1874. 

Te   Wananga.    [  He  panuitanga  tena  kia    kite   koutou.   |   "  Tihe  Mauri-ora." 
I  Nama,    1.     Pakowhai,  Wenerei,  Akuhata,  5,   1874.     Pukapnka   1.     [The 
Medium,     The  proclamation  that  all  may  know.     "  God  bless  you."] 

Sm.  4to.  Pp.  varied  from  4  to  16.  Double  columns.  Usually  issued 
weekly.  Price  6d.  Imprint :  "  He  mea  ta  e  Henare  Hira,  a  he  mea  panui 
e  Henare  Tomoana,  e  te  tangata  nana  tenei  nupepa,  i  te  whare  ta  o  '  Te  Wa- 
nanga '  i  Pakowhai,  Nepia."     (Printed  by  Henry  Hill;   published  by  Henare 


Maori.  545 


Te  Wananga — continued. 

Tomoana,  the  proprietor  of  this  newspaper,  at  the  printing-office  of  Te  Wa- 
nanga, at  Pakowhai,  Napier.) 

The  place  of  publication  was  soon  changed  to  Napier.  There  were  five 
yearly  vols,  (pukapuka),  extending  from  the  4th  August,  1874,  to  the  21st 
December,  1878,  but  consecutive  pagination  ran  through  all,  p.  664  being  the 
last,  when  there  appears  a  notification  that  the  paper  will  shortly  be  en- 
larged and  improved.  There  was,  however,  no  further  publication.  Much 
of  the  matter  has  an  English  translation. 

This  paper  is  of  an  interesting  character,  containing  native  news  of  all 
kinds,  numerous  letters,  reports  of  native  meetings,  discussions  in  Parlia- 
ment on  land  and  other  special  subjects,  and  excellent  articles.  It  opposed 
the  Waka  Maori,  the  Government  or  Sir  Donald  McLean's  organ. 


1882. 

Te  Korimako.  [  He  nupepa  whakaatu  i  nga  rongo  o  te  ao  katoa,  nga  tikanga 
o  te  I  Whakapono  me  era  atu  mea.  |  Nama  1.  Akarana,  Maehe,  1882.  Te 
utu,  3d.  [The  Bell-bird  :  a  newspajaer  giving  the  news  of  the  whole  world, 
the  reasons  for  belief,  and  other  things.] 

Sm.  4to.  Pp.  4,  sometimes  8  or  12,  with  an  occasional  supplement 
(apiti)  and  illustrations.  Three  columns.  In  Maori ;  no  translations.  Im- 
print :  "He  mea  ta  e  Henare  Perete  [Henry  Brett]  mo  te  Rangatira  o  tenei 
nupepa  i  Winamu-tirete,  Akarana,  i  te  17  o  nga  ra  o  Aperiri,  1882." 

A  monthly  periodical,  containing  interesting  little  paragraphs,  letters, 
important  leaders,  essays,  poetry,  and  general  news.  It  was  founded  by 
Mr.  W.  P.  Snow,  a  pious  and  benevolent  American  gentleman  of  means, 
who  died  of  fever  in  March,  1883,  in  the  Red  Sea,  whilst  on  his  passage  to 
England.     Mr.  C.  0.  Davis  was  editor  and  manager. 

The  first  number  appeared  in  March,  1882  ;  the  last  (probably  No.  75) 
on  the  16th  May,  1888. 

1892. 

Te  Paki  o  Matariki.  |  Ko  te  mana  motuhake  o  Te  Kingitanga  o  Aotearoa. 
No.  5.]  Te  Kauhanganui,  Maungakawa,  Hune  20th,  1892.  [Wh.  23.  [The 
Girdle  of  the  Pleiades.  The  independent  power  of  the  Kingdom  of  New 
Zealand.] 

Long  fcp.  f ol.     Pp.  4.     No  imprint.     Oddly  printed  and  translated. 

Later  numbers  were  embellished  with  an  extraordinary  illustration,  on 
the  title-page,  of  two  Maori  gods  supporting  a  piece  of  circular  car-Cing,  on 
which  rests  a  distorted  human  figure  on  whose  head  is  a  cross  thrust  into  a 
heart,  the  stars  of  the  Pleiades  looking  down  on  the  whole — doubtless  intended 
as  an  analogue  of  our  own  Royal  Arms. 

The  paper  was  the  official  organ  of  King  Tawhiao  ;  was  printed  at 
Cambridge  on  various-sized  paper  and  issued  very  irregularly  from  some  time 
in  1891  to  October,  1902.  In  it  are  published  reports  of  native  meetings, 
discussions  with  the  Government,  Tawhiao's  addresses  to  his  council,  his 
laws,  Gazette  notices,  and  other  similar  curious  matter. 


1898. 

He  Kupu  Whakamarama.  |  Nelson  :   Bond,  Finney,  and  Co.,  printers. 

Sm.  4to.     Pp.  2.     No.  1,  March,  1898. 
Edited  by  the  Rev.  F.  A.  Bennett. 

No.   11  appeared  witli  a  zinc-block  illustration  on  the  outside  page- 
a  Maori  whare  with  native  family — and  an  altered  title :  "  Pipi-wharauroa

^46  Bibliography  of  New  Zealand  Literature. 


He  Kupu  Whakamarama — continued. 

He  Kupu  Whakamarama  "  [Cuckoo  :  a  word  of  enlightenmefit].  No.  16 
(Nelson,  June,  1899)  contained  pp.  10,  and  had  a  supplement  of  pp.  2,  printed 
by  H.  W.  Williams,  Te  Rau  press,  Gisborne.  From  No.  18  the  paper 
was  wholly  printed  at  the  Te  Rau  press,  and  the  editorship  passed  to  Mr. 
R,,  T.  M.  Kohere,  a  native,  doubtless  with  the  supervision  of  the  Rev. 
Herbert  W.  Williams,  principal  of  the  Maori  Training  College.  No.  27 
("  Te  Pipiwharauroa,"  &c.)  discarded  the  illustration. 

The  publication,  which  has  now  (May,  1909)  reached  its  134th  number, 
is  issued  monthly,  aims  at  being  a  newspaper  with  religious  and  other  im" 
proving  articles,  is  occasionally  illustrated  with  portraits  of  eminent  persons, 
and  is  conducted  by  Anglicans. 

Te  Pipiwharauroa.     ( FtcZe  the  preceding,  "He  Kupu  Whakamarama.") 

The  Jubilee.  |  Maori  Newspaper.  |  No.  1,  Vol.  1.  |  Te  Tiupiri.  |  January  4,  1898. 
Whanganui.  |  Hanuare  4,  1898. 

4to.  Pp.  8.  Double  columns.  In  coloured-paper  wrapper,  with  the 
above  title,  a  photograph  of  Queen  Victoria,  and  advertisements.  Imprint : 
"  He  mea  perehi  na  Teoni  D.  Kuini,  ki  tona  Tari  ta  pepa  i  Ritiwe  Tiriti  o 
Whanganui,  Niu  Tireni.  |  Printed  by  John  D.  Quin  at  his  registered  printing 
works,  Ridgway  Street,  Whanganui,  N.Z." 

Issued  at  first  weekly,  then  fortnightly,  and  then  at  irregular  intervals. 
Subscription,  12s.  6d.  per  annum.  The  last  was  No.  80,  Vol.  iii,  19th  July, 
1900. 

A  memento  of  the  Queen's  jubilee.  An  excellent  and  loyal  paper,  with 
varied  information,  conducted  by  a  Maori  committee  in  the  interests  of  the 
native  race.  Contains  some  legendary  history.  Written  chiefly  in  Maori. 
Ceased  publication  from  non-payment  of  subscriptions  and  lack  of  support. 
In  a  list  of  defunct  Maori  papers  the  names  are  given  of  Te  Hioi  (The 
Ground-lark),  Te  Kotohitanga,  and  Te  Huia  tangata  Kotahi. 

Te-Puke-ki-Hikurangi.      [The  Hill  of  Hikurangi.]  ' 

Long  fcp.  fol.  Pp.  4,  sometimes  8.  Triple  columns.  In  Maori.  On  the 
first  page,  below  the  title,  is  an  illustration  of  the  mythical  hill  or  islet  of 
Hikurangi,  surrounded  by  the  canoes  of  those  escaping  from  the  deluge  of  Rua- 
tapu.  Imprint :  "  Printed  and  published  by  T.  Renata,  under  the  authority 
of  H.  T.  Mahupuku,  at  his  registered  office,  Main  Street,  Greytown  North, 
Wairarapa."     Issued  fortnightly,  the  first  number  appearing  in  1898. 

On  7th  November,  1904,  it  appeared  in  4to,  with  paper  wrapper,  on 
which  was  the  additional  title  of  "  and  Maori  Record  ;  a  Journal  devoted 
to  the  Advancement  of  the  |  Maori  People.  |  Wellington,  N.Z.  :  |  Printed  for 
the  proprietors  by  Blundell  Bros.  Ltd.,  at  the  '  Evening  Post '  general 
printing  office." 

Vol.  1,  No.  1.  Pp.  8,  8.  First  half  in  Maori,  second  half  in  English. 
In  this  the  co-editor  with  Renata  was  Mr.  Robert  Studholme  Thompson,  of 
Normanby,  an  interpreter,  and  warm  friend  of  the  Maori.  The  new  ar- 
rangement not  proving  satisfactory,  the  paper  reverted  to  its  original  title, 
with  the  addition  on  the  wrapper  of  "  Huihuia  mai  o  koutou  whakaaro 
I  ki  runga  i  to  tatau  Maunga  Tapu  |  te  oha  a  te  hunga  kua  wehe  |  [Bring 
your  thoughts  together  on  this  sacred  mountain  as  a  tribute  to  those  who 
have  departed  from  us].  Greytown  North,  Wairarapa,  N.Z.  |  Printed  and 
published  for  the  proprietors  by  T.  Renata,  at  their  registered  printing 
•office."  4to.  Pp.  8.  Issued  weekly  nominally.  Triple  columns.  In  Maori. 
Apparently  it  ceased  publication  with  No.  33,  "o  te  tau  tuaono  "  [vol.  8], 
nth  September,  1906. 

In  July,  1905,  Mr.  R.  S.  Thompson  issued  the  first  number  of  his  monthly 
paper,  entitled  "  The  Maori  Record  \  a  Journal  devoted  tp  |  the  Advance- 
ment of  the  Maori  People.  |  Normanby,  N.Z.  :  [  Printed  for  the  proprietor 
Jby  W.  A.  Parkinson,  at  the  '  Hawera  Star '  general  printing  office.  ]  1905."

1840. 
He  I  Maramatakahaere  :  [  ara,  |  he  pukapuka  [  Hei  wakakite  i  nga  Ra  Tapu,  i 
nga  Ra  Hakari,  i  |  nga  Ra  e  kowiti  ai  e  hua  ai  te  Marama,  i  nga  |  aha  noa 
atu,  mo  nga  ra  katoa  nei  o  te  tau  |  1841,  o  to  tatou  Ariki  o  Ihu  Karaiti.  ] 
"A,  mea  ana  te  Atua  .  .  ."  Kenehi  1,  14.  |  "  I  te  wenua  hoki  e 
toe  ana  .  .  ."  Kenehi,  8,  22.  |  No  Paihia:  |  he  mea  ta  i  te  perehi  o 
nga  mihanere  o  te  |  Hahi  o  Ingarani.  |  1840. 

8vo.     Pp.  16.     Verses  of  Scripture  interspersed. 

(The  moon  or  month  as  it  goes  along  ( =  the  calendar),  or  book  showing 
the  Sundays,  festivals,  changes  of  the  moon,  many  other  things,  and  all  the 
days  of  the  year  of  our  Lord  Jesus  Christ  1841.) 

The  first  almanac  printed  in  New  Zealand.  Was  yearly  printed,  with 
some  variations,  until  1845.  Numerous  others  have  since  been  issued  from 
various  places  under  the  title  "Maramataka"  (the  changes  of  the  moon). 
Amongst  these  the  one  of  most  undoubted  and  perhaps  unique  interest  is 
**Ko  te  Hikurangi  |  Tainui  Marama  Whiti,  |  1891.  |  He  mea  tango  mainota 
te  Maori  |  whakahaerenga  i  nga  ra  o  te  tau.  |  E  Hoani  Nahi,  Omahu,  Hau- 
raki.  |  Printed  at  the  Advertiser  Office,  Albert  Street,  Thames."  8vo. 
Pp.  35  (n.p.).  (The  Hikurangi  Tainui  [Calendar]  of  the  moon's  appearances. 
Something  taken  from  the  Maori  observations  of  the  days  of  the  year.) 
Written  by  an  old  tohunga,  it  gives  ancient  tradition  connected  with  the 
Tainui  migration,  the  native  names  of  the  months  and  the  days  of  the 
month,  observations  on  the  various  changes  and  appearances  of  the  moon, 
and  the  omens  drawn  therefrom,  minutely  specifying  the  auspicious  and. 
inauspicious  days  for  various  pursuits.

VARIA. 


[There  are  many  papers,  such  as  Proclamations,  notices,  and  other  docu- 
ments (some  of  historical  importance,  others  of  a  fugitive  or  temporary 
•character,  but  all  interesting),  which  are  difficult  of  full  record.  Some  of  the 
chief  of  these  are  here  noted.] 

1813. 

A  Circular  describing  the  formation  of  an  institution  to  be  called  "  The  New- 
South  Wales  Society  for  affording  Protection  to  the  Natives  of  the  South 
Sea  Islands,  and  promoting  their  Civilization." 

Fcp.  fol.     Pp.  3.     Dated  Sydney,  20th  December,  1813. 
Fifteen  resolutions.     Promoted  by  the  Rev.  Samuel  Marsden,  who  had 
taken  a  keen  interest  in  the  subject. 


1814. 

Government  and  General  Orders.  |  Government  House,  Sydney,  New  South 
Wales,  I  9th  November,  1814.  1  .Civil  Department. 

Fcp.  fol.  P.  1.  Printed  on  parchment.  Signed  by  command  of  His 
Excellency  the  Governor  (Macquarie),  John  Thomas  Campbell,  Secretary. 
No  imprint. 

Provides  for  the  vigorous  punishment  of  those  traders  who  offer  insult 
or  injury  to  the  natives  of  New  Zealand.  Mr.  Thomas  Kendall,  and  the 
chiefs  Dewaterra  (Ruatara),  Shungie  (Hongi),  and  Korra  Korra  (Korokoro) 
have  authority  in  the  matter. 

1824. 

New  South  Wales.  ]  Proclamation. 

Roy.  4to.  P.  1.  Signed  by  H.E.  Sir  Thomas  Brisbane  and  F.  Goul- 
burn,  Colonial  Secretary.     Imprint :   "  R.  Howe,  Government  Printer." 

The  Proclamation  states  that  outrages  and  crimes  committed  in  New 
Zealand  or  other  islands  in  the  Indian  or  Pacific  Ocean  shall  be  punished. 


1831. 

■.  Government  Order.  |  Colonial  Secretary's  Office,  Sydney,  April  16,  1831. 

Fcp.  fol.     P.  1.     By  His  Excellency's  command,  Alexander  McLeay. 

The  order  states  that  steps  are  to  be  taken  to  prevent  the  inhuman 
and  disgusting  traffic  in  human  heads  at  New  Zealand.  His  Excellency 
(Sir  Ralph  Darling)  trusts  that  those  possessing  human  heads  recently  brought 
from  New  Zealand  by  the  schooner  "  Prince  of  Denmark  "  will  immediately 
deliver  them  up  to  be  restored  to  the  relatives  of  the  deceased  parties  to 
whom  those  heads  belonged.

1835. 

The  British  Resident  at  New  Zealand  to  His  Britannic  |  Majesty's  Subjects 
who  are  Residing  or  Trading  in  New  Zealand. 

Fop.  fol.  P.  1.  Printed  at  Paihia,  on  the  Church  Missionary  press. 
Signed  by  James  Busby,  British  Resident,  British  Residency,  at  New  Zealand,. 
Bay  of  Islands,  16th  October,  1835. 

In  this  letter  Mr.  Busby  states  that  he  has  received  from  "  Charles,  Baron 
de  Thierry,  Sovereign  Chief  of  New  Zealand,  and  King  of  Nuhuheva,"  a  de- 
claration that  he  is  about  to  establish  an  independent  sovereignty  in  New 
Zealand,  &c.  Mr.  Busby  is  sure  that  his  countrymen  will  pay  no  attention 
to  the  ambitious  projects  of  any  adventurer,  and  he  will  further  call  a  meeting 
of  chiefs  to  assert  their  independence. 

The  outcome  of  this  was  the  "  He  Wakaputanga,"  &c.,  or  Declaration 
of  Independence,  immediately  below.  A  copy  of  these  documents  is  in  the 
Report  of  the  House  of  Lords  Committee  on  New  Zealand,  1838,  pp.  244-46. 

He  Wakaputanga  o  te  Rangatiratanga  o  |  Nu  Tirene. 

Fcp.  fol.     P.  1.     Printed  at  Paihia,  on  the  Church  Missionary  press. 
(The  Declaration  of  Independence  by  Confederated  Maori  Chiefs.) 
This  was  signed  by  35  chiefs  at  Waitangi,  Bay  of  Islands,  on  the  28thi 
October,  1835. 
(Ft<?f  p.  304.) 


1837. 

Address  |  of  |  the  Baron  de  Thierry  j  to  the  |  White  Residents  in  New  Zealand. 

Demy  fol.  Pp.  2.  Printed  at  the  "  Herald "  office.  Lower  George- 
Street,  Sydney.  Signed  :  Charles  Baron  de  Thierry,  Sydney,  20th  September, 
1837. 

In  this  lengthy  declaration,  with  appended  articles  of  agreement,  de 
Thierry  sets  forth  his  proposed  mode  of  government,  and  calls  upon  all  his 
future  subjects  to  assist. 

A  copy  of  this  address,  with  that  of  the  deed  on  which  his  assumption, 
is  founded,  is  in  the  Report  of  the  Lords  Committee  on  New  Zealand,  1838, 
pp.  299-305. 


1839. 

Prospectus  |  of  an  |   Institution  |  for  the  |  Maintenance  and  Education  of 
Children,  |  the  Offspring  of  English  Fathers  by  New-Zealand  Mothers. 

Fcp.  fol.     P.  1.     Printed  at  Paihia,  on  the  Church  Missionary  press. 

Proposes  that  a  meeting  be  held  at  Kororareka  for  the  purpose.  To  bo 
called  the  Victoria  Institution,  to  be  located  in  Mr.  Busby's  proposed  town- 
ship of  Victoria,  and  to  be  partly  supported  by  the  investment  of  one-fifth 
of  the  funds  derived  from  the  sale  of  the  township  sections.  Lengthy 
details  of  the  proposal  given  in  9  clauses. 


Varia.  551 


1840. 


Proclamation.   |  By   His   Excellency  William    Hobson,    Esquire,    Lieutenant- 
I  Governor  of  the  British  Settlements  in  progress  in  New  |  Zealand. 

Fop.  fol.  P.  1.  Printed  at  Paihia,  on  the  Church  Missionary  press. 
Dated  "  Kororareka,  30th  January,  1840,"  the  day  after  Captain  Hobson's 
arrival  in  New  Zealand. 

The  Proclamation  notifies  that  no  titles  to  land  will  be  recognised  unless 
as  Crown  grants  ;  that  no  further  purchases  from  natives  are  to  be  made  ; 
and  that  a  Land  Court  will  be  at  once  instituted. 

Previous  to  this  a  Proclamation  was  read  asserting  the  Queen's  authority 
over  British  subjects  in  New  Zealand. 

Notices  were  at  the  same  time  sent  to  the  chiefs  that  a  meeting  to  consider 
a  treaty  would  be  held  on  the  5th  February. 


The  Treaty  of  Waitangi. 

Fcp.  fol.  P.  I.  Signed  by  Captain  Hobson,  6th  February,  1840,  at 
Waitangi.     Printed  at  Paihia,  on  the  Church  Missionary  press. 

In  Maori. 

Mr.  Colenso  tells  me  that  many  copies  were  printed  on  a  cheaper  paper, 
large  4to  size,  for  general  distribution  amongst  the  natives. 

An  issue,  with  English  translation,  fcp.  fol.,  pp.  2  {n.p.),  was  printed  at 
Auckland  by  Christopher  Fulton,  the  Government  Printer,  in  1844. 

(F^•f^ep.  304.) 

A  Circular  Letter  dated  to  the  natives,  "  No  Paihia,  no  te  27  o  nga  ra  o  Eperira 
[April],  1840,"  and  signed  "Na  to  Koutou  hoa,  Na  te  Kawana"  [from  your 
friend  the  Governor  (Hobson)]. 

Sm.  4to.     P.  1.     Printed  at  Paihia,  on  the  Church  Missionary  press. 
Cautions  the  natives  against  listening  to  the  evil  reports  of  disaffected 
persons. 


He  Wakapuakanga.     [A  Proclamation.] 

Fcp.  fol.  P.  1.  Printed  at  Paihia,  on  the  Church  Missionary  press, 
and  dated  the  2nd  May,  1840,  by  Governor  Hobson. 

In  Maori.  Cautions  the  natives,  under  heavy  penalties,  from  bujing 
from  the  soldiers  their  horses,  guns,  powder,  or  any  accoutrement,  as  they 
belong  to  the  Queen.     Published  also  in  English. 

Proclamation. 

Fcp.  fol.  P.  1.  Printed  at  Paihia,  on  the  Church  Missionary  press, 
and  signed  the  21st  May,  1840,  by  Lieutenant-Governor  Hobson,  at  Govern- 
ment House,  Russell,  Bay  of  Islands. 

Claims  full  sovereignty  over  the  Northern  Island  of  New  Zealand  by  virtue 
of  cession. 


Proclamation. 

Fcp.  fol.  P.  1.  Printed  at  Paihia,  on  the  Church  Missionary  press, 
and  signed  the  21st  May,  1840,  by  Lieutenant-Governor  Hobson,  at  Go- 
vernment House,  Russell,  Bay  of  Islands. 

Claims  full  sovereignty  over  the  Islands  of  New  Zealand,  by  virtue  of 
command  from  Her  Majesty  Queen  Victoria. 


552  Bibliography  of  New  Zealand  Literature. 

Protest  I  of  the  Subjects  of  the  British  and  other  Powers  residing  in  Xew  Zea- 
land. 

Fep.  fol.  Pp.  2.  Double  columns.  No  date  [1840].  Imprint :  "  Koro- 
rareka  : — Printed  by  G.  A.  Eagar  &  Co." 

A  bitter  appeal,  in  7  paragraphs,  against  the  flagrant  Act  which  dis- 
annuls or  interferes  with  the  right  of  land-purchase  from  the  natives  exercised 
before  the  assertion  of  the  Queen's  sovereignty.  Reprinted  in  Dr.  Martin's 
"  New  Zealand,"  1845,  q.v. 

Memorial   |   to  Her  Most  Gracious  Majesty  |   Victoria,    |    .     .     .     |   From  the- 
Landed  Proprietors,  Merchants,  and  |  others  settled  in  New  Zealand     .     .     . 

Fcp.  fol.  P.  1.  Double  columns.  No  date  [1840].  Imprint :  "  Koro- 
rareka  :— Printed  by  G.  A.  Eagar  &  Co." 

Refers  at  great  length  to  the  matters  contained  in  the  preceding  protest,, 
and  prays  Her  Majesty  to  withhold  her  consent  from  the  unjust  and  cruel 
Act. 


1845. 

Proclamation.     Wakarongo. 

Fcp.  fol.  Pp.  2.  English  and  Maori.  Signed  by  Robert  Fitzroy,  Go- 
vernor, at  Government  House,  Auckland,  8th  January,  1845.  Printed  by 
Christopher  Fulton,  Government  Printer. 

A  reward  of  £150  offered  for  the  apprehension  of  three  chiefs  who  had 
committed  a  depredation  and  a  flagrant  robbery  at  the  Bay  of  Islands  and 
at  Matakana.  Until  they  are  delivered  up  to  justice  the  waiver  of  the  Crown's 
right  of  pre-emption  shall  cease. 

Proclamation.     Wakarongo, 

Fcp.  fol.  Pp.  2.  English  and  Maori.  Signed  by  Governor  Fitzroy^ 
at  Auckland,  15th  January,  1845.     Christopher  Fulton,  Government  Printer. 

A  reward  of  £100  offered  for  the  apprehension  of  Hone  Heke,  who  had 
committed  a  serious  outrage  at  Russell  on  the  10th  January. 

Soon  afterwards  Hone  Heke  retorted  by  offering  a  reward  for  Governor 
Fitzroy's  head. 

Proclamation.     Wakarongo. 

Fcp.  fol.  Pp.  2.  English  and  Maori.  Signed  by  Governor  Fitzroy, 
at  Auckland,  3rd  March,  1845.     Printed  by  Christopher  Fulton. 

Ae  restitution  has  been  made  by  the  three  chiefs,  the  Proclamation  of 
the  8th  of  January  is  rescinded. 

Wakarongo.     Notice. 

Fcp.  fol.  Pp.  2.  Maori  and  English.  Signed  by  M.  Richmond,  Superin- 
tendent, Southern  Division,  New  Zealand.  Wellington,  31st  March,  1845. 
No  imprint. 

Recounts  the  outbreak  at  Kororareka  by  Hone  Heke,  who  has  again 
transgressed  the  law  and  defied  the  Governor,  and  assures  the  southern 
natives  that  so  long  as  they  are  peaceably  disposed  no  harm  will  befall  them. 

Wakarongo.     Proclamation. 

Fcp.  fol.  Pp.  2.  Maori  and  English.  Signed  by  Governor  Fitzroy,  at 
Auckland,  20th  September,  1845.     Printed  by  C.  Fulton. 

Proclaims  that  every  native  retiring  from  the  rebel  chiefs  Kawiti  and 
Heke  shall  be  pardoned  on  condition  of  remaining  at  peace.

Rules  [  for  reducing  a  prosperous  colony  |  to  a  state  of  insignificance,  poverty, 
and  distress,  |  Taken  from  an  old  author,  [  and  dedicated  |  to  the  |  Prime 
Minister  |  of  the  |  King  of  the  Cannibal  Islands, 

Fcp.  fol.     P.  1.     No  imprint  or  date  [1845]. 

A  well-written,  bitter  diatribe,  in  7  paragraphs,  on  Governor  Fitzroy, 
who  was  nicknamed  "  King  of  the  Cannibal  Islands." 

To  His  Excellency  |  Captain  Robert  Fitzroy,  R.N.,  &c.,  &c. 

Fcp.  fol.  Pp.'  2.  No  imprint.  Signed  by  Junius  Zealandi,  Auckland, 
New  Zealand,  3rd  November,  1845. 

A  still  more  bitter  attack  than  the  preceding  on  Governor  Fitzroy  on  the 
eve  of  his  departure  from  the  colony.  He  is  accused  of  the  grossest  mis- 
management. 

The  writer  is  stated  to  have  been  J.  S.  Polack. 


1846. 


Proclamation. 


Demy  fol.  P,  1.  Signed  by  Lieut. -Governor  George  Grey,  at  Wel- 
lington, 21st  February,  1846.     Printed  at  the  office  of  the  Independent 

A  waiver  in  favour  of  the  New  Zealand  Company  of  the  right  of  pre-emp- 
tion of  lands  in  the  Northern  and  Middle  Islands. 

This  was  preliminary  to  the  founding  of  the  settlements  of  Otago  and 
Canterbury.
